sessions.title: Mock Interview

activities.description: Focus on: requirements (5 min) → estimation (5 min) → architecture (20 min) → deep dive (10 min) → follow-ups (5 min).

sessions.content:
## Session 10: Mock Interview

```mermaid
graph TB
    C1[Client 1] <-->|WebSocket| GW[API Gateway]
    C2[Client 2] <-->|WebSocket| GW
    GW --> CS[Chat Service]
    CS --> MQ[Message Queue]
    CS --> DB[(Message Store)]
    MQ --> Push[Push Notification]
```


### Full Interview Simulation (45 Minutes)

**Interviewer:** "Design WhatsApp — the core messaging features."

---

**[Minutes 0-5: Requirements]**

**Candidate:** "Core features to confirm: 1-on-1 messaging, group chat, online presence, message history. What's the group size limit?"

**Interviewer:** "500 members max."

**Candidate:** "Media — images only, or video too?"

**Interviewer:** "Images only for now."

**Candidate:** "Scale — DAU?"

**Interviewer:** "50 million DAU."

**Candidate:** "Non-functional: message delivery under 500ms when both online. High availability — messages must never be lost. Global deployment? Or single region?"

**Interviewer:** "Global — users in US, EU, Asia."

**Candidate:** "Got it. Out of scope: voice/video calls, payments, user authentication system."

---

**[Minutes 5-10: Capacity Estimation]**

**Candidate:** "50M DAU, peak concurrent 20% = 10M connections. Messages: 100M/day = ~1,200/sec, peak 6,000/sec.

Storage: 100M messages × 200 bytes = 20GB/day. 5 years = 36TB. Need Cassandra.

Images: assume 10% of messages = 10M images/day × 500KB = 5TB/day → S3.

Connections: 10M WebSockets, each server holds 100K → need 100 Chat Servers."

---

**[Minutes 10-20: High-Level Architecture]**

**Candidate:** "I'll split into three main paths: connection management, message routing, and storage.

Connection path: Client connects via WebSocket to a Chat Server. Load balancer uses consistent hashing by user_id for sticky sessions. 100 Chat Servers handle 10M connections.

Message routing: When A (on Server 1) sends to B (on Server 47), Server 1 checks Redis connection registry to find B's server. Publishes message to Kafka. Server 47 consumes from Kafka, pushes to B.

Storage: Messages persist to Cassandra before ACK is sent to sender. Image uploads go directly to S3 via presigned URLs; CDN serves images to recipients."

**Interviewer:** "Why not have Chat Servers communicate directly instead of through Kafka?"

**Candidate:** "Direct communication creates an N² connection mesh — 100 servers × 100 peers = 10,000 connections to maintain. Kafka is a hub: each server connects to Kafka only. Also, Kafka provides message replay and durability. If Chat Server 47 crashes, messages queue in Kafka and the replacement server consumes them on restart."

---

**[Minutes 20-30: Deep Dive — Offline Delivery]**

**Interviewer:** "Walk me through what happens when B is offline."

**Candidate:** "Server 1 looks up B in connection registry → no entry → B is offline. Message is persisted to Cassandra with `delivered = false`. Notification Service sends a push notification via APNS/FCM to B's registered device token.

When B reconnects: B's client opens WebSocket to a Chat Server (let's say Server 82). Server 82 registers B in connection registry. Then queries Cassandra for all messages where B is recipient and `delivered = false`, ordered by TIMEUUID ascending. Delivers all missed messages to B's WebSocket. B's client sends `delivered` receipt for each. Server updates Cassandra and notifies senders."

**Interviewer:** "What if B has been offline for 30 days and has 10,000 missed messages?"

**Candidate:** "Fetch in batches of 200 messages. Client paginates. Also, implement a 30-day retention for delivery — after 30 days of non-delivery, messages are still stored (1-year TTL) but we stop attempting delivery notifications. User can still scroll back and see them manually."

---

**[Minutes 30-40: Failure Modes]**

**Interviewer:** "What happens when a Chat Server crashes mid-message?"

**Candidate:** "Three scenarios. First: crash before Cassandra write — sender's ACK timer fires after 5 seconds, client retries with same `client_msg_id`. Server processes as new message (idempotent insert). Second: crash after Cassandra write but before sender ACK — same retry path, but second attempt finds existing message (via `IF NOT EXISTS`) and returns existing server_msg_id. No duplicate. Third: crash after sender ACK but before recipient delivery — Kafka message hasn't been acknowledged. On Chat Server recovery (or rebalance to another server), Kafka redelivers the message."

**Interviewer:** "What about the presence system at 50M DAU?"

**Candidate:** "Heartbeat every 30 seconds per client. 10M active connections × (1/30) = 333K presence updates/sec if we hit Redis every heartbeat. That's too heavy. Optimization: Chat Servers manage presence in-memory. Only on connect/disconnect do they write to Redis. Heartbeats reset the in-memory timer (60s TTL). Redis sees ~20K writes/sec — manageable. Presence fan-out is further limited: only notify friends currently active in the app, not all friends."

---

**[Minutes 40-45: Scaling to 10x]**

**Interviewer:** "How does your design scale to 500M DAU?"

**Candidate:** "Main bottlenecks to address: Connections: 100M simultaneous → 1,000 Chat Servers. Kafka: increase partitions to 500, scale brokers to 50. Cassandra: scale from 30 to 300 nodes. Redis: shard connection registry across 100 Redis nodes.

Multi-region becomes critical: US, EU, Asia each get a full deployment. Messages within a region stay regional — low latency. Cross-region: route through regional backbone. Eventual consistency across regions is acceptable for chat.

CDN for images becomes even more critical — 50TB/day of image traffic needs aggressive edge caching."

---

### Key Trade-offs to Articulate

| Decision | Chosen | Alternative | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protocol | WebSocket | Long polling | Real-time bidirectional, low latency |
| Message store | Cassandra | PostgreSQL | High write throughput, time-series fit |
| Routing | Kafka hub | Direct server mesh | N² complexity, Kafka adds durability |
| Delivery | At-least-once + dedup | Exactly-once | Exactly-once needs distributed transactions |
| Presence | 30s heartbeat | Event-driven | Balance accuracy vs. Redis write load |

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Mock Interview — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Mock Interview ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Mock Interview");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

### Python Implementation

```python

def demonstrate():
    """Mock Interview — Key concepts demonstration"""
    print(f"=== Mock Interview ===")

    # Core data structures
    config: Dict[str, Any] = {
        'topic': 'Mock Interview',
        'complexity': 'O(n)',
        'space': 'O(1)',
    }
    for k, v in config.items():
        print(f'{k}: {v}')

    # Typical operations
    data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    print(f'Input: {data}')
    print(f'Size: {len(data)}')
    print(f'Contains 3: {3 in data}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    demonstrate()
```

quizBank.question: Why is WebSocket preferred over HTTP for a chat application?
options: WebSocket is bidirectional and maintains persistent connection
quizBank.answer: WebSocket is bidirectional and maintains persistent connection
quizBank.explanation: HTTP is request-response (client initiates). WebSocket allows server to push messages to client at any time with low overhead. This is essential for real-time chat delivery.

quizBank.question: Alice is connected to Chat Server 1. Bob is connected to Chat Server 2. Alice sends Bob a message. How is it delivered?
options: Alice's server sends directly to Bob's server via HTTP
Message is stored in DB, Bob polls for new messages
Message is published to shared Redis Pub/Sub channel, Bob's server is subscribed
A centralized router server handles all cross-server delivery
quizBank.answer: Message is published to shared Redis Pub/Sub channel, Bob's server is subscribed
quizBank.explanation: Redis Pub/Sub is a common pattern for cross-server message routing. Each chat server subscribes to channels for the users connected to it. When a message arrives for Bob, it's published to his channel, and his server delivers it via WebSocket.

quizBank.question: Why is Cassandra chosen for message storage over PostgreSQL?
options: Cassandra handles write-heavy time-series workloads with linear scalability
quizBank.answer: Cassandra handles write-heavy time-series workloads with linear scalability
quizBank.explanation: Messages are append-only, partitioned by conversation_id, clustered by time. Cassandra's LSM-tree storage engine handles high write throughput. PostgreSQL would require complex sharding to match the write scale of 100B messages/day.

quizBank.question: A chat server crashes while 50,000 users are connected. What happens to their messages and connections?
quizBank.answer: Connections are dropped immediately — clients get disconnection events. Client should auto-reconnect with exponential backoff. Messages in flight (published to Kafka but not delivered) are safe — Kafka retains them. On reconnect, client sends last_seen_message_id, server replays missed messages from Cassandra. For offline duration, missed messages are retrieved from message history. The crashed server's load is redistributed via load balancer to remaining servers.

quizBank.question: What does 'delivered' status mean vs 'read' status in WhatsApp?
options: Delivered means reached server; Read means recipient opened the message
Delivered means recipient's device received it; Read means they saw it
Delivered means sent; Read means downloaded
quizBank.answer: Delivered means recipient's device received it; Read means they saw it
quizBank.explanation: Three states: Sent (server ACK), Delivered (recipient device ACK via WebSocket), Read (recipient opened chat and message was visible). Each requires an explicit ACK from the recipient's client.

quizBank.question: You have a group chat with 100,000 members (think a public community). Someone sends a message. How do you fan-out?
quizBank.answer: Fan-out on write would generate 100,000 WebSocket pushes per message — unacceptable. Use fan-out on read: store message once in DB. When a member opens the chat, they pull messages since their last_read_id. For online members who have the chat open, push in real-time but batch: segment members by chat server and push once per server (each server then delivers to its connected members). Prioritize: push to members who are actively viewing, lazy-load for background.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'message ordering' when messages arrive out of order due to network issues?
quizBank.answer: Use client-side sequence numbers per conversation. Each message includes: client-generated temp_id, server-assigned message_id (Snowflake, time-ordered), conversation_id, and seq_num (monotonic counter per conversation). Client sorts by message_id (Snowflake contains timestamp). If gap detected (seq_num jump), client requests missing messages from server. Server stores in Cassandra ordered by message_id DESC — reads always return correctly ordered messages.

quizBank.question: How many WebSocket servers are needed for 100 million concurrent connections, if each server handles 50,000 connections?
quizBank.answer: 2,000 servers
quizBank.explanation: 100M / 50,000 = 2,000 servers. In practice, add 20% buffer = 2,400 servers. Modern servers with event-loop architecture (Go, Node.js) can handle 50,000-100,000 WebSocket connections per server.

quizBank.question: A user receives 1,000 notifications while their phone is off. When they turn it on, what should happen?
quizBank.answer: Mobile OS batches and delivers push notifications (FCM/APNS handles queuing during offline). App receives push notification with badge count. On app open, client connects via WebSocket and sends last_seen_message_id per conversation. Server queries Cassandra for messages after that ID. Client renders 'X unread messages' badge. Individual notifications are collapsed (FCM supports notification collapse keys) to avoid notification flooding.

quizBank.question: Explain how you would implement end-to-end encryption for group messages.
quizBank.answer: Use Signal Protocol's Sender Keys: (1) Group creator generates a random Sender Key (group encryption key). (2) Sender Key is encrypted with each member's public key (X3DH key exchange) and distributed to all members. (3) Messages are encrypted with the Sender Key — only once per message regardless of group size. (4) Server stores only ciphertext — cannot read messages. (5) When a member joins/leaves: rotate Sender Key, re-distribute to current members. (6) Key management: each device has its own key pair. The server stores encrypted key bundles.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a Hybrid Logical Clock (HLC) in a distributed chat system?
options: To assign causally consistent timestamps that capture message ordering across nodes
quizBank.answer: To assign causally consistent timestamps that capture message ordering across nodes
quizBank.explanation: Lamport clocks provide causal ordering but not wall-clock time. Physical clocks drift. HLC combines both: max(physical_time, logical_time) + counter. This gives messages timestamps that are close to real time and causally consistent, enabling correct ordering even across distributed servers.

quizBank.question: How do you handle typing indicators without overloading the server?
quizBank.answer: Throttle: client sends 'typing start' event once, then suppresses further events for 3 seconds. After 5 seconds of no typing, send 'typing stop'. Server fan-outs to conversation members via Redis Pub/Sub. Do NOT persist typing events to DB — pure in-memory. Rate limit: max 1 typing event per user per conversation per 3 seconds. For large groups (>20 members), aggregate: 'Alice and 3 others are typing' rather than individual indicators.

quizBank.question: What is the role of Kafka in the chat message pipeline?
options: Decouple message processing: persistence, notifications, analytics
quizBank.answer: Decouple message processing: persistence, notifications, analytics
quizBank.explanation: Kafka is a durable event bus. Chat server publishes message events to Kafka. Multiple consumers process independently: Message Store writes to Cassandra, Notification Service sends push notifications, Analytics Service logs events. This decoupling enables independent scaling and failure isolation.

quizBank.question: Design the unread message count feature. How do you efficiently show '5 unread' per conversation?
quizBank.answer: Per-member approach: store last_read_message_id in conversation_members table. Unread count = SELECT COUNT(*) FROM messages WHERE conversation_id=? AND message_id > last_read_message_id. This is expensive at scale. Better: maintain a Redis counter per (user, conversation): INCR unread:{user_id}:{conv_id} on new message, RESET to 0 on read. Trade-off: Redis counter can drift if messages are deleted. Periodic reconciliation query from DB as ground truth.

quizBank.question: Your Kafka cluster goes down. What happens to message delivery?
quizBank.answer: Messages still flow via WebSocket (real-time delivery is not Kafka-dependent in the primary path). Kafka outage affects: (1) Message persistence — messages not written to Cassandra, risk of data loss for delivered-but-not-persisted messages. (2) Push notifications — offline users don't get notified. (3) Analytics — events dropped. Mitigation: Circuit breaker — if Kafka down, write directly to DB (synchronous, higher latency). Buffer events in local memory, replay when Kafka recovers. Alert on Kafka consumer lag.

quizBank.question: What Cassandra partition key design supports efficient message retrieval for a conversation?
options: Partition by conversation_id, cluster by message_id
Partition by date, cluster by conversation_id
quizBank.answer: Partition by conversation_id, cluster by message_id
quizBank.explanation: All messages for a conversation are in the same partition (fast range scans by time). Clustering by message_id (Snowflake, time-ordered) enables efficient pagination queries: 'give me 50 messages before message_id X'. Partitioning by user_id would scatter group messages.

quizBank.question: How would you design message search ('find all messages containing hello') at scale?
quizBank.answer: Do NOT query Cassandra with LIKE — it cannot do full-text search efficiently. Options: (1) Elasticsearch: index messages in near-real-time. Search by user's conversations + keyword. Expensive storage. (2) Apache Solr with conversation-level sharding. (3) For privacy (E2E encrypted): client-side search — store encrypted index on device, search locally. WhatsApp uses client-side search for this reason. For non-E2E: Elasticsearch with conversation_id filter, paginated results, highlight matching text.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between push notifications and WebSocket delivery? When do you use each?
quizBank.answer: WebSocket: app is open and connected. Message delivered instantly via persistent connection. No involvement of FCM/APNS. Push notifications: app is in background or closed. Server sends to FCM/APNS → OS delivers notification to device. Wakes app if configured. Use WebSocket for active users; push notification for offline/background users. Check presence before deciding which path to use. Many systems do both in parallel: WebSocket for immediate delivery, push notification as fallback.

quizBank.question: What mechanism prevents a message from being delivered twice if the sender retries due to network timeout?
quizBank.answer: Idempotency keys (unique message_id per message)
quizBank.explanation: Client generates a unique message_id (UUID or Snowflake) before sending. Server does INSERT IF NOT EXISTS (or upsert) by message_id. If client retries the same message, the second insert is a no-op. The server returns the same result. This achieves exactly-once delivery semantics.

quizBank.question: A chat server needs to be deployed with zero downtime. How do you drain WebSocket connections?
quizBank.answer: Graceful shutdown procedure: (1) Remove server from load balancer (stop new connections). (2) Send a 'reconnect' signal to all connected clients via WebSocket. (3) Clients reconnect to other servers (they get routed to different servers by LB). (4) Wait for all connections to drop (timeout: 30 seconds). (5) Shut down process. Load balancer health check fails → server is removed from rotation. Redis user→server mapping is updated when clients reconnect. Total downtime for individual users: <1 second.

quizBank.question: How do you handle the case where two users send messages to each other at the exact same millisecond?
quizBank.answer: Network non-determinism means message ordering can be ambiguous. Solutions: (1) Snowflake IDs have sub-millisecond resolution (12-bit sequence counter within same millisecond). Messages from different servers still get unique IDs but relative order is ambiguous. (2) Use conversation-level sequence numbers: single Redis INCR per conversation ensures strict monotonic ordering. Trade-off: Redis becomes a bottleneck for high-frequency conversations. (3) Accept eventual consistency: display both messages, let Cassandra's row ordering by message_id determine display order. Users perceive this as simultaneous — acceptable UX.

quizBank.question: What is the 'session affinity' or 'sticky session' problem with WebSocket servers?
options: Users can only connect to one server forever
Standard load balancers route WebSocket upgrades incorrectly
WebSocket connections are stateful — user must stay connected to same server
WebSocket requires more CPU than HTTP
quizBank.answer: WebSocket connections are stateful — user must stay connected to same server
quizBank.explanation: Unlike HTTP (stateless), a WebSocket connection is a persistent stateful connection to a specific server. The load balancer must route the initial HTTP upgrade request and then maintain that connection to the same server. Use IP hash or cookie-based routing in LB, or use a Layer 7 LB that understands WebSocket.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Chat System (WhatsApp/Slack)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design a News Feed (Facebook/Twitter)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Users can post updates (text, images, videos)
- Users see a personalized feed of posts from people they follow
- Feed is ranked (not chronological) by relevance/engagement
- Real-time updates: new posts appear without page refresh
- Pagination: infinite scroll
- Like, comment, share interactions
- Hashtags and mentions

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Low latency feed load: <200ms p99
- High availability: 99.99%
- Eventual consistency acceptable (feed can be slightly stale)
- Scale: 1B DAU, 500M posts/day, each user follows avg 200 people

## 2. Capacity Estimation

### Traffic
- Post writes: 500M/day = ~5,800 writes/sec
- Feed reads: assume each user reads feed 10x/day = 10B reads/day = ~116,000 reads/sec
- Like/comment events: ~2B/day = ~23,000 events/sec

### Fan-out
- User with 1M followers posts → 1M feed updates needed (fan-out on write)
- Average 200 followers: 5,800 posts/sec x 200 = 1.16M feed updates/sec
- Celebrity problem: 1 post → 1M updates in seconds

### Storage
- Post: ~1KB (text + metadata)
- 500M posts/day x 1KB x 365 = ~183TB/year for posts
- Feed cache: store 500 posts per user in Redis: 1B users x 500 x 8 bytes (post_id) = 4TB RAM (just IDs)
- Media: 10% posts with 1MB media = 50M x 1MB = 50TB/day

## 3. Fan-out Strategies

### Fan-out on Write (Push Model)
- When user posts, immediately push post_id to all followers' feed caches
- Pros: fast feed read (pre-computed), simple read path
- Cons: huge write amplification for celebrities, wasted work for inactive users

### Fan-out on Read (Pull Model)
- Feed is computed at read time: fetch posts from all followed users
- Pros: no write amplification, always fresh
- Cons: high read latency (must query N users' timelines), complex aggregation

### Hybrid Model (Recommended)
- Normal users (< 10K followers): fan-out on write
- Celebrities (> 10K followers): fan-out on read
- Active users: pre-compute feed
- Inactive users (>30 days offline): skip fan-out, compute on next login

```mermaid
graph TD
    User([User Posts])
    PostService[Post Service]
    PostDB[(Post Store - MySQL Sharded)]
    MQ[Kafka - post_events]
    FanoutWorker[Fan-out Worker]
    FollowerService[Follower Service]
    FeedCache[(Redis Feed Cache per user)]
    CelebPosts[(Celebrity Post Index)]
    FeedService[Feed Service]
    RankingEngine[Ranking Engine ML]
    Client([Client - Read Feed])
    CDN[CDN - Media]
    ObjectStore[S3 - Media]

    User -->|POST /post| PostService
    PostService --> PostDB
    PostService --> MQ
    MQ --> FanoutWorker
    FanoutWorker --> FollowerService
    FanoutWorker -->|Normal users| FeedCache
    FeedCache --> FeedService
    CelebPosts --> FeedService
    FeedService --> RankingEngine
    RankingEngine --> Client
    PostDB -->|On read miss| FeedService
    ObjectStore --> CDN
    CDN --> Client
```

## 4. Feed Ranking Algorithm

### Signals Used
- **Affinity**: how often do you interact with this person?
- **Weight**: post type (video > image > text), recency
- **Time decay**: older posts score lower exponentially
- **Engagement velocity**: posts getting rapid likes/shares boosted
- **User preferences**: topics/hashtags user engages with

### Score Formula (simplified)
```
score = affinity_score * type_weight * time_decay(age_hours) * engagement_velocity
time_decay = e^(-age_hours / 24)
```

### Ranking Architecture
- Candidate generation: fetch top 500 candidate posts from feed cache
- Scoring: ML model assigns score to each candidate
- Filtering: remove blocked users, already-seen posts, spam
- Return top 20-50 posts per page

## 5. Data Model

### Posts Table (MySQL, sharded by user_id)
```sql
CREATE TABLE posts (
    post_id     BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,  -- Snowflake ID
    user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    content     TEXT,
    media_urls  JSON,
    post_type   ENUM('text','image','video'),
    like_count  INT DEFAULT 0,
    comment_count INT DEFAULT 0,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    is_deleted  BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE
);
CREATE INDEX idx_user_posts ON posts(user_id, created_at DESC);
```

### Social Graph (follows) — Neo4j or MySQL
```sql
CREATE TABLE follows (
    follower_id   BIGINT,
    followee_id   BIGINT,
    created_at    TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id)
);
CREATE INDEX idx_followees ON follows(followee_id);  -- who follows celebrity X
```

### Feed Cache (Redis)
```
Key: feed:{user_id}
Type: Sorted Set
Score: post_rank_score (or timestamp for chronological)
Member: post_id
Capacity: 500 post_ids per user
```

## 6. API Design

```
POST /api/v1/posts
Body: { "content": "...", "media_ids": [...], "visibility": "public" }
Response 201: { "post_id": "...", "created_at": "..." }

GET /api/v1/feed?cursor={last_seen_post_id}&limit=20
Response 200: { "posts": [...], "next_cursor": "...", "has_more": true }

GET /api/v1/users/{user_id}/posts?before={post_id}&limit=20
Response 200: { "posts": [...] }

POST /api/v1/posts/{post_id}/like
Response 200: { "like_count": 1523 }

GET /api/v1/posts/{post_id}/comments?cursor={cursor}&limit=20
Response 200: { "comments": [...], "next_cursor": "..." }

GET /api/v1/trending?hashtag={tag}
Response 200: { "posts": [...] }
```

## 7. Real-Time Feed Updates

### Options
- **Polling**: Client polls /feed every 30 seconds. Simple, high latency, wasteful.
- **Long polling**: Client holds connection. Server responds when new post arrives. Better latency.
- **Server-Sent Events (SSE)**: Server pushes new post IDs to client. Client fetches full post. Recommended.
- **WebSocket**: Overkill for feed (server-to-client only needed).

### SSE-based Real-Time Updates
- Client connects: `GET /api/v1/feed/stream`
- Server holds SSE connection
- When new post arrives for user (fan-out complete), push `data: {"new_post_id": "..."}`
- Client fetches full post details and prepends to feed

## 8. Pagination Strategy

### Cursor-based Pagination (Recommended)
- Use post_id (Snowflake, time-ordered) as cursor
- `GET /feed?cursor=12345&limit=20` → returns posts where post_id < 12345
- Stable: not affected by new posts being added to top of feed
- Efficient: index seek on post_id, no OFFSET

### Why not OFFSET pagination?
- `LIMIT 20 OFFSET 1000` scans 1020 rows, returns last 20 — O(n) inefficiency
- Unstable: new posts shift pages, causing duplicates or skipped posts

## 9. Media Handling

### Upload Flow
1. Client requests presigned S3 URL from Post Service
2. Client uploads directly to S3 (large file, no round-trip through app servers)
3. S3 triggers Lambda → generates thumbnails, transcodes video
4. Media URL stored in post record
5. CDN caches media with long TTL (media is immutable)

## 10. Scalability & Bottlenecks

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Celebrity (10M followers)
    participant PS as Post Service
    participant K as Kafka
    participant FW as Fan-out Workers (pool)
    participant RC as Redis Cluster
    participant FS as Feed Service
    participant U as User A

    C->>PS: Post new update
    PS->>K: Publish post_event
    Note over K,FW: Celebrity: skip fan-out on write
    K->>FW: Fan-out to normal followers only
    FW->>RC: ZADD feed:{follower_id} score post_id (bulk)
    U->>FS: GET /feed
    FS->>RC: ZRANGE feed:{user_id} (pre-built for normal users)
    FS->>PS: Fetch celebrity posts (on-demand)
    FS-->>U: Merged, ranked feed
```

- **Like counter**: use Redis INCR (in-memory), batch-write to DB periodically to avoid DB hammering
- **Fan-out lag**: for celebrities, workers process in parallel by partitioning follower list
- **Feed cache eviction**: LRU, keep only 500 post IDs per user
- **Cold start**: new user with no follows → show trending/recommended posts

## 11. Monitoring
- Feed load latency p50/p95/p99
- Fan-out queue depth (Kafka lag)
- Feed cache hit rate (target >95%)
- Post write throughput
- Like/comment event processing lag
- ML ranking model inference latency

sessions.title: Fan-out Strategies
sessions.goals: Master push vs pull vs hybrid models.

activities.description: Design pure fan-out on write system.
Design pure fan-out on read system.
Draw data flow for each approach.

sessions.content:
## Session 3: Fan-out Strategies

### Fan-out on Write (Push Model)

When User A posts:
1. Save post to Posts DB
2. Look up all of A's followers (from Followers table)
3. For each follower, insert `post_id` into their feed cache

**Pros:**
- Feed reads are instant — data is pre-computed
- No computation at read time
- Feed read latency is O(1) Redis GET

**Cons:**
- Writing to 100M follower caches takes too long (celebrity problem)
- Wasted writes: followers who never open the app still get feed updates
- Data staleness: if a post is deleted after fan-out, you need cache invalidation across 100M entries

**Suitable for:** Regular users with <10,000 followers.

### Fan-out on Read (Pull Model)

When User B requests their feed:
1. Look up all accounts B follows (N accounts)
2. Query each account's recent posts
3. Merge and rank the results

**Pros:**
- No write fan-out — works for any follower count
- Always shows current data (no cache invalidation issues)
- No wasted computation for inactive users

**Cons:**
- Read is expensive: if B follows 1,000 accounts, that's 1,000 DB queries per feed request
- Latency: p99 feed load > 1 second at scale
- Not feasible for users with large followee lists

**Suitable for:** Celebrity accounts (reading their posts on demand).

### Hybrid Fan-out (Production Approach)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Post[New Post Created]
    FW[Fan-out Worker]
    UT[User Type Check]
    Cache[Redis Feed Cache
for regular users]
    DB[(Posts DB)]
    Merge[Feed Merge Service
at read time]

    Post -->|publish| FW
    FW -->|check follower count| UT
    UT -->|<10K followers| Cache
    UT -->|celebrity| DB
    Merge -->|read cache| Cache
    Merge -->|pull celebrity posts| DB
    Merge -->|final merged feed| User([User])
```

**Logic:**
- Threshold: if user has <10,000 followers → fan-out on write
- If user has ≥10,000 followers (celebrity) → fan-out on read
- At feed read time: merge pre-computed cache + fresh celebrity posts

### Fan-out Worker Implementation

```mermaid
graph LR
    PS[Post Service] -->|publish| K[Kafka
post-events]
    K -->|partition by user_id| FW1[Fan-out Worker 1]
    K -->|partition by user_id| FW2[Fan-out Worker 2]
    FW1 -->|batch write| Redis[(Redis
Feed Cache)]
    FW2 -->|batch write| Redis
    FW1 -->|fetch followers| FDB[(Followers DB)]
```

**Batch optimization:** Instead of writing one Redis entry per follower sequentially, batch pipeline:
```python
pipeline = redis.pipeline()
for follower_id in followers:  # up to 10K
    feed_key = f"feed:{follower_id}"
    pipeline.lpush(feed_key, post_id)
    pipeline.ltrim(feed_key, 0, 199)  # keep only 200 most recent posts
pipeline.execute()  # single round-trip
```

**Throughput:** 2,000 posts/sec × 500 avg followers × 1 Redis op = 1M Redis ops/sec. Redis cluster handles 500K ops/sec per shard → need 2+ shards.

### Interview Q&A

**Q: What's the threshold for celebrity vs. regular user?**
A: Common choices are 1,000, 10,000, or 100,000 followers. It's a tunable parameter. Lower threshold means more pull computation at read time; higher threshold means more fan-out write load. 10,000 is a reasonable default — covers <0.01% of users but >50% of posts.

**Q: How do you handle a user who oscillates around the celebrity threshold?**
A: Hysteresis: use two thresholds (celebrity at >10K, back to regular at <8K). Cache the classification in Redis with 1-hour TTL. Prevents thrashing for users right at the boundary.

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Fan-out Strategies — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Fan-out Strategies ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Fan-out Strategies");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

Implement a working solution in Java and Python

sessions.title: Feed Ranking

activities.description: List ranking signals: affinity, weight, time decay, engagement.
Design candidate generation → scoring → filtering pipeline.
Where does ML model live? How is it updated?

sessions.content:
## Session 5: Feed Ranking

### Why Not Pure Chronological?

Chronological feed has a fundamental problem: a user following 1,000 accounts generates an overwhelming volume of posts. Users miss important posts from close friends because they're buried under noise from media accounts they casually follow.

ML ranking solves this by surfacing the most relevant posts first.

### Ranking Signals

**Recency score:** Exponential decay over time.
```
recency_score = e^(-λ × hours_since_posted)
where λ = 0.1 → half-life ~7 hours
```

**Engagement score:** Normalized engagement relative to author's typical engagement.
```
engagement_score = (likes × 1 + comments × 3 + shares × 5) / author_avg_engagement
```

**Relationship score:** Strength of relationship between viewer and author.
```
relationship_score = f(mutual_follows, comment_history, DM_history, profile_visits)
```

**Content relevance:** Similarity between post topics and user's interest graph.

### Scoring Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    FeedReq[Feed Request
from User B]
    PostIDs[200 Post IDs
from Cache]
    Scorer[Scoring Service]
    RModel[Ranking Model
(lightweight ML)]
    Ranker[Sort & Filter]
    Result[Top 20 Posts]

    FeedReq --> PostIDs
    PostIDs -->|batch fetch metadata| Scorer
    Scorer -->|feature vectors| RModel
    RModel -->|scores| Ranker
    Ranker --> Result
```

**Score computation:** For each of 200 candidate posts, compute a scalar score in ~1ms. Total: 200ms for scoring + 20ms for Redis reads = within 200ms p99 budget.

**Model:** Logistic regression or shallow gradient boosting tree (not deep learning — too slow for 100K req/sec). Features: 20-30 numerical signals. Inference: <1ms per post.

### Candidate Generation vs. Ranking

Not all posts can be scored — at 500M users × 200 posts each = 100 billion posts in the DB. Ranking pipeline has two stages:

**Stage 1: Candidate Generation (pre-computed)**
- Feed cache provides 200 candidates (already filtered to users you follow)
- Celebrity posts fetched at read time (up to 10 celebrities × 20 recent posts = 200 more candidates)
- Total: ~400 candidates

**Stage 2: Ranking**
- Score all 400 candidates
- Sort by score descending
- Return top 20 (first page)

### Feed Freshness vs. Computation Cost

**Problem:** Re-ranking 400 posts on every feed request at 100K req/sec is expensive.

**Solution: Cached ranked feed with TTL**
- After first ranking computation: cache ranked post_id list in Redis with 5-minute TTL
- Subsequent requests within 5 minutes: serve from ranked cache
- After 5 minutes: expire cache, trigger re-ranking on next request

```
Key: ranked_feed:{user_id}
Value: [post_id_1, ..., post_id_200]  (pre-ranked)
TTL: 300 seconds
```

**Real-time injection:** When user is actively browsing (WebSocket connected), new posts from followed accounts are injected at the top of the feed without re-ranking the entire list.

### Filtering

Before ranking, apply hard filters:
- Remove posts from blocked/muted accounts
- Remove already-seen posts (track per user using Redis Set with 24h TTL)
- Remove posts older than 7 days (from non-celebrity accounts)
- Remove posts flagged by content moderation

### Interview Q&A

**Q: How do you A/B test ranking algorithm changes?**
A: Route 5% of traffic to new ranking model. Compare engagement metrics (click-through rate, time spent, return rate) between control and experiment groups. Run for 2 weeks for statistical significance. Gradually ramp if positive.

**Q: What happens to ranking when the user has very few follows (new user)?**
A: Cold start problem. For new users with <10 follows: supplement feed with "trending" posts from popular accounts in their declared interest categories. As they interact with posts (likes, comments), build personalization signal over time.

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Feed Ranking — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Feed Ranking ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Feed Ranking");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

### Python Implementation

```python
from typing import Dict, List, Any

def demonstrate():
    """Feed Ranking — Key concepts demonstration"""
    print(f"=== Feed Ranking ===")

    # Core data structures
    config: Dict[str, Any] = {
        'topic': 'Feed Ranking',
        'complexity': 'O(n)',
        'space': 'O(1)',
    }
    for k, v in config.items():
        print(f'{k}: {v}')

    # Typical operations
    data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    print(f'Input: {data}')
    print(f'Size: {len(data)}')
    print(f'Contains 3: {3 in data}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    demonstrate()
```

Analyze time and space complexity

sessions.title: Real-Time Updates
sessions.goals: Push new posts to user feed in real-time.

activities.description: Compare polling vs long-poll vs SSE vs WebSocket for feeds.
Design notification for new posts available.

sessions.content:
## Session 7: Real-Time Updates

### Real-Time Feed Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    User([Active User])
    WS[WebSocket Server]
    PS[Post Service]
    FW[Fan-out Worker]
    Kafka[Kafka
feed-updates topic]
    Redis[Redis
Feed Cache]

    PS -->|new post created| Kafka
    Kafka --> FW
    FW -->|push to active followers| WS
    FW -->|update feed cache| Redis
    WS -->|WebSocket push| User
```

### Presence-Aware Fan-out

The Fan-out Worker knows which followers are currently online (via Presence Service). For online users: push new post directly via WebSocket for immediate display. For offline users: write to feed cache only (they get it on next app open).

This optimizes resource usage — no point pushing WebSocket events to offline devices.

```python
def fanout_post(post_id, poster_id):
    followers = get_followers(poster_id)  # up to 10K for non-celebrities
    online_followers = presence_service.filter_online(followers)
    offline_followers = [f for f in followers if f not in online_followers]

    # Real-time push to online followers
    for follower_id in online_followers:
        ws_server = connection_registry.get_server(follower_id)
        kafka.publish(f"ws_server_{ws_server}", {
            "type": "new_post",
            "recipient": follower_id,
            "post_id": post_id
        })

    # Cache update for all followers
    pipeline = redis.pipeline()
    for follower_id in followers:
        pipeline.lpush(f"feed:{follower_id}", post_id)
        pipeline.ltrim(f"feed:{follower_id}", 0, 199)
    pipeline.execute()
```

### WebSocket Connection Management for Feed

Dedicated feed WebSocket connection per user (separate from chat WebSocket):
- Client connects on app open, disconnects on background
- Server sends typed events: `new_post`, `post_updated` (like count), `post_deleted`
- Heartbeat: ping/pong every 30 seconds

**Backpressure:** If user has 500 follows and all post simultaneously (unlikely but possible during events), rate-limit injections to 5 posts/second per user. Buffer remaining in a client-side queue.

### Like Count Real-Time Updates

```
User A likes a post → POST /api/v1/posts/{post_id}/likes
Like Service:
  1. Record like in likes table (idempotent)
  2. INCR post:{post_id}:likes in Redis (fast counter)
  3. Publish like_event to Kafka

Kafka consumer:
  1. Update like_count in Cassandra posts table (eventual)
  2. Broadcast to users currently viewing that post:
     WebSocket: { "type": "like_count_update", "post_id": "...", "count": 43 }
```

### SSE as Alternative to WebSocket

Server-Sent Events (SSE) is simpler than WebSocket for one-directional server → client updates:
- Client connects to `GET /api/v1/feed/stream` with `Accept: text/event-stream`
- Server pushes events as they occur
- Automatic reconnection built into browser EventSource API

**Trade-off:** SSE is simpler to implement (regular HTTP, no upgrade negotiation) but only server → client. For feeds where updates only flow from server to client, SSE is sufficient and more operationally simple than WebSocket.

### Feed "New Posts Available" Badge

Instead of auto-inserting new posts (which disrupts reading), show a badge:
```
WebSocket event: { "type": "new_posts_available", "count": 5 }
Client shows: "5 new posts" button at top of feed
On tap: scroll to top, fetch fresh feed from API
```

This prevents jarring scroll position jumps while keeping the feed feel fresh.

### Interview Q&A

**Q: How do you handle a user who follows 5,000 accounts? Fan-out takes too long.**
A: Apply celebrity threshold to followees too. If B follows >1,000 accounts, switch to pull model for B's feed: instead of pushing to B's cache, B's feed is computed on-demand by querying recent posts from their top 200 most-engaged followees. Pre-rank followees by interaction history and only fetch from top-N for performance.

**Q: What if the WebSocket connection drops while new posts are queued?**
A: On reconnect, client sends its last-seen post_id. Server computes: "how many posts appeared after this ID in your feed?" and sends a catch-up batch. This ensures no missed posts even through connection interruptions.

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Real-Time Updates — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Real-Time Updates ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Real-Time Updates");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

sessions.title: Like Counters & Interactions

activities.description: Batch write to DB every 5 seconds.

sessions.content:
## Session 8: Like Counters & Interactions

### The Counter Problem

Likes are the highest-write operation in a news feed system. A viral post can receive 100,000 likes/minute. Naive approach: `UPDATE posts SET like_count = like_count + 1 WHERE post_id = ?` — this creates a write hotspot.

### Counter Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    LikeReq[Like Request] -->|INCR| Redis[(Redis Counter
post:id:likes)]
    Redis -->|async flush every 60s| Batch[Batch Updater]
    Batch -->|UPDATE like_count| Cass[(Cassandra)]
    Redis -->|real-time read| API[Like Count API]
```

**Redis as the source of truth for real-time counts:**
```
INCR post:{post_id}:likes    → atomic increment
GET  post:{post_id}:likes    → current count (real-time)
```

**Cassandra for persistence (flushed every 60s):**
```cql
UPDATE posts SET like_count = like_count + {delta}
WHERE user_id = ? AND post_id = ?;
```

Delta = (current Redis count) - (last flushed count). Batch updates reduce Cassandra write pressure from 100K/sec to 1 write/minute per post.

### Likes Table (Who Liked What)

```sql
CREATE TABLE likes (
    post_id    UUID NOT NULL,
    user_id    BIGINT NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    PRIMARY KEY (post_id, user_id)
);
CREATE INDEX idx_likes_user ON likes(user_id);
```

**Idempotent like:** `INSERT INTO likes ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` prevents double-counting.

**Unlike:** `DELETE FROM likes WHERE post_id = ? AND user_id = ?`, then `DECR post:{post_id}:likes`.

### Distributed Like Race Condition

Two users like the same post simultaneously:
- Redis INCR is atomic — no race condition in the counter
- PostgreSQL `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` handles duplicate inserts
- Total count is always accurate

### Comments Architecture

Comments are deeply nested in some systems (Twitter has threads, Facebook has flat comments). Design for flat comments with optional reply depth of 1 (reply to a comment, but not to a reply).

```sql
CREATE TABLE comments (
    comment_id  BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    post_id     UUID NOT NULL,
    user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    parent_id   BIGINT REFERENCES comments(comment_id),  -- for replies
    content     TEXT NOT NULL,
    like_count  BIGINT DEFAULT 0,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_comments_post ON comments(post_id, created_at DESC);
```

**Fetch top comments:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM comments
WHERE post_id = ? AND parent_id IS NULL
ORDER BY like_count DESC, created_at DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

### Share/Repost Mechanics

Shares create a new post object with reference to the original:

```sql
CREATE TABLE posts (
    -- ... other fields ...
    is_repost        BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    original_post_id UUID REFERENCES posts(post_id)
);
```

Repost fan-out: same fan-out worker handles reposts — post_id of the repost (not original) is pushed to follower feeds.

### Interview Q&A

**Q: How do you prevent like farming (bots liking posts)?**
A: Rate limit per user: max 100 likes/hour via Redis token bucket. Require authenticated session (JWT). Bot detection: if account age <24h and has liked >50 posts, flag for review. IP-based rate limiting as secondary layer.

**Q: Should like counts be exact or approximate?**
A: For posts with <10K likes, exact is feasible and expected. For viral posts with >1M likes, approximate (±0.1%) via Redis HyperLogLog or delayed counting is acceptable — users don't distinguish 1,247,832 from 1,247,891. Most systems use exact counts up to ~100K, then round to "1.2M" for display.

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Like Counters & Interactions — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Like Counters & Interactions ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Like Counters & Interactions");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

### Python Implementation

def demonstrate():
    """Like Counters & Interactions — Key concepts demonstration"""
    print(f"=== Like Counters & Interactions ===")

    # Core data structures
    config: Dict[str, Any] = {
        'topic': 'Like Counters & Interactions',
        'complexity': 'O(n)',
        'space': 'O(1)',
    }
    for k, v in config.items():
        print(f'{k}: {v}')

    # Typical operations
    data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    print(f'Input: {data}')
    print(f'Size: {len(data)}')
    print(f'Contains 3: {3 in data}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    demonstrate()

sessions.title: Media Pipeline
sessions.content:
## Session 9: Media Pipeline

### Image Upload Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant AS as API Server
    participant S3 as S3 Storage
    participant MQ as Media Queue (SQS)
    participant MP as Media Processor (Lambda)
    participant CDN

    Client->>AS: POST /api/v1/media/presign {filename, size}
    AS->>S3: Generate presigned upload URL
    AS->>Client: {upload_url, media_id}
    Client->>S3: PUT image (direct to S3)
    S3->>MQ: S3 Event notification (upload complete)
    MQ->>MP: Trigger processing
    MP->>S3: Read original, process, write thumbnails
    MP->>AS: Callback: media_id ready
    Note over CDN: CDN serves from S3 origin
```

### Media Processing

For each uploaded image, Lambda generates:
1. **Original** (compressed): WebP at 85% quality (typically 40% smaller than JPEG)
2. **Large**: 1080px wide, WebP, quality 80 — for post view
3. **Thumbnail**: 400×400 square crop, WebP, quality 75 — for feed list
4. **Blur hash**: 32-byte perceptual hash — for placeholder before image loads

Storage path: `s3://media-bucket/{user_id}/{media_id}/{variant}.webp`

CDN URL: `https://media.example.com/{user_id}/{media_id}/thumb.webp`

### Media Validation

Before accepting upload:
- File type: only JPEG, PNG, WebP, HEIC (detected by magic bytes, not extension)
- File size: max 10MB
- Dimensions: min 100×100, max 8000×8000 pixels
- Content safety scan: async — moderate after upload, flag if NSFW

### CDN Configuration

```
Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable
```

Media URLs include content hash — once uploaded, content never changes. CDN can cache indefinitely. Cache invalidation is never needed (immutable content with content-addressed URLs).

**Edge locations:** Use 50+ PoPs. Images served with <50ms latency globally. Cache hit rate: >95% for popular posts (viral posts served entirely from CDN edge).

### Video Support (Brief)

Even though current requirements are images-only, briefly address video for completeness:

**Key differences from images:**
- Transcoding is CPU-intensive: 1 minute of 1080p video → 10 minutes of transcoding
- Multiple resolutions needed: 240p, 360p, 480p, 720p, 1080p
- Adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH) for network-aware playback
- Dedicated video processing cluster (not Lambda — too slow for large files)

**Architecture: separate Video Processing Service** using AWS MediaConvert or custom FFmpeg cluster.

### Storage Cost Optimization

**Lifecycle policies:**
- S3 Standard: first 30 days (hot access)
- S3 Standard-IA: 30 days to 1 year (infrequent access, 40% cheaper)
- S3 Glacier: after 1 year (archival, 80% cheaper)

**Deduplication:** Hash image content (SHA-256) at upload time. If hash exists: return existing media_id without re-storing. Saves storage for commonly re-shared images.

**Estimated cost:** At 5TB/day images:
- S3 storage: 5TB × 30 days × $0.023/GB ≈ $3,450/month
- CDN bandwidth: assuming 50% cache hit, 2.5TB/day × $0.085/GB ≈ $6,600/month
- Lambda processing: 10M images/day × $0.0001/image ≈ $30/day = $900/month

### Interview Q&A

**Q: Why use presigned URLs instead of uploading through your API server?**
A: Direct-to-S3 upload skips your API servers entirely — client uploads at full bandwidth to the nearest S3 edge. Your API server only handles the tiny presign request (1KB) instead of a 10MB file upload. This reduces API server load by orders of magnitude and avoids the API server becoming a bandwidth bottleneck.

**Q: How do you handle partially uploaded images (client crashes mid-upload)?**
A: S3 multipart upload with a cleanup lifecycle rule. Parts from incomplete uploads are automatically deleted after 7 days by S3 lifecycle policy. The media_id is only marked "ready" after the Media Processor confirms successful processing — partial uploads never reach users.

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Media Pipeline — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Media Pipeline ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Media Pipeline");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

### Python Implementation

def demonstrate():

    # Core data structures
    config: Dict[str, Any] = {
        'topic': 'Media Pipeline',

        'space': 'O(1)',
    }
    for k, v in config.items():
        print(f'{k}: {v}')

    # Typical operations
    data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    print(f'Input: {data}')
    print(f'Size: {len(data)}')
    print(f'Contains 3: {3 in data}')

    demonstrate()

sessions.title: Mock Interview

activities.description: Must cover: fan-out decision, celebrity handling, ranking concept, cache strategy.

sessions.content:
## Session 10: Mock Interview

### Full Interview Simulation (45 Minutes)

**Interviewer:** "Design Facebook's News Feed."

---

**[Minutes 0-5: Requirements]**

**Candidate:** "Clarifying questions: Is the feed ranked or chronological?"

**Interviewer:** "Ranked, like Facebook — ML-based."

**Candidate:** "Bidirectional friends or unidirectional follows?"

**Interviewer:** "Bidirectional friends."

**Candidate:** "Max friends per user?"

**Interviewer:** "5,000 friends — Facebook's limit."

**Candidate:** "Scale — DAU?"

**Interviewer:** "1 billion DAU."

**Candidate:** "That changes my capacity math significantly. Are there celebrity accounts with millions of followers, or is 5,000 the hard cap?"

**Interviewer:** "Pages can have unlimited followers, users capped at 5,000."

**Candidate:** "Functional: view feed (top 20 ranked posts from friends + pages), create posts (text/images), like/comment. Real-time updates when active. Non-functional: feed load <200ms p99, 99.99% availability, eventual consistency for feed updates up to 10 seconds."

---

**[Minutes 5-10: Capacity Estimation]**

**Candidate:** "1B DAU. Posts: assume 1 post per user per 10 days average = 100M posts/day = ~1,160/sec. Peak 5x: ~6,000/sec.

Feed reads: 1B users × 5 feed opens/day / 86,400 = ~57,000 req/sec. Peak: 170K req/sec.

Fan-out: 1,160 posts/sec × avg 500 friends = 580K cache writes/sec. Redis cluster with 10+ shards handles this.

Feed cache: 1B users × 200 post_ids × 8 bytes = 1.6TB. Huge — need tiered approach: only cache feeds for users active in last 24 hours (~30% = 300M users → 480GB). Still large, but manageable across 20+ Redis nodes."

---

**[Minutes 10-20: Architecture]**

**Candidate:** "Three major services: Post Service, Fan-out Service, Feed Service.

Post creation: Client → API Gateway → Post Service → Cassandra (persist) + Kafka (event).

Fan-out: Kafka consumer (Fan-out Service) reads post event. For regular users (<5K friends) with full friend list: batch write post_id to each friend's Redis feed list. For Pages (potentially millions of followers): fan-out on read — page posts are fetched at read time.

Feed read: Feed Service → Redis LRANGE (200 post_ids) → batch fetch post metadata → Ranking Service → return top 20.

Real-time: Fan-out Service also pushes to WebSocket servers for online users."

**Interviewer:** "How do you handle the 1TB+ feed cache size?"

**Candidate:** "Two optimizations. First, only maintain feed cache for users active in the last 24 hours — 1B × 30% active = 300M users. Second, for users whose cache has expired (inactive >24h): on next feed request, compute feed on-demand by fetching recent posts from their friends (pull from Cassandra). Cache the result for 24 hours. This amortizes the cost across time."

---

**[Minutes 20-32: Deep Dives]**

**Interviewer:** "Walk through ranking."

**Candidate:** "200 candidate posts from cache. Apply hard filters: blocked users, already seen, older than 7 days. Remaining ~150 candidates. Compute features for each: recency score (exponential decay), engagement rate (likes+comments normalized by author's history), relationship strength (comment/like interaction history with this friend), content interest match.

Scoring model: gradient boosting tree, 30 features, inference <1ms per post. Total ranking: 150ms for 150 posts. Fits in 200ms budget.

Cache ranked result per user for 5 minutes to avoid re-ranking on every request."

**Interviewer:** "What about the cold start problem — new users with no friends?"

**Candidate:** "Three phases: First, during onboarding, collect interest categories (sports, tech, news). Second, show 'suggested content' from popular pages in those categories — pull-based, no fan-out. Third, as user adds friends, fan-out kicks in gradually. The feed transitions from interest-based to social-graph-based over the first week."

---

**[Minutes 32-42: Failure Modes]**

**Interviewer:** "Redis cluster goes down — what happens?"

**Candidate:** "Feed reads fall back to on-demand computation from Cassandra. Latency increases from 50ms to 500ms — noticeable but functional. Post writes continue — posts go to Cassandra and Kafka. Fan-out stalls until Redis recovers. Recovery: Redis restores from RDB snapshot (last 15 minutes of data) + replays Kafka events from the last 15 minutes to rebuild feed caches.

Key insight: Redis is a performance layer, not the source of truth. Cassandra is durable. Recovery is catching up, not data loss."

**Interviewer:** "A celebrity page posts and triggers fan-out to 50 million followers."

**Candidate:** "Pages use fan-out on read — no fan-out writes happen. When a user opens their feed, Feed Service fetches recent posts from each page the user follows (up to 20 pages, last 5 posts each = 100 additional candidates). These merge with the cached feed from regular friends. The celebrity/page post appears within seconds without any fan-out write."

---

**[Minutes 42-45: Summary]**

**Key trade-offs articulated:**

| Decision | Choice | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Fan-out strategy | Hybrid | Write for friends (5K max), read for pages (unlimited) |
| Cache scope | Active users only (30%) | 1TB full cache impractical; lazy-load for inactive users |
| Ranking | Cached 5 minutes | Balance freshness vs. CPU cost at 170K req/sec |
| Feed consistency | 10s eventual | Real-time fan-out at 1B DAU too expensive; acceptable delay |
| Counter updates | Redis + async DB flush | Avoid Cassandra write hotspot on viral posts |

### Java Implementation

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    // Mock Interview — Key concepts demonstration

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Mock Interview ===");

        // Example: core data structures used in this topic
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Mock Interview");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)");

        for (var entry : config.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // Example: typical operations
        List<Integer> data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5);
        System.out.println("Input: " + data);
        System.out.println("Size: " + data.size());
        System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3));
    }
}
```

### Python Implementation

    }
