quizBank.question: What is the difference between FCM and APNS?
options: FCM is for email, APNS for push
FCM is Google's push service (Android/Web), APNS is Apple's push service (iOS)
FCM is for marketing, APNS for transactional
quizBank.answer: FCM is Google's push service (Android/Web), APNS is Apple's push service (iOS)
quizBank.explanation: Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) handles push notifications for Android devices and web browsers. Apple Push Notification Service (APNS) handles iOS/macOS push. Both require device tokens registered by the app. FCM can also deliver to iOS via FCM→APNS bridge.

quizBank.question: What happens when you try to send a push notification to an invalid/expired device token?
options: FCM/APNS returns an error — token should be removed from database
The notification is queued until token refreshes
quizBank.answer: FCM/APNS returns an error — token should be removed from database
quizBank.explanation: Invalid tokens: device was reset, app was uninstalled, or user revoked permissions. FCM returns 'NotRegistered' or 'InvalidRegistration'. Your system must remove this token from device_tokens table to stop wasting API calls.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'quiet hours' — not sending notifications between 10pm and 8am in user's local time?
options: Use UTC time and check globally
Store user's timezone preference, convert send time to user's local time, check if within quiet hours window
Send during server's business hours only
quizBank.answer: Store user's timezone preference, convert send time to user's local time, check if within quiet hours window
quizBank.explanation: User's preference table includes timezone (e.g., 'America/New_York'). At send time: convert current UTC to user's local time. If 22:00 < local_time < 08:00: either delay until 8am or drop notification (depending on priority). Critical notifications (security alerts) should override quiet hours.

quizBank.question: Your email notification service sends 2 million emails/day. SendGrid has a 4-hour outage. How do you handle this?
quizBank.answer: Multi-provider strategy: (1) Primary: SendGrid. (2) Failover: AWS SES configured in parallel. (3) Circuit breaker: detect SendGrid failures (error rate >10%), automatically route to SES. (4) Queue durability: emails queued in Kafka — not lost during outage. (5) On SendGrid recovery: process backlog, throttle to avoid overwhelming SendGrid. (6) User impact: transactional emails (OTP) delayed — alert on-call. Marketing emails: delay acceptable, process after recovery. (7) SLA: communicate to stakeholders. Monitor both providers' status pages.

quizBank.question: What legal requirement mandates that marketing emails include an unsubscribe link?
quizBank.answer: CAN-SPAM Act (US) and GDPR (EU)
quizBank.explanation: CAN-SPAM requires: clear opt-out mechanism, physical address, no misleading subject lines. GDPR requires: explicit consent to receive marketing emails, easy opt-out. One-click unsubscribe (List-Unsubscribe header) is now required by Gmail/Yahoo for bulk senders.

quizBank.question: You need to send a promotional notification to 50 million users at 9am in their local timezone. How do you architect this?
quizBank.answer: Cannot send all at once (provider rate limits ~1M/sec). Time-zone batching: (1) Group users by timezone. Compute send time per group: 9am EST = 14:00 UTC, 9am PST = 17:00 UTC, etc. (2) Schedule batch jobs per timezone group, staggered over 24 hours as timezones roll. (3) Each batch: load user IDs from DB, fetch device tokens, call FCM batch API (500 per request). (4) Parallelism: 1000 workers processing in parallel. 50M / 500 per batch = 100K batch API calls. At 1000 parallel workers: 100 seconds per timezone group. (5) Rate limiting: respect FCM's 1M notifications/sec global limit.

quizBank.question: How do you prevent notification fatigue — users getting too many notifications and turning them off?
quizBank.answer: Multi-layer approach: (1) User preferences: let users control frequency and channels. (2) Server-side rate limiting: max 5 push notifications per day per user (configurable per category). (3) Batching: combine multiple events into a single digest notification. (4) Smart send time: ML model predicts when user is most likely to engage — send then. (5) Priority filtering: only send high-priority items when user is in 'low engagement' mode (detected by recent non-opens). (6) A/B testing: test notification copy and frequency, optimize for engagement not volume. (7) Unsubscribe intelligence: when user opts out, understand why — too frequent, wrong content, wrong time.

quizBank.question: What is a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) in a notification system?
options: Queue where notifications go after exhausting all retry attempts, for manual review
quizBank.answer: Queue where notifications go after exhausting all retry attempts, for manual review
quizBank.explanation: After 3-5 retry attempts with backoff, a notification moves to DLQ. DLQ prevents poison-pill messages from blocking the main queue. Operators can inspect DLQ, fix root cause, and replay messages. Alert when DLQ depth exceeds threshold.

quizBank.question: A user has 5 devices (phone, tablet, 2 computers, smart TV). How do you manage notifications across all of them?
quizBank.answer: Per-device tokens: device_tokens table has one row per device per user. Push to all active devices by default. Issues: (1) Duplicate notification: same notification on all 5 devices simultaneously. (2) Solution: 'Read on one, dismiss on others' — require client SDK to mark notification as read; push 'cancel' notification to other devices. (3) Device priority: let user set primary device — only push to primary for certain categories. (4) Activity-based: push to most recently active device. (5) Token cleanup: remove tokens for devices not used in 90 days (TTL-based eviction from device_tokens table).

quizBank.question: How do you design idempotent notification delivery to prevent duplicate sends?
quizBank.answer: Every notification has a unique notification_id (UUID, generated by caller or by notification service). Idempotency check: (1) Before enqueueing: check if notification_id exists in notification_log. If yes: return existing status. (2) Before sending to provider: check notification_log. If status='sent': skip. (3) Provider-level: FCM supports idempotency keys. (4) Delivery log write: INSERT IF NOT EXISTS by notification_id. Use conditional writes (optimistic locking). (5) Exactly-once semantics difficult: at-least-once + idempotency check = effectively-once. Trade-off: idempotency check adds DB read per notification — can be Redis cache for hot notifications.

quizBank.question: A notification system sends 1,000 notifications/sec. Each notification requires one database read for user preferences. How do you avoid overloading the database?
options: Cache user preferences in Redis with 5-minute TTL
Store preferences in the notification payload
Only check preferences for some notifications
quizBank.answer: Cache user preferences in Redis with 5-minute TTL
quizBank.explanation: 1,000 reads/sec for user preferences: DB can handle it but cache is much faster (0.1ms vs 5ms). Cache: Redis Hash per user_id storing preference flags. TTL: 5 minutes (acceptable staleness for preference changes). Cache miss: read from DB, populate cache. Result: 95%+ cache hit rate, DB sees only ~50 reads/sec instead of 1,000.

quizBank.question: How do you handle email bounces and complaints to protect your email sending reputation?
quizBank.answer: SES/SendGrid provide bounce/complaint webhooks. Bounce handling: (1) Hard bounce (invalid email): immediately mark email as invalid in user table, never send again. (2) Soft bounce (mailbox full): retry up to 5x with 24-hour intervals. Complaint handling: user marks email as spam → immediate unsubscribe, never send marketing to this user again. Monitoring: bounce rate <2%, complaint rate <0.1% (Gmail threshold). Above threshold: ESP may block your account. Warm-up: new sending domains/IPs send gradually increasing volume. List hygiene: remove inactive emails periodically.

quizBank.question: Design the notification system's template versioning — how do you update a template without breaking in-flight notifications?
quizBank.answer: Template versioning: each template has version number. Notification payload stores template_id + template_version. Two strategies: (1) Snapshot at enqueue: render template at queue time, store rendered content in notification record. Guarantees template change doesn't affect in-flight messages. Uses more storage. (2) Lazy render: store template_id + version + variables. Render at delivery time. Template update: create new version, don't delete old version (in-flight messages reference old version). Deprecate old version after in-flight messages clear (queue drain time). Blue/green template deployment: test new version with small percentage, then roll out.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a tracking pixel in email notifications?
options: A 1x1 transparent image that triggers an HTTP request when email is opened, indicating delivery and open
quizBank.answer: A 1x1 transparent image that triggers an HTTP request when email is opened, indicating delivery and open
quizBank.explanation: Tracking pixel: tiny image in email HTML. When user opens email, browser loads image from your server — you log the request as 'opened'. Update notification_log: opened_at = now(). Privacy concern: Apple Mail Privacy Protection (iOS 15+) pre-fetches images, causing false opens. Complement with click tracking for more accurate engagement metrics.

quizBank.question: Your notification system needs to support A/B testing different push notification copy. How do you implement this?
quizBank.answer: Experiment infrastructure: (1) Experiment config: {experiment_id, template_a, template_b, traffic_split: 0.5, metric: open_rate}. (2) User bucketing: hash(user_id + experiment_id) % 100 < 50 → variant A, else B. Deterministic bucketing — same user always gets same variant. (3) Template rendering: select variant template based on bucket. (4) Delivery logging: include experiment_id, variant in notification_log. (5) Metrics: track open rate per variant (notification_log WHERE experiment_id=X, variant=A). (6) Statistical significance: calculate before declaring winner. (7) Rollout: winning variant becomes default template. (8) Guard rail: if either variant shows negative metric (unsubscribe spike), abort experiment.

quizBank.question: What is 'send time optimization' in notification systems?
options: Optimizing server send queue processing speed
ML-based prediction of when each user is most likely to open a notification
Sending during off-peak hours to reduce server load
Compressing notification payload for faster transmission
quizBank.answer: ML-based prediction of when each user is most likely to open a notification
quizBank.explanation: Send time optimization (STO): ML model trained on user engagement history predicts optimal send time per user. Instead of sending at 9am to everyone, send to each user when they historically engage most (e.g., user A engages at 7am, user B at 9pm). Improves open rates by 20-30%. Features: historical open times, day of week, user activity patterns.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a scenario where FCM rate limits your push notifications?
quizBank.answer: FCM rate limit response: HTTP 429 with Retry-After header. Handling: (1) Back off for duration specified in Retry-After. (2) Circuit breaker: if FCM returns >10% 429s, slow down sending rate. (3) Queue accumulation: notifications pile up in Kafka during rate limit period. (4) Priority preservation: process critical notifications first when rate limit lifts. (5) Monitoring: alert when FCM 429 rate spikes. (6) Provider rotation: route some traffic to alternative push providers (e.g., AWS SNS → FCM). (7) Batch optimization: maximize batch size (500 per FCM request) to reduce request count. (8) Negotiate higher rate limits with FCM for high-volume senders.

quizBank.question: How would you design notification preferences for a complex hierarchy: global mute → category mute → channel mute?
quizBank.answer: Hierarchical preference model: (1) Global opt-out: user disables all notifications. One boolean field in user table. Check first. (2) Category level: marketing, transactional, social, security. User can disable per category. Stored in user_notification_prefs. (3) Channel level: push, email, SMS per category. Matrix: (user, category, channel) → enabled/disabled. (4) Emergency override: critical/security notifications bypass all user preferences (OTP, fraud alerts). Make this explicit and documented in UX. (5) Evaluation: check global → category → channel → priority override. If any gate fails (except override), stop. (6) UI: clear settings screen showing full hierarchy. One-click global mute. (7) Audit log: track preference changes for GDPR compliance.

quizBank.question: What is the recommended way to handle a user who uninstalls your app but still has their device token in your database?
options: Delete immediately on app uninstall event
FCM/APNS will return an invalid token error — remove token on first delivery failure
quizBank.answer: FCM/APNS will return an invalid token error — remove token on first delivery failure
quizBank.explanation: Apps cannot signal token invalidation on uninstall (no network call made). Discovery is passive: send to token → FCM returns 'NotRegistered' → mark token inactive in DB → stop sending to it. Alternative: FCM token refresh events (app must be running). Active cleanup: tokens not seen in 90 days flagged for removal.

quizBank.question: How do you guarantee an OTP (one-time password) SMS is delivered within 30 seconds?
quizBank.answer: OTP delivery SLA = 30 seconds. Multi-layer approach: (1) Priority queue: OTP goes to critical topic, dedicated fast consumers. (2) No user preference check: security notification, bypass quiet hours and rate limits. (3) Fast provider: use Twilio with dedicated shortcode (faster delivery than longcode). (4) Multi-provider: if Twilio fails, immediately retry with backup (AWS SNS, Vonage). No exponential backoff — retry immediately. (5) Geographic routing: use local carrier connections for each country (lower latency). (6) Monitoring: alert if OTP delivery p99 > 20 seconds. (7) Fallback: if SMS fails after 2 attempts → offer voice call OTP (text-to-speech). (8) SLA monitoring: track per-carrier delivery times.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between transactional and marketing notifications? Why does it matter architecturally?
quizBank.answer: Transactional: triggered by user action (order confirmed, password reset, OTP). User expects/needs it. Legally can send without marketing opt-in. High priority, must deliver reliably. Marketing: promotional content, newsletters, offers. Requires explicit consent (GDPR). User can opt out. Lower priority, acceptable to delay or batch. Architectural differences: separate Kafka topics (different consumers, priorities). Separate email sending domains/IPs — transactional IP reputation protected from marketing bounces. Different user preference checks — marketing checks opt-in, transactional only checks hard bounces. Different retry policies — transactional retries aggressively, marketing accepts drops. Different rate limits — transactional: unlimited, marketing: max N/week.

quizBank.question: What is 'notification deduplication window' and why is it important?
options: Time window to collect notifications before batching
Period during which the same notification_id is considered duplicate and rejected
Window during which users can delete notifications
Time period for A/B test measurement
quizBank.answer: Period during which the same notification_id is considered duplicate and rejected
quizBank.explanation: Deduplication window: idempotency check period. If notification_id=X was processed 5 minutes ago and arrives again (retry): check log, find it was already sent, reject duplicate. Window must be long enough to cover retry periods (hours for email). Implementation: Redis cache of recently processed notification_ids with TTL = max retry window. After TTL: assume it's a genuinely new notification (not a retry).

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Notification System
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design an E-Commerce System (Amazon)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Product catalog: browse, search, filter products
- Shopping cart: add/remove/update items
- Checkout: address, payment, order confirmation
- Payment processing: credit card, wallet, COD
- Inventory management: stock levels, reservations
- Order tracking: placed, confirmed, shipped, delivered
- Reviews and ratings
- Seller portal: list products, manage inventory

### Non-Functional Requirements
- High availability: 99.99% (especially checkout)
- Low latency: product search <200ms, checkout <2s
- Consistency: inventory must not oversell (strong consistency for stock deduction)
- Scale: 100M DAU, 10M orders/day, 500M product catalog

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- Product catalog: 500M products x 2KB = 1TB catalog data
- Orders: 10M/day = 116/sec average, 1,000/sec peak (Prime Day: 10,000/sec)
- Search: 100M searches/day = 1,200/sec
- Cart operations: 500M/day = 5,800/sec
- Payment: 10M/day = 116/sec (financial-grade consistency needed)
- Images: avg 10 images/product x 500KB = 500M x 5MB = 2.5PB

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([Client Web/Mobile])
    CDN[CDN - CloudFront]
    AG[API Gateway]
    PS[Product Service]
    SS[Search Service - Elasticsearch]
    CS[Cart Service]
    OS[Order Service]
    PAY[Payment Service]
    INV[Inventory Service]
    NS[Notification Service]
    Kafka[Event Bus - Kafka]
    PDB[(Product DB - DynamoDB)]
    CDB[(Cart DB - Redis)]
    ODB[(Order DB - MySQL Sharded)]
    IDB[(Inventory DB - MySQL)]
    S3[S3 - Product Images]

    Client --> CDN
    CDN --> AG
    AG --> PS
    AG --> SS
    AG --> CS
    AG --> OS
    AG --> PAY
    PS --> PDB
    PS --> S3
    SS --> PDB
    CS --> CDB
    OS --> ODB
    OS --> Kafka
    PAY --> Kafka
    INV --> IDB
    Kafka --> INV
    Kafka --> NS
    Kafka --> SS
```

## 4. Product Catalog

### Data Model (DynamoDB)
```
Product Table:
PK: product_id
SK: METADATA
Attributes: title, description, brand, category_path, seller_id,
            price, discount_price, rating_avg, review_count,
            is_active, created_at

Product-Category GSI:
PK: category_id, SK: rating_avg (for sorting by rating)

Product Images:
PK: product_id, SK: image_sequence
Attributes: s3_url, is_primary, width, height
```

### Category Hierarchy
- Materialized path: Electronics > Computers > Laptops → stored as string for efficient prefix queries
- Breadcrumb navigation
- Category-level inventory and search facets

## 5. Search Service

### Elasticsearch Architecture
- Index: products
- Indexed fields: title, description, brand, category, tags
- Numeric fields for filtering: price, rating, stock_available
- Geo for location-based (local delivery)

```
Search Query:
GET /products/_search
{
  "query": {
    "bool": {
      "must": { "multi_match": { "query": "laptop", "fields": ["title^3", "description", "brand^2"] } },
      "filter": [
        { "range": { "price": { "gte": 500, "lte": 2000 } } },
        { "term": { "is_active": true } },
        { "range": { "stock_available": { "gt": 0 } } }
      ]
    }
  },
  "sort": [{ "_score": "desc" }, { "rating_avg": "desc" }],
  "aggs": {
    "brands": { "terms": { "field": "brand.keyword" } },
    "price_ranges": { "range": { "field": "price", "ranges": [...] } }
  }
}
```

## 6. Shopping Cart

### Redis-Based Cart
```
Key: cart:{user_id}
Type: Hash
Fields: {product_id} → {quantity, price_at_add, seller_id}
TTL: 30 days (abandoned cart expiry)

Operations:
HSET cart:123 product_456 '{"qty": 2, "price": 29.99}'
HGETALL cart:123  → all items
HDEL cart:123 product_456  → remove item
DEL cart:123  → clear cart on checkout
```

### Cart Price Validation
- Price stored at add time (snapshot)
- At checkout: revalidate against current product price
- If price changed: notify user, require confirmation
- If product unavailable: remove from cart, notify

## 7. Order Processing & Inventory

### The Oversell Problem
- 100 units in stock. 1000 concurrent checkout attempts.
- Without locking: all see stock=100, all proceed, 1000 orders confirmed. 900 oversold.

### Solution: Optimistic Locking + Reserve
```sql
-- Inventory reservation (atomic)
UPDATE inventory
SET reserved = reserved + ?, available = available - ?
WHERE product_id = ? AND available >= ?

-- If rows_affected = 0: insufficient stock → return error
-- Reservation held for 15 minutes (TTL)
-- On payment success: COMMIT (reserved→sold)
-- On payment failure/timeout: ROLLBACK (reserved→available)
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant OS as Order Service
    participant INV as Inventory Service
    participant PAY as Payment Service
    participant Kafka
    
    U->>OS: POST /checkout
    OS->>INV: Reserve items (atomic UPDATE)
    INV-->>OS: Reserved (reservation_id, TTL: 15min)
    OS->>OS: Create order (status: PENDING_PAYMENT)
    OS-->>U: Order created, redirect to payment
    U->>PAY: Submit payment
    PAY->>PAY: Process with payment gateway
    PAY-->>OS: Payment success
    OS->>INV: Confirm reservation → sold
    OS->>OS: Update order status: CONFIRMED
    OS->>Kafka: order_confirmed event
    Kafka->>NS: Send confirmation email
```

## 8. Payment Processing

### Idempotency
- Generate idempotency key: UUID before payment call
- Send to payment gateway with key
- On retry (network timeout): gateway returns same result, no double charge

### Payment States
```
PENDING → PROCESSING → SUCCESS
                     → FAILED
                     → REFUNDED
```

### PCI DSS Compliance
- Never store full card numbers in your DB
- Tokenize: Stripe/Braintree stores card, returns token
- Your DB stores token, not card
- TLS everywhere, field-level encryption for PII

## 9. API Design

```
Product Catalog:
GET /api/v1/products/{product_id}     — product details
GET /api/v1/search?q=laptop&filters=  — search
GET /api/v1/categories/{id}/products  — browse by category

Cart:
GET  /api/v1/cart                     — get cart
POST /api/v1/cart/items               — add item {product_id, qty}
PUT  /api/v1/cart/items/{product_id}  — update qty
DEL  /api/v1/cart/items/{product_id}  — remove item

Orders:
POST /api/v1/checkout                 — initiate checkout
GET  /api/v1/orders                   — order history
GET  /api/v1/orders/{order_id}        — order status
POST /api/v1/orders/{order_id}/cancel — cancel order

Payment:
POST /api/v1/payments                 — initiate payment
POST /api/v1/refunds                  — request refund
```

## 10. Data Models

### Orders Table (MySQL Sharded by user_id)
```sql
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id      VARCHAR(36) PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id       BIGINT NOT NULL,
    status        ENUM('pending_payment','confirmed','shipped','delivered','cancelled','refunded'),
    total_amount  DECIMAL(10,2),
    currency      VARCHAR(3) DEFAULT 'USD',
    shipping_addr JSON,
    payment_id    VARCHAR(36),
    created_at    TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at    TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE order_items (
    order_id    VARCHAR(36),
    product_id  BIGINT,
    seller_id   BIGINT,
    qty         INT,
    unit_price  DECIMAL(10,2),
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id)
);
```

## 11. Scalability & Bottlenecks

- **Product catalog reads**: DynamoDB + ElastiCache caching → millions reads/sec
- **Search**: Elasticsearch cluster, 3 shards x 2 replicas
- **Cart**: Redis Cluster, session-based, low latency
- **Inventory**: MySQL with row-level locking for atomicity; consider RDBMS for ACID guarantees
- **Order DB**: shard by user_id; order_id lookup via DynamoDB routing table
- **Prime Day scaling**: pre-scale inventory service, enable queue-based checkout (reserve slot in queue)
- **Images**: S3 + CloudFront CDN, immutable URLs with long TTL

## 12. Monitoring
- Checkout funnel: cart→checkout→payment→confirmed conversion rate
- Inventory reservation failure rate (stock-out indicator)
- Payment success rate per gateway
- Order processing latency p99
- Search latency and relevance (CTR on top results)
- Cart abandonment rate

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows e-commerce has products, cart, checkout. Can describe basic order flow.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Designs individual services. Knows SQL schema for orders. Understands basic inventory tracking.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Handles inventory consistency (no oversell). Designs search with Elasticsearch. Implements idempotent payments.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Designs flash sale handling (high concurrency checkout), Kafka event-driven pipeline, PCI compliance, sharded order DB.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs multi-region e-commerce, global inventory visibility, ML-based fraud detection, seller performance analytics, A/B testing pricing.

quizBank.question: Why is Redis preferred for shopping cart storage over a relational database?
options: Redis provides sub-millisecond latency and flexible data structures for cart operations
quizBank.answer: Redis provides sub-millisecond latency and flexible data structures for cart operations
quizBank.explanation: Cart operations need <5ms latency. Redis Hash stores cart as {product_id: quantity} with O(1) operations. Cart doesn't need ACID transactions (relaxed consistency acceptable). Redis handles 5,800 cart ops/sec easily. TTL handles cart expiration automatically.

quizBank.question: A product has 10 units in stock. 100 users all click 'Buy' simultaneously. How do you prevent overselling?
quizBank.answer: Atomic reservation using SQL optimistic locking: UPDATE inventory SET available = available - 1, reserved = reserved + 1 WHERE product_id = X AND available >= 1. Returns rows_affected: 1 if success, 0 if stock depleted. Each of the 100 users tries this UPDATE. MySQL row-level locking ensures only 10 succeed (atomically). The other 90 get rows_affected=0 and receive 'Out of stock' error. Reservation held with TTL (15 min); if user doesn't pay, TTL expiry job releases reservation back to available.

quizBank.question: What is an idempotency key in payment processing?
options: A key that encrypts payment data
A unique identifier sent with payment request so retries don't cause duplicate charges
A key that identifies the payment provider
A key for PCI DSS compliance
quizBank.answer: A unique identifier sent with payment request so retries don't cause duplicate charges
quizBank.explanation: Client generates UUID before payment call. Sends to Stripe with Idempotency-Key header. If network timeout: client retries with same key. Stripe returns same response (no duplicate charge). Idempotency key stored for 24 hours by Stripe. This makes payment safe to retry.

quizBank.question: Why should raw credit card numbers never be stored in your e-commerce database?
options: PCI DSS compliance requires tokenization; storing raw cards is a legal violation and security risk
Credit cards are too large for database storage
quizBank.answer: PCI DSS compliance requires tokenization; storing raw cards is a legal violation and security risk
quizBank.explanation: PCI DSS: storing PANs (Primary Account Numbers) requires encryption, restricted access, audit trails, quarterly scans. Any breach = massive fines + lawsuits. Tokenization: Stripe stores card, gives you a token. Your DB stores token. Breach of your DB: attacker gets useless tokens, not card numbers.

quizBank.question: During Amazon Prime Day, your checkout service gets 10,000 orders/sec (normally 100/sec). How do you handle this?
quizBank.answer: Pre-scaling: auto-scale checkout, inventory, and payment services before Prime Day. Cache warming: pre-load inventory counts into Redis. Queue-based checkout: accept checkout request → queue in Kafka → process at sustainable rate (prevents inventory service overload). Virtual waiting room: show users queue position, process in order. Redis DECR for inventory (atomic, fast, handles 100K ops/sec). Pre-generate order IDs (Snowflake). DB write buffering: batch-write orders to DB. Separate flash sale products: dedicated inventory service instances for high-demand SKUs. Circuit breakers: shed non-critical load (recommendations, reviews) during peak.

quizBank.question: How do you handle the case where a user's payment succeeds but the order confirmation email fails to send?
quizBank.answer: Event-driven architecture: payment success triggers a Kafka event. Order service consumes event → updates order status → publishes order_confirmed event. Notification service consumes order_confirmed → sends email. If email fails: notification service retries with backoff (not the entire order). Order is still confirmed in DB regardless of email. User can always check order status in app. Idempotency: if notification service crashes and replays Kafka event, email service checks if email was already sent (log in notification_log) before sending again. The saga pattern ensures compensation logic at each step.

quizBank.question: What data structure is best for Elasticsearch product faceted search (filters by brand, price range, category)?
options: Aggregations on keyword and numeric fields
quizBank.answer: Aggregations on keyword and numeric fields
quizBank.explanation: Elasticsearch aggregations: 'terms' aggregation on brand.keyword returns top brands and their counts. 'range' aggregation on price field returns count per price bracket. These aggregations run in parallel with the search query, returning facet counts for UI filter display.

quizBank.question: How do you keep Elasticsearch product index in sync with the DynamoDB product catalog?
quizBank.answer: Event-driven sync: product update in DynamoDB → DynamoDB Streams → Lambda → Elasticsearch index update. Near real-time: 1-5 second lag. For bulk updates (price change across 1M products): batch processor reads DynamoDB, bulk-indexes to Elasticsearch. Consistency: Elasticsearch is always eventually consistent with DynamoDB — primary source of truth is DynamoDB. If Elasticsearch goes down: search falls back to DynamoDB scan (slow, emergency use only). Re-indexing: periodic full re-sync for data consistency (weekly). Delta sync: track last_updated timestamp, sync only changed records.

quizBank.question: How would you design the order state machine for an e-commerce system?
quizBank.answer: States and transitions: CART → PENDING_PAYMENT (on checkout initiation) → PAYMENT_PROCESSING → CONFIRMED (payment success) → WAREHOUSE_PROCESSING → SHIPPED (tracking number assigned) → OUT_FOR_DELIVERY → DELIVERED. Failure paths: PENDING_PAYMENT → PAYMENT_FAILED (user retry or abandon). CONFIRMED → CANCELLED (user cancels before ship). SHIPPED → RETURN_REQUESTED → RETURNED → REFUNDED. Implementation: order status stored in DB. Kafka events drive transitions: payment_success event → CONFIRMED. Each transition: validate current state (can't go SHIPPED → PENDING). Compensation: PAYMENT_FAILED → release inventory reservation. State stored in orders table, history in order_events table.

quizBank.question: How do you shard the orders database to support efficient queries by both user_id and order_id?
options: Shard by user_id; maintain a lookup table order_id → shard for direct order access
Don't shard — use read replicas only
quizBank.answer: Shard by user_id; maintain a lookup table order_id → shard for direct order access
quizBank.explanation: Shard by user_id: all user's orders on same shard (efficient order history queries). Problem: given order_id, which shard? Solution: order routing table in DynamoDB: {order_id → user_id or shard_id}. Order ID encodes shard info (use Snowflake with shard_id bits). Customer service can look up any order via routing table.

quizBank.question: How do you handle cart merge when a guest user logs in to an existing account?
quizBank.answer: Guest cart: stored in Redis keyed by session_id. On login: fetch guest cart (session_id) AND user cart (user_id). Merge strategy: (1) Add guest cart items to user cart. (2) For conflicts (same product in both): take higher quantity, or prompt user. (3) Delete guest cart after merge. (4) If user cart is empty: move guest cart to user cart (change key). Implementation: WATCH session_cart user_cart, MULTI, merge logic, EXEC (Redis transaction). Edge case: guest added item, same item sold out by login time — validate all items at merge.

quizBank.question: How would you design a dynamic pricing system like Amazon's, where prices change multiple times per day?
quizBank.answer: Price calculation engine: factors = base_cost + margin + competitor_prices + demand_signal + inventory_level + time_of_day. ML model trained on historical demand vs price. Real-time signals: competitor price scraping (web crawler), current inventory level (low stock → price up), demand surge detection. Architecture: pricing service consumes product events, calculates optimal price, publishes price_updated event. Product DB updated. Elasticsearch updated via DynamoDB Streams. Cache invalidated. Customer display: current_price vs original_price for discount display. Audit trail: price_history table for all price changes. Legal: price history displayed to show 'was $X, now $Y' — many jurisdictions require truth in pricing.

quizBank.question: What is the 'saga pattern' and when is it used in e-commerce?
options: A design pattern for product recommendations
A distributed transaction pattern where each service has a compensating action for failures
A caching pattern for product catalogs
quizBank.answer: A distributed transaction pattern where each service has a compensating action for failures
quizBank.explanation: Checkout saga: reserve inventory → charge payment → create order. If payment fails: compensating action releases inventory reservation. If order creation fails: compensating action refunds payment AND releases inventory. Sagas replace ACID distributed transactions (which don't exist across microservices) with sequential steps + compensations. Choreography (Kafka events) or orchestration (Order Service as coordinator).

quizBank.question: A seller fraudulently lists 1,000,000 units of an iPhone at $1. How do you detect and prevent this?
quizBank.answer: Multi-layer fraud detection: (1) Price validation: price must be within reasonable range of market price. ML model trained on category + price distributions — flags statistical outliers. (2) Seller verification: new sellers have strict limits until trust established. (3) Inventory validation: sellers must prove inventory (purchase records for electronics). (4) Human review: items flagged by ML routed to human reviewer before going live. (5) Rate limiting: sellers cannot list too many items too quickly. (6) Soft launch: high-risk listings appear in catalog but with limited visibility until reviewed. (7) Real-time monitoring: spike in orders for single product at abnormally low price → auto-pause listing. (8) Buyer protection: max purchase quantity per user per day.

quizBank.question: How do you implement product recommendations ('Customers who bought X also bought Y')?
quizBank.answer: Collaborative filtering: build item-item co-occurrence matrix from order history. If A and B frequently appear in same order, they're related. Batch job (Spark): process order history, compute co-occurrence scores, store top-N related items per product. Real-time: serve recommendations from Redis: ZRANGE related:{product_id} 0 9. ML enhancement: matrix factorization (ALS algorithm) for latent factor model. Session-based: track current session view/cart history, find products frequently bought with session items. Personalization: weight recommendations by user's purchase history and browsing. Cold start: new products: use category-level recommendations. Infrastructure: Spark batch job nightly, results cached in Redis, served by recommendation API.

quizBank.question: What approach prevents a user from seeing stale inventory counts in the product listing?
options: Always query live inventory DB for every page load
Cache inventory with short TTL (30 seconds); show 'In Stock' vs 'Out of Stock' not exact count
Refresh inventory count only on checkout
quizBank.answer: Cache inventory with short TTL (30 seconds); show 'In Stock' vs 'Out of Stock' not exact count
quizBank.explanation: Showing exact count (e.g., '7 left') requires live DB query — too expensive at scale. Show only binary status (In Stock / Low Stock / Out of Stock) with 30-60 second cache TTL. Exact validation happens at checkout (reservation attempt). Trade-off: user may see 'In Stock' but fail at checkout — acceptable with good error messaging.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a product return and refund flow?
quizBank.answer: Return request: user initiates in app (within return window). Order service validates: return window not expired, not already returned. Return label generated (carrier API). Item returned to warehouse. Warehouse confirms receipt → triggers refund. Refund: payment service calls Stripe refund API with charge_id. Stripe credits card (5-10 business days). Inventory: on return, inspect → if sellable: increment available inventory. If damaged: write off. State transitions: DELIVERED → RETURN_REQUESTED → RETURN_LABEL_ISSUED → ITEM_RECEIVED → REFUND_INITIATED → REFUNDED. Notifications at each state. Fraud check: excessive return rate flagged for review.

quizBank.question: How would you design a seller portal for inventory management at scale (millions of sellers)?
quizBank.answer: Seller isolation: each seller's inventory is isolated (no global lock). Seller onboarding: verify identity, bank account, tax information. Product listing: seller submits product → moderation queue → published to catalog. Inventory updates: seller uploads CSV or uses API to update stock levels. Bulk updates: process asynchronously (large sellers have millions of SKUs). Inventory sync: if seller uses external WMS (warehouse management system), webhook-based sync. Fulfillment options: FBA (Amazon fulfills) vs FBM (seller fulfills). FBA inventory: tracked by Amazon's fulfillment centers (different DB, real-time synced). Seller analytics: sales, returns, inventory turnover. Rate limiting: API limits per seller. Multi-tenant: each seller sees only their products/orders (row-level security).

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of storing the price in the cart (price snapshot) at add time?
options: To show users the price at time of adding, and detect price changes before checkout
quizBank.answer: To show users the price at time of adding, and detect price changes before checkout
quizBank.explanation: Price snapshot: when item added to cart, current price captured. At checkout: compare snapshot_price to current_price. If increased: notify user, require confirmation. If decreased: show discount (good UX). This also handles flash sales: item added at sale price, sale ends before checkout — system must decide whether to honor sale price (UX choice).

quizBank.question: How do you design a global e-commerce system where inventory is shared between multiple geographic fulfillment centers?
quizBank.answer: Multi-region inventory: each fulfillment center has its own inventory DB (local reads/writes). Global inventory service aggregates across centers. At checkout: route to nearest fulfillment center with sufficient inventory (minimize shipping time). Reservation: reserve from specific center's inventory. If center out of stock: route to next nearest with stock. Global view: global inventory API aggregates center inventories — eventually consistent (minutes lag acceptable for catalog display). Transfers between centers: batch jobs move inventory based on demand predictions. Challenges: split shipments (order fulfilled from 2 centers), international shipping restrictions, customs. Analytics: real-time inventory position by center for replenishment planning.

quizBank.question: How do you calculate and display shipping estimates accurately?
quizBank.answer: Shipping estimate calculation: inputs = origin (fulfillment center), destination (user address), carrier, weight/dimensions, service level. Carrier API: real-time call to FedEx/UPS API for estimated days. Cached: cache carrier estimates by origin-destination zone + weight class (cache for 1 hour). Display: 'Arrives by Friday' = order by 3pm today + carrier transit time. Cut-off time: orders placed after cut-off ship next business day. Complexity: weekends, holidays, carrier zone maps, remote areas. Local delivery: same-day or next-day using local delivery network (2-hour promise for Prime). Estimate accuracy: ML model trained on historical actual delivery vs estimated — corrects carrier estimate bias per route.

quizBank.question: What happens if the inventory reservation succeeds but the database write for the order record fails?
options: Saga compensating transaction releases the reservation; user sees failure and can retry
quizBank.answer: Saga compensating transaction releases the reservation; user sees failure and can retry
quizBank.explanation: Saga pattern: each step has a compensating action. Order write failure → compensating action: release inventory reservation (UPDATE inventory SET available = available + qty, reserved = reserved - qty WHERE reservation_id = X). User sees checkout error, can retry. Reservation TTL also handles stuck reservations. This avoids distributed transactions while maintaining data consistency.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: E-Commerce System (Amazon)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design a Video Streaming Platform (YouTube/Netflix)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Video upload: accept large video files (up to 10GB)
- Transcoding: convert to multiple resolutions and formats
- Adaptive bitrate streaming: switch quality based on bandwidth
- CDN delivery: low-latency video playback globally
- Search and discovery: find videos by title, tags, creator
- Recommendation engine: personalized video feed
- Watch history: resume from where you left off
- Comments, likes, subscriptions
- Live streaming (out of scope for this session)

### Non-Functional Requirements
- High availability: 99.99% for playback (users can't watch = bad)
- Low latency: video starts in <2 seconds
- Scale: 500M DAU, 500 hours of video uploaded every minute, 1B hours watched daily
- Durability: videos stored permanently (3-9s durability)

## 2. Capacity Estimation

### Upload
- 500 hours/minute uploaded = 8.33 hours/sec
- Average video: 5 minutes = 300 seconds
- 8.33 hours/sec = 8.33 x 3600 / 300 = 100 videos/sec uploaded
- Raw video: 100 videos/sec x 1GB average = 100GB/sec upload throughput
- After transcoding: 5 quality levels x 100 videos/sec x 500MB = 250GB/sec storage writes
- Total storage: 500 hours/min x 60 = 30,000 hours/day x 3600s x 2Mbps compressed = ~21.6PB/day (extreme estimate)
- Realistic: 500 hours/min x 300MB/hour (compressed) = 150GB/min = 9TB/hour

### Watch
- 1B hours watched daily = ~694,000 hours/sec
- At 5Mbps average bitrate: 694,000 x 5Mbps = 3.47Tbps bandwidth
- CDN must handle 3.5Tbps sustained egress

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Creator([Creator Client])
    Viewer([Viewer Client])
    LB[Load Balancer]
    US[Upload Service]
    TS[Transcoding Service Workers]
    MS[Metadata Service]
    SS[Search Service]
    RS[Recommendation Service]
    OS[Object Store - S3/GCS]
    MDB[(Metadata DB - Cassandra)]
    SDB[(Search Index - Elasticsearch)]
    CDN[CDN - CloudFront/Akamai]
    MQ[Message Queue - Kafka]
    Cache[Redis Cache]

    Creator -->|Upload video| LB
    LB --> US
    US -->|Raw video| OS
    US -->|transcode_job| MQ
    MQ --> TS
    TS -->|Transcoded segments| OS
    TS -->|Job complete| MS
    MS --> MDB
    MS --> SDB
    Viewer -->|Get video URL| LB
    LB --> MS
    MS --> Cache
    Cache -->|Signed URL| Viewer
    Viewer -->|Stream video| CDN
    CDN -->|Cache miss| OS
```

## 4. Video Upload Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Creator
    participant US as Upload Service
    participant S3 as Object Store
    participant Kafka
    participant TS as Transcoding Workers
    participant CDN

    C->>US: POST /videos/initiate-upload
    US-->>C: presigned_upload_url, video_id, upload_id
    C->>S3: PUT (multipart upload, 10MB chunks)
    S3-->>C: Upload progress per chunk
    C->>US: POST /videos/{video_id}/complete
    US->>Kafka: Publish transcode_job {video_id, s3_path, formats}
    US-->>C: 202 Accepted (processing)
    Kafka->>TS: Consume transcode_job
    TS->>S3: Read raw video
    TS->>TS: Transcode to 1080p, 720p, 480p, 360p
    TS->>TS: Generate thumbnails
    TS->>S3: Write transcoded segments (HLS chunks)
    TS->>US: Job complete webhook
    US->>CDN: Warm cache for new video
    US-->>C: Video published notification
```

### Multipart Upload
- Split large files into 10MB chunks
- Upload chunks in parallel (faster, resumable)
- S3 multipart upload: initiate → upload parts → complete
- If upload interrupted: resume from last successful chunk
- Server generates presigned URLs — client uploads directly to S3

## 5. Transcoding Service

### Why Transcoding?
- Input: many formats (MP4, MOV, AVI, MKV, 4K ProRes)
- Output: standardized formats for all devices
- Multiple resolutions for adaptive streaming
- Reduce file size: compress with H.264/H.265/AV1

### Output Formats
```
HLS (HTTP Live Streaming):
- Splits video into 2-10 second segments (.ts or .fmp4 files)
- Master playlist (.m3u8) lists all quality variants
- Each quality level has its own segment playlist
- Client downloads segments sequentially, switches quality as needed

Resolutions produced:
- 1080p (1920x1080) @ 5Mbps
- 720p  (1280x720)  @ 2.5Mbps
- 480p  (854x480)   @ 1Mbps
- 360p  (640x360)   @ 0.5Mbps
- 240p  (426x240)   @ 0.25Mbps (low bandwidth fallback)
```

### Distributed Transcoding
- FFmpeg workers on EC2/spot instances
- Video split into 1-minute segments for parallel transcoding
- Each segment transcoded independently → merged after
- Worker pool: auto-scale based on Kafka queue depth
- Priority: longer videos get lower priority (fairness)

## 6. Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (ABR)

```
Player algorithm:
1. Start at medium quality (480p)
2. Measure: available bandwidth = downloaded_bytes / time
3. Predict: next_bitrate = f(available_bandwidth, buffer_level)
4. If buffer < 5 seconds: step down quality immediately
5. If buffer > 20 seconds AND bandwidth supports: step up quality
6. Pre-fetch: download next N segments at chosen quality

HLS segment structure:
masters.m3u8
  → 1080p/playlist.m3u8 → seg_0.ts, seg_1.ts, ...
  → 720p/playlist.m3u8  → seg_0.ts, seg_1.ts, ...
  → 480p/playlist.m3u8  → seg_0.ts, seg_1.ts, ...
```

## 7. CDN Strategy

- Videos stored in origin (S3)
- CDN edge nodes: Akamai, CloudFront (200+ POPs worldwide)
- Long TTL: videos are immutable (segments don't change), TTL = 1 year
- Popular videos: cached at all edge nodes (proactive push)
- Cold videos: cached at region level or fetched from origin on demand
- URL signing: signed URLs with expiry prevent unauthorized access

## 8. Metadata Service & Data Model

### Videos Table (Cassandra)
```sql
CREATE TABLE videos (
    video_id       UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    creator_id     BIGINT,
    title          TEXT,
    description    TEXT,
    status         TEXT,  -- uploading, processing, published, deleted
    duration_secs  INT,
    view_count     BIGINT,
    like_count     BIGINT,
    thumbnail_url  TEXT,
    hls_url        TEXT,   -- master playlist URL
    tags           LIST<TEXT>,
    created_at     TIMESTAMP,
    published_at   TIMESTAMP
);
```

### Watch History (Cassandra)
```sql
CREATE TABLE watch_history (
    user_id        BIGINT,
    video_id       UUID,
    watched_at     TIMESTAMP,
    watch_duration INT,   -- seconds watched
    completed      BOOLEAN,
    resume_position INT,  -- seconds from start
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, watched_at)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (watched_at DESC);
```

## 9. API Design

```
POST /api/v1/videos/upload/initiate     — start upload session
PUT  /api/v1/videos/{id}/upload         — complete upload
GET  /api/v1/videos/{id}               — video metadata + stream URL
GET  /api/v1/videos/{id}/stream        — redirect to CDN HLS URL
POST /api/v1/videos/{id}/view          — log view event
POST /api/v1/videos/{id}/like          — like/unlike

GET  /api/v1/search?q={query}&filters= — search videos
GET  /api/v1/recommendations           — personalized feed
GET  /api/v1/users/{id}/history        — watch history
PUT  /api/v1/users/{id}/history/{vid}  — update resume position

GET  /api/v1/creators/{id}/videos      — creator's video list
POST /api/v1/subscriptions/{creator_id} — subscribe to creator
```

## 10. Recommendation Engine

### Two-Tower Model
- User tower: encodes user's watch history, likes, subscriptions
- Item tower: encodes video metadata, transcript, engagement signals
- Dot product similarity = recommendation score

### Candidate Generation → Ranking → Filtering
1. **Candidates**: find 500 candidate videos (collaborative filtering + content-based)
2. **Ranking**: ML model scores each candidate (watch probability, session length impact)
3. **Filtering**: remove watched videos, flagged content, creator blocks
4. **Diversity**: ensure mix of topics, languages, formats

## 11. View Counter at Scale

- 1B views/day = ~12,000 view events/sec
- Do NOT increment DB directly (bottleneck)
- Redis INCR: in-memory, 100K ops/sec per node
- Batch write: Redis counter → MySQL every 5 seconds via background job
- Display: read from Redis (real-time) or MySQL (slightly stale OK)
- For YouTube-scale: approximate counts (floor to significant figures)

## 12. Scalability & Monitoring

- Transcoding: biggest cost — use spot instances, parallelize per segment
- CDN: 3.5Tbps egress — negotiate with Akamai/CloudFront for volume pricing
- Search: Elasticsearch cluster scaled for write volume (metadata updates)
- Recommendation: offline training (Spark) + online serving (TensorFlow Serving)
- Monitor: time-to-first-frame, rebuffer rate, quality switch rate, upload success rate
- Alert: rebuffer rate >1%, time-to-first-frame >3s

quizBank.question: Why is video transcoding necessary before streaming?
options: To convert uploaded videos to standardized formats and multiple resolutions for different devices and bandwidths
quizBank.answer: To convert uploaded videos to standardized formats and multiple resolutions for different devices and bandwidths
quizBank.explanation: Users upload videos in many formats (MOV, AVI, MKV, 4K ProRes). Not all devices support all formats. Transcoding to H.264/H.265 MP4 ensures universal compatibility. Multiple resolutions (1080p, 720p, 480p) support adaptive bitrate streaming.

quizBank.question: What is HLS (HTTP Live Streaming)?
options: A streaming protocol that segments video into small chunks and uses a playlist file for adaptive bitrate streaming
quizBank.answer: A streaming protocol that segments video into small chunks and uses a playlist file for adaptive bitrate streaming
quizBank.explanation: HLS: video split into 2-10 second .ts segments. Master playlist (.m3u8) lists all quality levels. Player downloads segments via regular HTTP — works through any CDN/proxy. Client switches quality between segments based on bandwidth measurement.

quizBank.question: Why are CDN cache TTLs set to very long (1 year) for video segments?
options: Video segments are immutable — once created they never change, making long TTL safe and maximizing cache hit rate
quizBank.answer: Video segments are immutable — once created they never change, making long TTL safe and maximizing cache hit rate
quizBank.explanation: Video segments (seg_0001.ts, seg_0002.ts) are content-addressed and immutable. If a video is re-transcoded, new segment files with new names are created. Since segments never change, browsers and CDN nodes can cache them for 1 year safely, maximizing hit rate.

quizBank.question: A creator uploads a 2-hour 4K video. How do you transcode it in under 5 minutes?
quizBank.answer: Parallel transcoding: split 2-hour video into 120 one-minute segments. Process each segment in parallel across worker pool. 120 segments x ~15 seconds transcoding each = 15 seconds wall-clock time with 120 workers (ignoring overhead). Worker pool: EC2 spot instances, auto-scaled based on Kafka queue depth. FFmpeg for transcoding. Merge segments after all are done using MPEG-TS concatenation. Distribute across multiple resolutions simultaneously: each segment transcoded to all 5 resolutions in parallel. Total: approximately 3-5 minutes for a 2-hour 4K video with sufficient parallelism.

quizBank.question: How does adaptive bitrate streaming decide which quality to serve?
quizBank.answer: ABR player algorithm: (1) Start at medium quality (480p). (2) After downloading each segment: measure download_time and segment_size → calculate bandwidth = size/time. (3) Predict: can we download next_quality segments fast enough to maintain buffer? (4) Buffer level triggers: if buffer < 5 seconds AND bandwidth low: step down quality. If buffer > 20 seconds AND bandwidth supports higher: step up. (5) Hysteresis: don't switch quality too frequently (jarring UX). Require sustained bandwidth improvement before stepping up. (6) Goal: maximize quality while keeping rebuffer rate (stall events) near zero. Different algorithms: BOLA (buffer-based), MPC (model predictive control).

quizBank.question: A viral video suddenly gets 10 million concurrent viewers. How does your CDN handle this?
quizBank.answer: CDN design for viral events: (1) First request to edge POP: CDN fetches from origin (S3). Subsequent requests: served from CDN cache. (2) Popular video detection: if view velocity spikes, proactively push segments to all CDN POPs (cache warming). (3) Multiple CDN providers: failover to secondary CDN if primary has capacity issues. (4) Anycast routing: DNS routes each viewer to nearest CDN POP. 10M viewers distributed across 200+ POPs = ~50,000 per POP — manageable. (5) Bandwidth: 10M x 5Mbps (1080p) = 50Tbps — need CDN with sufficient edge capacity. (6) Origin protection: CDN shields origin, only CDN misses hit S3. At steady state, cache hit rate 99%+ for viral videos.

quizBank.question: Why does YouTube use view count approximations (e.g., 1.2M views, not 1,234,567)?
options: Exact counts require synchronous DB writes — approximation allows high-throughput async counting with periodic DB sync
quizBank.answer: Exact counts require synchronous DB writes — approximation allows high-throughput async counting with periodic DB sync
quizBank.explanation: 12,000 view events/sec: synchronous DB write per view would overload MySQL. Solution: Redis INCR (fast, approximate, in-memory) + batch write to DB every 5-10 seconds. Minor discrepancy between Redis and DB acceptable. YouTube showed 301 views for a while on viral videos — this was their batch processing threshold.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'resume from where you left off' for video playback?
quizBank.answer: Track watch position: client sends progress events every 15 seconds during playback: {video_id, user_id, position_seconds}. Server updates Cassandra: INSERT INTO watch_history (user_id, video_id, resume_position, watched_at) VALUES (?,?,?,?) IF NOT EXISTS else UPDATE. On video open: GET /videos/{id} includes resume_position for authenticated user. Client seeks player to resume_position before starting. Edge cases: if user watches 90%+ of video: mark completed=true, don't offer resume (show replay). Multi-device: last_updated wins (simple LWW). If position regresses (user rewound): update only if new position is more recent.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a 'master playlist' in HLS?
options: To list all videos on the platform
To list all available quality renditions with their bandwidth and resolution, so the player can select the appropriate stream
quizBank.answer: To list all available quality renditions with their bandwidth and resolution, so the player can select the appropriate stream
quizBank.explanation: Master playlist (master.m3u8) example: #EXT-X-STREAM-INF:BANDWIDTH=5000000,RESOLUTION=1920x1080 → 1080p/playlist.m3u8. Player reads master playlist, selects best rendition based on device capability and bandwidth. ABR algorithm then switches between renditions using individual quality playlists.

quizBank.question: How do you detect and remove videos that violate copyright (like YouTube's Content ID)?
quizBank.answer: Content ID pipeline: (1) Rights holders submit reference files (their movies, music). (2) Fingerprinting: compute audio and visual fingerprints from reference content (perceptual hashing — robust to compression, format change). (3) On video upload: compute fingerprint of uploaded video. (4) Match against fingerprint database (approximate nearest neighbor search). (5) Action based on rights holder policy: block, monetize (ads go to rights holder), track. (6) Scale: fingerprint generation is CPU-intensive — run as part of transcoding pipeline using GPU workers. (7) Appeal: creators can dispute matches. (8) Live content: real-time fingerprinting during live stream with higher latency tolerance.

quizBank.question: How would you design the recommendation system to avoid the 'rabbit hole' effect (showing increasingly extreme content)?
quizBank.answer: Guard rail metrics: alongside engagement optimization (CTR, watch time), track session-level metrics: topic diversity, sentiment diversity, content category distribution. Diversity injection: for every 10 recommendations, ensure 2-3 are from different topics than recent watch history. Freshness: inject some newer/less-watched content. Hard filters: remove content flagged as potentially harmful (quality signals from human raters + classifier). Policy-based demotion: certain content categories deprioritized even if high engagement. Feedback loops: train model to predict long-term satisfaction (survey: 'are you happy with what you watch?') not just short-term engagement. A/B test: measure session satisfaction surveys across recommendation variants.

quizBank.question: What is 'pre-fetching' in video streaming and why does it matter?
options: Downloading video before user requests it
Player downloading the next several segments in advance to buffer against network jitter
quizBank.answer: Player downloading the next several segments in advance to buffer against network jitter
quizBank.explanation: Pre-fetching: player maintains a buffer of future segments (e.g., 20-30 seconds ahead). Network jitter won't cause stall if buffer exists. Player downloads segments at higher-than-playback speed during good network conditions to fill buffer. Buffer level drives ABR quality decisions.

quizBank.question: How do you handle video thumbnail generation at scale?
quizBank.answer: Thumbnail generation is part of the transcoding pipeline. FFmpeg: extract frames at regular intervals (e.g., every 10 seconds). Select best frame: brightness, sharpness, face detection (avoid blurry or blank frames). Generate multiple sizes: 1280x720, 640x360, 320x180 (for different display contexts). Store in S3, cache via CDN. Auto-generated thumbnail: available within minutes of upload. Custom thumbnail: creator can upload their own image (moderated for policy violations before display). Animated thumbnails: short GIF from video highlights (YouTube feature) — generated lazily when video becomes popular. WebP format for web clients (smaller than JPEG with comparable quality).

quizBank.question: Why is progressive download (HTTP byte-range) inferior to HLS/DASH for streaming?
quizBank.answer: Progressive download: browser downloads MP4 from beginning. Can play as bytes arrive. Problems: (1) Single bitrate — cannot adapt to changing network conditions. Network drops → video stalls. (2) Seeking: jumping to end of video requires downloading all preceding bytes (unless server supports range requests). (3) No quality switching: stuck with one resolution for entire video. HLS/DASH advantages: (1) Adaptive quality: switches resolution per segment based on bandwidth. (2) Efficient seeking: jump directly to segment at target timestamp. (3) CDN-friendly: small segments cached efficiently. (4) Error recovery: if segment fails, retry same or lower quality. HLS/DASH is universally preferred for video streaming.

quizBank.question: A transcoding job for a 3-hour movie takes 45 minutes. How do you parallelize it?
options: Split movie into 1-minute segments, transcode each segment independently in parallel, merge results
quizBank.answer: Split movie into 1-minute segments, transcode each segment independently in parallel, merge results
quizBank.explanation: 180 minutes → 180 one-minute segments. Process all 180 in parallel across worker pool. Theoretical minimum: wall-clock time of transcoding 1 minute of video (≈10-15 seconds). Total: ~15 seconds with 180 workers. Merge: concatenate segments using ffmpeg concat. Practical: 10-20 minutes due to start-up overhead, queue latency, merge time. GPU workers further reduce per-segment time.

quizBank.question: How would you design the analytics pipeline to show creators 'audience retention' — which second of their video viewers drop off?
quizBank.answer: Event collection: client sends playback events every 10 seconds: {video_id, user_id, position_seconds, timestamp}. Also: seek events, skip forward/backward, exit events. Pipeline: events → Kafka → Flink (real-time aggregation) → time-bucketed aggregation (% of viewers still watching at second N). For creator dashboard: aggregate position_seconds into 5-second buckets. COUNT viewers still at each bucket. Normalize: retention_at_second[T] = viewers_at_T / total_viewers. Batch refinement: Spark job nightly aggregates full accuracy numbers. Display: audience retention graph (YouTube Studio format). Spike at certain seconds: indicates rewatched section. Drop at section: indicates boring part or confusing content.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a creator deleting a video that is currently being watched by 100,000 users?
quizBank.answer: Soft delete: mark video as deleted in metadata DB (is_active=false). CDN segments remain cached and playable for in-progress viewers. Players that have already received segment URLs can continue streaming (segments are signed with expiry = now + 1 hour). New requests: metadata service returns 404 for deleted video. CDN cache invalidation for the master playlist (m3u8) — new viewers can't start watching. Cache invalidation propagates within seconds. CDN segments: allowed to expire naturally (don't purge — expensive and disruptive to in-progress viewers). Object store deletion: scheduled 24 hours after soft delete (give time for CDN cache to drain). Legal holds: if video deleted for legal reasons, purge CDN immediately.

quizBank.question: What metric best indicates whether your video streaming quality is good?
options: Rebuffer rate (percentage of sessions with playback stalls)
quizBank.answer: Rebuffer rate (percentage of sessions with playback stalls)
quizBank.explanation: Rebuffer rate: % of playback sessions where video stalled waiting for data. Industry target: <0.5%. Direct measure of user experience quality. Netflix found that each 1% increase in rebuffer rate causes X% increase in abandonment. Other related metrics: time-to-first-frame (<2s target), quality level distribution (% of watch time at each resolution), quality switches per session.

quizBank.question: How would you design automatic subtitle/caption generation for every uploaded video?
quizBank.answer: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) pipeline: (1) Extract audio track from transcoded video. (2) Split audio into 30-second chunks. (3) Send to ASR service (OpenAI Whisper, Google Speech-to-Text). (4) Receive transcript with word-level timestamps. (5) Format as VTT (WebVTT) or SRT subtitle file. (6) Store subtitle file in S3. (7) Update video metadata: subtitle_url, languages available. Display: HLS manifests include subtitle tracks. Parallelism: audio chunks processed in parallel. Language detection: identify spoken language, generate appropriate subtitles. Translation: machine translate to additional languages (YouTube's 100+ language captions). Creator override: creator can upload custom subtitles which replace auto-generated. Quality: auto-captions flagged as 'auto-generated' to set user expectations.

quizBank.question: How would you design a video search system that understands what's in the video (not just title/tags)?
quizBank.answer: Multi-modal indexing: (1) Speech-to-text: transcribe video audio → full text index in Elasticsearch. Search matches transcript content. (2) Visual recognition: extract keyframes, run image classification (scene type, objects, faces) using CV models. Index detected labels. (3) OCR: extract text visible in video frames (slides, signs). (4) Chapter markers: auto-detect topic changes from transcript. Index each chapter separately with timestamps. (5) Semantic search: embed transcript chunks with language model, store embeddings in vector DB (Pinecone/Weaviate). Query embedding compared against chunk embeddings. (6) Results: video cards link directly to timestamp where query term appears in video. Infrastructure: this pipeline runs post-transcoding as async batch jobs on video content.

quizBank.question: What is 'time-to-first-frame' and why is it critical for video platforms?
options: Time for video to fully download
Time from user clicking play to first video frame displaying — directly impacts abandonment rate
Time for CDN to cache a video
quizBank.answer: Time from user clicking play to first video frame displaying — directly impacts abandonment rate
quizBank.explanation: TTFF (Time-To-First-Frame): latency from play button click to video starting. Studies show 10%+ users abandon if video doesn't start within 2 seconds. Reducing TTFF: CDN edge proximity (<10ms latency), low-resolution preview loaded first, pre-fetching next video in feed, fast DNS resolution, HTTP/2 connection reuse.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'chapters' in YouTube videos (click to jump to specific sections)?
quizBank.answer: Creator-defined chapters: creator writes timestamps in description (e.g., '0:00 Intro, 1:30 Setup, 5:00 Demo'). Parser extracts timestamps and titles from description. Store: video_chapters table (video_id, start_seconds, title). Display: chapter markers on video progress bar. Click: player seeks to chapter start_seconds. Auto-generated chapters: ASR transcription → NLP topic segmentation → detect topic boundaries → auto-generate chapter titles. Validation: chapters must be sequential, start from 0:00, last at least 10 seconds. API: GET /videos/{id}/chapters returns chapter list. HLS integration: deep link URLs include timestamp parameter #t=90 for sharing specific moments.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Video Streaming Platform (YouTube/Netflix)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design Search Autocomplete (Google)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- As user types, show top 5-10 suggestions within 100ms
- Suggestions ranked by query frequency
- Support prefix matching (typing 'app' → 'apple', 'application', 'app store')
- Personalization: weight suggestions by user's history
- Multi-language support
- Filter out inappropriate/spam suggestions
- Support for operators: site:, filetype:
- Trending queries get a boost

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Ultra-low latency: <100ms end-to-end (including network)
- High availability: 99.99%
- Scale: Google handles 8.5B searches/day → autocomplete for each keystroke
- Eventually consistent: new trending queries appear in suggestions within minutes

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- Google: 8.5B searches/day = ~100,000 searches/sec
- Average query length: 5 words, typed one character at a time
- Autocomplete triggered: every 2-3 characters = ~3 requests per search
- Total autocomplete QPS: 100,000 x 3 = 300,000 requests/sec
- Cache hit rate for top queries: 99%+ (top 1000 queries = 80% of traffic)
- Trie nodes: 26 characters, branching factor ~4 for common prefixes
- Storage: English has ~1M unique popular queries x 50 bytes = 50MB (fits in RAM)

## 3. Trie Data Structure

### Trie Basics
```
Trie for queries: apple, application, apply, apt

root
└─ a
   └─ p
      ├─ p (apple: freq=8.2B, apply: freq=2.1B, application: freq=5.4B)
      │  ├─ l
      │  │  ├─ e (apple: 8.2B)
      │  │  └─ i
      │  │     └─ c
      │  │        └─ a
      │  │           └─ t
      │  │              └─ i
      │  │                 └─ o
      │  │                    └─ n (application: 5.4B)
      │  └─ y (apply: 2.1B)
      └─ t (apt: 0.3B)

Each node stores:
- character
- is_terminal: bool (marks end of a valid query)
- frequency: int (how often this query is searched)
- top_k: List of top 5 queries under this prefix (cached)
```

### Optimization: Cache Top-K at Each Node
- Without optimization: for prefix 'ap', traverse all children, collect all terminal nodes, sort by frequency → O(N) per request
- With top-K cache: each node stores its top 5 completions, precomputed
- Lookup: O(L) where L = prefix length (just traverse to prefix node)
- Trade-off: trie consumes more memory (top-K per node)

## 4. System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([Browser / Mobile])
    CDN[CDN / Edge Cache]
    AG[API Gateway]
    ACS[Autocomplete Service]
    TCache[Trie Cache - Redis]
    TStore[(Trie Store - MongoDB/Redis)]
    LogProc[Log Aggregator - Kafka]
    FreqCalc[Frequency Calculator - Spark]
    TrieBuilder[Trie Builder]
    QDB[(Query Frequency DB - Cassandra)]
    FS[Filter Service - Blocklist]
    PS[Personalization Service]
    UserHist[(User History DB)]

    Client -->|GET /suggest?q=app| CDN
    CDN -->|Cache miss| AG
    AG --> ACS
    ACS --> TCache
    TCache -->|Miss| TStore
    ACS --> FS
    ACS --> PS
    PS --> UserHist
    Client -->|Search query submitted| LogProc
    LogProc --> FreqCalc
    FreqCalc --> QDB
    QDB --> TrieBuilder
    TrieBuilder --> TStore
```

## 5. Frequency Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant LS as Log Service
    participant K as Kafka
    participant SP as Spark
    participant QDB as Query Frequency DB
    participant TB as Trie Builder
    participant TS as Trie Store
    participant ACS as Autocomplete Service

    U->>LS: Submit search query
    LS->>K: Log query event
    K->>SP: Batch processing (every 5 min)
    SP->>SP: Count query frequencies
    SP->>SP: Apply time decay (older queries weighted less)
    SP->>QDB: Upsert query frequencies
    QDB->>TB: Top queries changed notification
    TB->>TB: Rebuild affected trie nodes
    TB->>TS: Update trie (atomic swap)
    TS->>ACS: New trie available
```

### Time Decay for Trending
```
score = Σ (count_in_window * decay_factor)
window decay factors:
  last 1 hour:    weight 1.0
  last 24 hours:  weight 0.8
  last 7 days:    weight 0.5
  last 30 days:   weight 0.3
  historical:     weight 0.1

This makes trending queries appear even with lower absolute count
(e.g., 'oscars 2024' trends on Oscar night despite lower historical count)
```

## 6. Trie Update Strategy

### Problem: How to update trie without downtime?

**Blue-Green Trie Deployment:**
1. Current trie (blue) serves all traffic
2. New trie (green) built from updated frequencies in background
3. Once green is ready: atomic pointer swap in Redis
4. New requests hit green trie
5. Old blue trie kept for 5 minutes (rollback), then discarded
6. Update frequency: every 5-10 minutes

**Incremental Update (Alternative):**
- Update only changed trie nodes
- Lock node during update (microsecond lock)
- More complex but lower memory

## 7. API Design

```
GET /api/v1/suggest?q={prefix}&lang={lang}&limit={n}&uid={user_id}
Response 200:
{
  "query": "app",
  "suggestions": [
    { "text": "apple", "freq_score": 0.95, "is_trending": false },
    { "text": "apple music", "freq_score": 0.87, "is_trending": false },
    { "text": "application", "freq_score": 0.82, "is_trending": false },
    { "text": "apple store", "freq_score": 0.79, "is_trending": false },
    { "text": "apple iphone", "freq_score": 0.71, "is_trending": false }
  ],
  "response_time_ms": 8
}

POST /api/v1/feedback             — track which suggestion was selected
GET  /api/v1/trending             — currently trending queries
DEL  /admin/v1/queries/{query}    — remove inappropriate suggestion
```

## 8. Data Models

### Query Frequency Table (Cassandra)
```sql
CREATE TABLE query_frequencies (
    query       TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    frequency   BIGINT,
    score       FLOAT,   -- weighted by time decay
    last_seen   TIMESTAMP,
    language    TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE trending_queries (
    language    TEXT,
    query       TEXT,
    rank        INT,
    score       FLOAT,
    updated_at  TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (language, rank)
);
```

### User Query History (for personalization)
```sql
CREATE TABLE user_query_history (
    user_id     BIGINT,
    query       TEXT,
    searched_at TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, searched_at)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (searched_at DESC);
```

## 9. Personalization

### Approach
- Weight suggestions by user's query history
- If user frequently searches for Python tutorials → 'python' prefix → boost 'python tutorial'
- Client sends user_id (authenticated) or anonymous session token
- Personalization service: fetch user's top N recent queries → boost matching suggestions

### Privacy Considerations
- Store minimal query history (90 days max)
- User can delete history
- Use differential privacy for aggregate frequency calculation
- Country/region-specific suggestions (GDPR: no logging in some jurisdictions)

## 10. Multi-Language Support

- Separate trie per language (English, Spanish, Chinese...)
- Language detection: browser Accept-Language header + user preference
- Unicode support: Chinese characters as trie node characters
- IME support: Japanese/Chinese pinyin → kanji suggestions
- RTL support: Arabic, Hebrew (right-to-left display)
- Language routing: request includes lang parameter → route to correct trie

## 11. Caching Strategy

```
Layer 1: Browser cache (JavaScript debounce + local cache)
  - Cache last 100 prefixes in browser memory
  - Debounce: don't send request until user pauses 100ms

Layer 2: CDN cache
  - Popular prefixes cached at CDN edge
  - TTL: 60 seconds (suggestions change frequently)
  - Covers 80% of traffic (top 1000 prefixes)

Layer 3: Redis (trie node cache)
  - Hot trie nodes cached in Redis
  - Full trie fits in RAM: ~50MB for English
  - Load entire trie into memory on startup

Layer 4: Database (cold path)
  - Only for extremely rare prefixes
  - Target: <0.1% of requests reach DB
```

## 12. Scalability & Monitoring

- Trie is read-only from Autocomplete Service perspective → can replicate freely
- Each ACS node loads trie into memory → pure in-memory lookup, no Redis needed
- Horizontal scale: add more ACS nodes, each with full trie replica
- Trie update: broadcast new trie to all ACS nodes every 5 minutes
- Monitor: suggestion latency p99 (target <10ms server-side), CDN hit rate, trie update lag
- Alert: latency >50ms, suggestion error rate >0.01%

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows autocomplete shows suggestions while typing. Can describe prefix matching concept.
skills: Simple SQL LIKE query for suggestions

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Knows Trie data structure. Can implement basic trie search. Understands why SQL LIKE is insufficient at scale.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Designs distributed autocomplete with trie optimization. Implements top-K caching at nodes. Handles frequency updates pipeline.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Adds personalization, trending detection with time decay, multi-language support, filter pipeline for inappropriate suggestions.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs ML-based semantic suggestions (not just prefix), real-time trending with stream processing, federated learning for personalization with privacy.
