quizBank.question: What data structure is primarily used for efficient prefix-based autocomplete?
quizBank.answer: Trie (prefix tree)
quizBank.explanation: Trie stores characters at each node, creating a tree where each path from root to a node represents a prefix. Prefix lookup is O(L) where L is prefix length. All suggestions for a prefix are in the subtree rooted at the prefix node.

quizBank.question: Why is SQL LIKE 'app%' insufficient for autocomplete at Google scale?
options: LIKE requires full table scan of billions of queries, making it too slow for 100ms SLA
quizBank.answer: LIKE requires full table scan of billions of queries, making it too slow for 100ms SLA
quizBank.explanation: LIKE 'app%' can use an index if it's a prefix search (not '%app%'). But with billions of queries, even indexed prefix search returns too many rows to sort by frequency within 10ms. Trie pre-computes the sorted top-K, making lookups O(prefix_length).

quizBank.question: What is the 'top-K cache at each node' optimization in a trie?
options: Pre-computing and storing the top 5 most frequent queries under each prefix node
Limiting trie depth to K levels
Caching the K most recently searched queries globally
quizBank.answer: Pre-computing and storing the top 5 most frequent queries under each prefix node
quizBank.explanation: Without optimization: find all terminal nodes under prefix → DFS, then sort by frequency → O(N) per request. With top-K cache: each node stores its top 5 completions pre-sorted. Lookup: traverse to prefix node, return stored top-5 → O(prefix_length). Much faster at the cost of memory.

quizBank.question: How do you update the trie when query frequencies change, without causing serving errors?
quizBank.answer: Blue-green deployment: (1) Current trie (blue) serves all traffic from memory. (2) Background: Spark calculates new frequencies. Trie Builder constructs new trie (green) in memory. (3) Atomic swap: update trie reference pointer (e.g., Redis key pointing to trie version). All new requests use green trie. (4) Blue trie kept for 5 minutes for rollback. Then garbage collected. Frequency: update every 5-10 minutes (acceptable staleness). Alternative: incremental update with read-write locks on individual nodes (millisecond locks). Simpler: load entire trie into each ACS server memory, broadcast new trie version periodically.

quizBank.question: Why should autocomplete requests be debounced on the client side?
options: To reduce server load by not sending a request for every single keystroke
quizBank.answer: To reduce server load by not sending a request for every single keystroke
quizBank.explanation: Without debounce: typing 'apple' fires 5 requests (a, ap, app, appl, apple). With 300ms debounce: only fires after user pauses 300ms. Reduces requests by ~80%. Implementation: setTimeout pattern — cancel previous timer on each keystroke, start new 100-300ms timer.

quizBank.question: How do you detect and boost trending queries in autocomplete?
quizBank.answer: Real-time frequency calculation: stream query events through Kafka. Kafka Streams or Flink: compute sliding window counts (e.g., last 60 minutes). Compare to baseline (same window last week): trending_score = current_count / historical_baseline. If trending_score > 5x: mark as trending. Time decay: weight recent queries more. Storage: trending_queries table updated every minute. Integration with autocomplete: when user's prefix matches a trending query, boost its rank score and add 'Trending' label. Threshold: trending queries must have minimum absolute count (100K searches in last hour) to filter noise.

quizBank.question: How do you handle race conditions when a user types quickly and multiple autocomplete requests are in-flight?
options: Queue all requests and process in order
Cancel previous in-flight request when new keystroke is detected; only process response matching current input
Show all responses as they arrive
quizBank.answer: Cancel previous in-flight request when new keystroke is detected; only process response matching current input
quizBank.explanation: User types 'ap' then 'app' quickly. Two requests in-flight. 'app' response may arrive before 'ap' response. Without handling: show 'ap' suggestions even though user typed 'app'. Solution: (1) Cancel previous XMLHttpRequest/AbortController. (2) Tag each request with the query string. Only render response if response.query === currentInput. This prevents stale suggestions from displaying.

quizBank.question: How would you implement personalized autocomplete without violating user privacy?
quizBank.answer: Approach: store user's recent query history server-side (last 90 days). At autocomplete time: fetch user's top N queries matching prefix. Boost these in suggestion ranking. Privacy safeguards: (1) Data minimization: store only query text and frequency, not full context. (2) Retention limit: auto-delete after 90 days. (3) User control: 'Pause search history' option, 'Delete all history'. (4) Differential privacy: add calibrated noise to frequency counts before training global model — prevents individual query identification. (5) On-device personalization (Apple approach): history never leaves device. Suggestions computed locally using on-device trie. More private but limited by device compute.

quizBank.question: What caching layer can serve 80% of autocomplete traffic without hitting the application server?
quizBank.answer: CDN edge cache
quizBank.explanation: Top 1000 prefixes (a, ap, app, apple, etc.) account for ~80% of traffic (Pareto principle). CDN caches responses for these popular prefixes at edge nodes worldwide. TTL: 60 seconds (suggestions update every few minutes). Requests for popular prefixes never reach origin server — served from CDN in <10ms.

quizBank.question: How do you support autocomplete in Chinese, which uses thousands of unique characters?
quizBank.answer: Approach 1 - Pinyin trie: users type pinyin (romanized pronunciation), trie built on pinyin. Suggestions show Chinese characters. 'n' → 'ni hao' (你好), 'zhongguo' (中国). Approach 2 - Unicode trie: trie nodes store Unicode code points. Each Chinese character is 3-4 bytes UTF-8. Trie size larger (more unique 'characters') but same algorithm. Approach 3 - IME integration: operating system IME handles character composition, sends composed character (一个汉字) to autocomplete. Trie built on Chinese characters directly. Separate trie per language: zh trie, en trie. Language detection from user preference. Query frequencies computed from zh-language searches only.

quizBank.question: How would you design autocomplete for voice search (spoken query autocomplete)?
quizBank.answer: Voice search autocomplete differs: no incremental typing. Partial speech recognized in real-time. ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition) converts audio to text incrementally. Stream partial transcription to autocomplete: as ASR outputs partial words, query autocomplete. Challenge: ASR output is uncertain — multiple hypotheses. Solution: (1) ASR provides N-best list (top 5 possible transcriptions with confidence scores). (2) Query autocomplete for each hypothesis. (3) Rank: combine ASR confidence x query frequency score. (4) Display: show suggestions while user is still speaking. (5) Low latency critical: ASR partial results + autocomplete < 200ms. Architecture: WebSocket for streaming partial ASR results to autocomplete service.

quizBank.question: What is the time complexity of finding top-5 suggestions for a given prefix in an optimized trie with top-K caching?
options: O(L) — traverse L characters to reach prefix node, then return cached top-5
O(K log N) — sort K candidates
O(N log N) — sort all queries
quizBank.answer: O(L) — traverse L characters to reach prefix node, then return cached top-5
quizBank.explanation: With top-K cache at each node: traverse L characters to reach prefix node (O(L)). Return pre-cached top-5 list (O(1)). Total: O(L) where L is prefix length (typically 2-7 characters). Without optimization: O(N) to traverse subtree and sort.

quizBank.question: A user searches for inappropriate content and it starts showing up as an autocomplete suggestion. How do you handle this?
quizBank.answer: Multi-layer filtering: (1) Blocklist: hardcoded list of banned terms and patterns. Applied at trie query time — filter out any suggestion matching blocklist. (2) ML classifier: model trained on safe/unsafe queries. Score each candidate suggestion. Filter if score below threshold. (3) Frequency threshold: minimum global frequency (1000/day) before suggestion appears — low-frequency inappropriate queries naturally filtered. (4) Abuse report: users can report suggestions via 'Report inappropriate suggestion'. Routes to moderation queue. (5) Regional compliance: illegal content in specific countries filtered by geo-IP routing. (6) Response time: blocklist check must be <1ms to maintain latency SLA — use Bloom filter or in-memory set for fast lookup.

quizBank.question: What is the trade-off between trie depth limit and suggestion quality?
quizBank.answer: Deeper trie: more specific suggestions for long prefixes. User typing 'machine learning python tuto' gets specific suggestions. More memory (more nodes). Slower to build and update. Shallower trie: only suggests for short prefixes (1-5 chars). Less memory. Faster lookup. Misses long-tail specific queries. In practice: depth of 20-25 characters covers most queries. Very long queries (sentences) handled by full-text search fallback. Alternative: trie for short prefixes (up to 8 chars), switch to Elasticsearch for longer queries. This balances memory efficiency with comprehensive coverage.

quizBank.question: What is an alternative to trie for autocomplete that Redis supports natively?
options: Redis Sorted Sets with ZRANGEBYLEX command
quizBank.answer: Redis Sorted Sets with ZRANGEBYLEX command
quizBank.explanation: ZRANGEBYLEX performs lexicographic range queries on sorted set members. Store all queries as members (score=0). ZRANGEBYLEX suggest '[app' '[app\xff' returns all members starting with 'app'. Combined with a second sorted set (member=query, score=frequency) for ranking. Simpler than trie, leverages Redis's existing infrastructure. Trade-off: all queries must fit in Redis sorted set; harder to implement top-K pre-computation.

quizBank.question: How do you handle the autocomplete system when a major news event causes a new query ('earthquake 2024') to need immediate autocomplete support?
quizBank.answer: Near-real-time update pipeline: event causes surge in query frequency for 'earthquake 2024'. Kafka receives thousands of these queries per second. Kafka Streams computes 5-minute rolling window counts. Frequency threshold crossed: query enters trending tier. Trie update: triggered more frequently during trending events (every 1 minute instead of 5). Hot path: bypass trie for trending queries — dedicated trending cache in Redis. If 'earthquake 2024' crosses trending threshold, it's injected into suggestions for 'earthquake' prefix regardless of trie. Label as 'Trending' for UX context. Staleness: with 1-minute update cycle, new viral queries appear in suggestions within 2-3 minutes of trending.

quizBank.question: How would you design autocomplete for a domain-specific search (e.g., medical queries) vs general web search?
quizBank.answer: Domain-specific differences: medical queries have specialized vocabulary (drug names, conditions, procedures). General approach still applies (trie + frequency). Differences: (1) Vocabulary: pre-populate trie with medical terminology from controlled vocabularies (ICD-10, SNOMED, DrugBank). Not only frequency-driven — important terms included regardless of search frequency. (2) Spelling correction: medical terms misspelled frequently — fuzzy matching important. (3) Safety: suggest proper medical terms, not colloquial misspellings. (4) Authority: suggestions from authoritative sources prioritized (Mayo Clinic, WebMD). (5) Personalization: doctor users get different suggestions than patient users. (6) Regulatory: HIPAA considerations for query logging.

quizBank.question: How frequently should the trie be updated from new query frequency data?
quizBank.answer: Every 5-10 minutes (batch update)
quizBank.explanation: Real-time: too expensive — rebuild trie for every query. Once per day: trending queries miss suggestions for hours. 5-10 minutes: balances freshness with cost. Trending queries appear in suggestions within ~10 minutes of becoming viral. Batch processing (Spark) aggregates enough queries for statistically significant frequency changes.

quizBank.question: How do you estimate the memory needed to store a trie for the top 1 million English search queries?
quizBank.answer: Average query length: 5 words ≈ 30 characters. Total characters across all queries: 1M queries x 30 chars = 30M characters. With prefix sharing (common prefixes share nodes), actual node count is much less. Estimate: 10M unique trie nodes (many queries share 'the', 'how to', 'what is' prefixes). Each node: char (1 byte) + frequency (8 bytes) + top-5 list (5 x 50 bytes = 250 bytes) + children pointers (26 x 8 bytes = 208 bytes) ≈ 470 bytes per node. Total: 10M nodes x 470 bytes = 4.7GB. In practice: compressed trie (DAWG) reduces this significantly. English autocomplete fits comfortably in a 16-32GB server RAM.

quizBank.question: How would you design a spell-correction feature that works alongside autocomplete?
quizBank.answer: Edit distance approach: compute Levenshtein distance between user input and known queries. For 'appel' → suggest 'apple' (edit distance 1). Problem: computing distance against all known queries is O(N x L) — too slow. Optimizations: (1) BK-Tree: metric tree for edit distance queries. Find all queries within distance 1-2 in O(log N). (2) Trigram index: index queries by character trigrams. User input trigrams → find candidates, compute exact edit distance only for candidates. (3) Phonetic matching: Soundex/Metaphone handles phonetic misspellings (common for proper nouns). (4) Neural approaches: character-level language model predicts intended query. (5) Threshold: only suggest correction if input not in trie AND edit distance ≤ 2. Show 'Did you mean: apple?' below autocomplete results.

quizBank.question: What is 'query normalization' in the context of building autocomplete frequency data?
options: Standardizing queries (lowercase, remove punctuation, trim spaces) before counting frequency
Normalizing query frequency scores to 0-1 range
quizBank.answer: Standardizing queries (lowercase, remove punctuation, trim spaces) before counting frequency
quizBank.explanation: Without normalization: 'Apple', 'apple', 'Apple.' and 'apple ' are treated as 4 different queries despite same intent. Normalization: lowercase, trim whitespace, remove trailing punctuation, collapse multiple spaces. Result: all map to 'apple' — frequency correctly aggregated. This doubles effective frequency count and reduces trie size.

quizBank.question: How would you scale autocomplete to 3 million requests per second (10x Google's current scale)?
quizBank.answer: 3M QPS requires massive parallelism. Current bottleneck: each request does trie lookup. Solutions: (1) In-memory serving: each ACS server loads full trie (50MB) into RAM. 3M QPS / 50K QPS per server = 60 servers. Trivially scalable. (2) CDN layer: 80% served from CDN (2.4M QPS absorbed). Only 600K QPS hit origin. (3) Client-side caching: JavaScript caches last 100 prefix→suggestions mappings. Many keystrokes serve from client cache. (4) Request collapsing: CDN collapses concurrent identical prefix requests into one origin call. (5) Pre-computation: top 10,000 prefixes pre-computed and pushed to CDN proactively. (6) Edge computing: run trie lookup at CDN edge (Cloudflare Workers) — sub-millisecond response without origin server.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Search Autocomplete (Google)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design a Distributed Key-Value Store

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- PUT(key, value): store a key-value pair
- GET(key): retrieve value by key
- DELETE(key): remove a key
- TTL support: keys expire automatically
- Replication: data replicated across multiple nodes
- Horizontal scaling: add nodes to increase capacity

### Non-Functional Requirements
- High availability: 99.999% (five nines)
- Low latency: <1ms for GET, <5ms for PUT
- Durability: data survives node failures
- Scalability: handle petabytes of data, millions of ops/sec
- CAP trade-off: choose AP (available + partition-tolerant) like Dynamo/Cassandra

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- 1M writes/sec, 10M reads/sec
- Average key: 100 bytes, value: 1KB
- Total data: 10TB across cluster
- Replication factor: 3 (3 copies of each key)
- Total storage needed: 30TB
- Nodes: 30TB / 2TB per node = 15 nodes (for storage)
- Read nodes: 10M reads/sec / 100K reads/sec per node = 100 read nodes
- Cluster size: ~100 nodes for read throughput

## 3. Core Architecture Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([Client])
    CL[Client Library / SDK]
    LB[Load Balancer]
    N1[Node 1 Primary]
    N2[Node 2 Replica]
    N3[Node 3 Replica]
    N4[Node 4 Primary]
    N5[Node 5 Replica]
    N6[Node 6 Replica]
    GS[Gossip Protocol]
    ZK[Coordinator ZooKeeper]

    Client --> CL
    CL -->|Consistent Hashing| LB
    LB --> N1
    LB --> N4
    N1 <-->|Replication| N2
    N1 <-->|Replication| N3
    N4 <-->|Replication| N5
    N4 <-->|Replication| N6
    N1 <-->|Gossip| N4
    N2 <-->|Gossip| N5
    N3 <-->|Gossip| N6
    ZK -->|Cluster membership| N1
    ZK -->|Cluster membership| N4
```

## 4. Consistent Hashing

### Why Consistent Hashing?
- Simple modulo hashing (key % N): adding/removing a node remaps all keys → massive data migration
- Consistent hashing: only K/N keys remapped on node addition/removal (K=keys, N=nodes)

### Implementation
```
Virtual nodes (vnodes):
- Hash ring: 0 to 2^32 (4 billion positions)
- Each physical node is responsible for multiple positions (vnodes)
- Default: 150 vnodes per physical node
- Key → hash → find next clockwise node on ring

Example:
Ring: [Node A at pos 100], [Node B at pos 200], [Node C at pos 300]
Key hash = 150 → Node B (next clockwise from 150)
Key hash = 250 → Node C
Key hash = 350 → Node A (wraps around)

Adding Node D at pos 175:
Only keys between 150 and 175 migrate from Node B to Node D
```

## 5. Replication

### Strategy
- Replication factor N=3 (default in Dynamo/Cassandra)
- Primary node + 2 replicas
- Replicas: next N-1 nodes clockwise on hash ring
- Write to coordinator (primary) → async replicate to replicas

### Quorum
```
N = total replicas
W = write quorum (must acknowledge)
R = read quorum (must respond)

For strong consistency: W + R > N
For availability: W=1, R=1 (fastest, eventual consistency)
For balanced: W=2, R=2, N=3 (Cassandra default QUORUM)

Common configurations:
- QUORUM: W=N/2+1, R=N/2+1 → consistent reads
- ONE:    W=1, R=1 → highest availability
- ALL:    W=N, R=N → strongest but least available
```

## 6. Data Storage Engine

### LSM-Tree (Log-Structured Merge Tree) — Used by Cassandra, RocksDB
```
Write path:
1. Write to Write-Ahead Log (WAL) — durable
2. Write to Memtable (in-memory sorted tree) — fast
3. When Memtable full: flush to SSTable on disk (immutable sorted file)
4. Background: merge/compact SSTables (remove duplicates, sort)

Read path:
1. Check Memtable (in-memory) — latest data
2. Check Bloom filter for each SSTable — does key exist here?
3. If Bloom filter positive: read SSTable
4. Return most recent value (by timestamp)

Advantages:
- Write-optimized: writes go to sequential log (fast)
- Bloom filters minimize disk reads for missing keys
Disadvantages:
- Read amplification: may check multiple SSTables
- Space amplification: compaction needed
```

### B-Tree (Used by MySQL, PostgreSQL, traditional RDBMS)
- Read-optimized, in-place updates
- Better read amplification, worse write amplification
- Less suitable for distributed KV stores at high write throughput

## 7. Conflict Resolution

### Vector Clocks
```
Each value tagged with vector clock: {node: counter}
Client A writes key=foo value=X: {nodeA: 1}
Client B also writes key=foo value=Y at same time: {nodeB: 1}
Conflict: two versions with incomparable clocks

Resolution strategies:
1. Last-Write-Wins (LWW): use wall clock timestamp, higher wins
   - Simple but prone to clock skew issues
2. Server-side merge: for certain types (counters: add them)
3. Client-side resolution: return both versions, client resolves
   (Amazon shopping cart uses this: merge item lists)
```

### Merkle Trees for Consistency
- Each node maintains Merkle tree of its data
- Tree hash reflects all key-value data
- Two nodes compare Merkle tree hashes to find inconsistencies
- Efficient: only exchange tree hashes (not data) to find differing subtrees
- Cassandra anti-entropy repair uses this

## 8. Gossip Protocol

```
Gossip for cluster membership and failure detection:
- Each node maintains a membership list: {node_id, heartbeat, last_seen}
- Every T seconds: pick K random nodes, share membership list
- Nodes update their list with newer heartbeat counts
- Failure detection: if heartbeat not updated in T_fail seconds → suspect dead
- Phi accrual failure detector: probabilistic, adapts to network conditions

Gossip handles:
- Node joins: new node announces itself, propagates via gossip
- Node failures: heartbeat stops updating, cluster detects in ~10 seconds
- Partition recovery: nodes reconcile state after partition heals
```

## 9. API Design

```
HTTP/REST API:
PUT /api/v1/keys/{key}
Body: { "value": "...", "ttl_seconds": 3600 }
Headers: X-Consistency: QUORUM
Response 200: { "version": "...", "node": "node-42" }

GET /api/v1/keys/{key}
Headers: X-Consistency: ONE
Response 200: { "key": "...", "value": "...", "ttl_remaining": 3540, "version": "..." }
Response 404: { "error": "key_not_found" }

DELETE /api/v1/keys/{key}
Response 204

GET /api/v1/keys/{key}/metadata — version, node info
GET /api/v1/cluster/status     — node health, ring state

Binary protocol (for production, lower overhead):
Redis RESP protocol or Memcached binary protocol
```

## 10. Data Model

### In-Memory Structure (per node)
```
Hash map: key → {value, version, ttl, created_at, last_updated}
Sorted set (for TTL): score=expire_time, member=key (for fast expiry scan)

Persistence:
- Write-Ahead Log (WAL): append-only log for durability
- Snapshots: periodic full data snapshot to disk
- SSTable: immutable sorted files (LSM-tree)
```

### Metadata Table
```sql
-- Node registry (kept in ZooKeeper or similar)
CREATE TABLE cluster_nodes (
    node_id      VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY,
    ip_address   VARCHAR(45),
    port         INT,
    vnode_ids    JSON,      -- list of virtual node positions
    status       VARCHAR(20), -- active, suspect, down, leaving
    joined_at    TIMESTAMP,
    last_seen    TIMESTAMP
);
```

## 11. Hinted Handoff

```
Scenario: Write arrives, target node is temporarily down
Solution:
1. Coordinator stores 'hint': {key, value, intended_node}
2. Hint stored on coordinator (or another available node)
3. When target node recovers: coordinator delivers hinted write
4. Provides 'sloppy quorum': write succeeds even when target down
5. Hint TTL: discard hints older than X hours (node likely permanently failed)
```

## 12. TTL Implementation

```
Lazy expiration: check TTL on every GET, return null if expired
Active expiration: background thread scans for expired keys
  - Redis: 100ms cycle, randomly sample 20 keys, delete expired ones,
    repeat if >25% expired (probabilistic)
  - Cassandra: expired rows handled by compaction

Memory: expired keys not removed immediately → memory waste
Solution: combine lazy + active expiration
```

## 13. Monitoring
- Read/write latency p99 per operation type
- Cache hit rate (for in-memory stores)
- Replication lag (primary → replica)
- Node health (gossip failure detector)
- Compaction progress (LSM-tree)
- Memory usage per node
- Key eviction rate
- Cluster rebalancing progress after node change

quizBank.question: What does the CAP theorem state?
options: A distributed system can have all three: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
A distributed system can guarantee at most two of: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
All distributed systems must sacrifice availability
CAP stands for Create, Archive, Purge
quizBank.answer: A distributed system can guarantee at most two of: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
quizBank.explanation: CAP theorem: in the presence of network partition (P), you must choose between Consistency (C — all nodes see same data) and Availability (A — every request gets a response). In practice, P is unavoidable in distributed systems, so choice is CA tradeoff. Dynamo chooses AP; Zookeeper/etcd choose CP.

quizBank.question: What is the advantage of consistent hashing over modulo hashing for data distribution?
options: Adding or removing a node only remaps K/N keys instead of remapping all keys
Consistent hashing supports more data types
quizBank.answer: Adding or removing a node only remaps K/N keys instead of remapping all keys
quizBank.explanation: Modulo: key % N. Add node → N changes → almost all keys remapped → massive data migration. Consistent hashing: hash ring. Adding a node only affects keys between it and its predecessor → only K/N keys migrate. N=10 nodes, K=1M keys → adding 1 node migrates ~100K keys (vs 1M with modulo).

quizBank.question: For N=3 replicas, W=2 writes, R=2 reads: is strong consistency achieved?
options: Yes, because W+R=4 > N=3, guaranteeing overlap between write and read quorums
No, need W=3 for strong consistency
quizBank.answer: Yes, because W+R=4 > N=3, guaranteeing overlap between write and read quorums
quizBank.explanation: W+R>N guarantees at least one node is in both write quorum and read quorum — that node has the latest write. With W=2, R=2, N=3: at least 1 node overlaps. Read returns latest value. Trade-off: higher quorum = lower availability (must wait for W/R responses).

quizBank.question: Two clients simultaneously write different values to the same key on two different nodes (network partition). How do you resolve this conflict?
quizBank.answer: Conflict occurs: node A has value=X with vector clock {A:1}, node B has value=Y with vector clock {B:1}. Neither clock dominates the other → conflict. Resolution options: (1) Last-Write-Wins (LWW): compare wall-clock timestamps, higher wins. Simple but prone to clock skew — wrong winner possible. (2) Multi-version: return both values to client, let client resolve. Amazon Dynamo uses this for shopping cart (merge both carts). (3) CRDT: use data structure that's inherently conflict-free (e.g., PN-Counter, OR-Set). (4) Application-specific merge: sum for counters, union for sets. In practice: LWW for most data, application-specific for critical data.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of virtual nodes (vnodes) in consistent hashing?
options: To create virtual servers for cost savings
To distribute each physical node across multiple points on the hash ring, improving load balance
To replicate data to virtual machines
quizBank.answer: To distribute each physical node across multiple points on the hash ring, improving load balance
quizBank.explanation: Without vnodes: physical nodes may get uneven key distribution by chance. With vnodes: each physical node occupies 150 positions on ring. Load is statistically uniform. Also: when a node fails, its load distributes to all other nodes (not just neighbors). When adding a node: it takes keys from many nodes (faster rebalancing).

quizBank.question: Explain the LSM-tree write path and why it's faster than B-tree for write-heavy workloads.
quizBank.answer: LSM-tree write path: (1) Write to WAL (append-only, sequential I/O → very fast). (2) Write to Memtable (in-memory sorted tree, e.g., skip list or red-black tree → O(log N) in RAM). (3) Return success to client — done. (4) Background: when Memtable reaches threshold (e.g., 64MB), flush to disk as immutable SSTable. (5) Compaction: merge SSTables, remove duplicates/tombstones. Why faster than B-tree: B-tree does random I/O for each write (must update page in-place on disk). LSM-tree converts random writes to sequential writes (WAL + sequential SSTable flush). Sequential I/O is 100x faster than random I/O on HDD, and significantly faster on SSD too.

quizBank.question: What is a Bloom filter used for in an LSM-tree based KV store?
options: To determine with certainty if a key exists
To avoid reading SSTables for keys that don't exist in that file
quizBank.answer: To avoid reading SSTables for keys that don't exist in that file
quizBank.explanation: Read path: key may be in any SSTable. Without Bloom filter: check every SSTable (expensive disk I/O). Bloom filter: probabilistic set membership test. If filter says 'key not in this SSTable' → skip (definitely not there). If 'may be in SSTable' → check SSTable. False positives possible (read SSTable unnecessarily) but no false negatives. Reduces read amplification by 10x.

quizBank.question: A node fails and recovers after 2 hours. How do you ensure it has consistent data?
quizBank.answer: Multi-mechanism recovery: (1) Hinted handoff: coordinator held hints for writes during node's absence. On recovery, deliver hints. Hints with TTL > 2 hours still valid → applied. Older hints discarded (node may have been replaced). (2) Merkle tree anti-entropy: recovered node computes Merkle tree hash of its data. Coordinator compares with expected state. Mismatched subtrees → stream missing data. (3) Read repair: during normal reads (QUORUM), if replica has stale data: coordinator sends correction. (4) Streaming repair: explicit repair command (Cassandra 'nodetool repair') for full consistency check. Priority: hinted handoff first (fast), then Merkle repair (thorough).

quizBank.question: What is 'eventual consistency' in a distributed KV store?
options: Given enough time with no new updates, all replicas will converge to the same value
Only some nodes are eventually consistent
Consistency is achieved after a fixed time period
quizBank.answer: Given enough time with no new updates, all replicas will converge to the same value
quizBank.explanation: Eventual consistency: replicas may temporarily diverge after a write, but will converge to the same state eventually (seconds to minutes) through replication. Reads may return stale data during this window. Trade-off for high availability — choose AP over CP in CAP theorem.

quizBank.question: How does the gossip protocol help detect node failures in a distributed KV store?
quizBank.answer: Gossip membership protocol: (1) Each node maintains membership list: {node_id, heartbeat_counter, last_updated}. (2) Every 1 second: select K random nodes, exchange membership lists. (3) Merge: update local list with highest heartbeat counts seen. (4) A live node increments its own heartbeat counter continuously. (5) Failure detection: if node X's heartbeat hasn't increased in T_fail seconds (e.g., 10s), mark as 'suspect'. If still no update after T_dead (e.g., 30s): mark as 'dead'. (6) Propagation: failure info propagates in O(log N) gossip rounds. Advantage: no single point of failure (no central coordinator for health), decentralized, scales to 1000s of nodes.

quizBank.question: What is 'hinted handoff' and what problem does it solve?
options: Routing hints to the correct node
Storing writes for temporarily failed nodes and delivering them on recovery
Hinting to clients which node to connect to
Hand-off of leadership in a Raft cluster
quizBank.answer: Storing writes for temporarily failed nodes and delivering them on recovery
quizBank.explanation: Scenario: write arrives, target node N3 is down. Without hinted handoff: write fails (can't meet quorum). With hinted handoff: coordinator stores {key, value, intended_node=N3} as hint. Returns success to client (sloppy quorum). When N3 recovers: coordinator delivers hint → N3 catches up. Enables availability during short-lived failures. Hint TTL prevents unbounded hint storage.

quizBank.question: How do you implement TTL expiration for 1 billion keys without scanning all of them every second?
quizBank.answer: Hybrid approach: (1) Lazy expiration: every GET checks if key is expired. Return nil and delete if expired. No background scan needed — O(1) per access. (2) Active expiration (sampled): every 100ms, randomly sample 20 keys from expiry index. Delete expired ones. If >25% of sampled were expired: repeat immediately (backpressure). Stop if <25% expired. Over time, most expired keys cleaned up. (3) TTL index: sorted set with score=expire_timestamp. Sample from front (earliest expiring). (4) Approximate: accept that expired keys may linger briefly. Application must handle 'key exists but may be expired' case. Redis uses this exact approach — very effective in practice.

quizBank.question: What are CRDTs and when should a KV store use them instead of last-write-wins?
quizBank.answer: CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type): data structure designed so all concurrent operations can be merged deterministically, always converging to same result. Examples: (1) G-Counter (grow-only counter): each node has own counter, total = sum of all. Merge = max per node. No conflicts. (2) PN-Counter: two G-Counters (increment/decrement). (3) OR-Set: add-wins set. (4) LWW-Element-Set: set with timestamps. Use CRDTs when: collaborative data (collaborative document editing, shared counters, distributed sets). Last-Write-Wins when: simple scalar values where latest is correct (user profile update, cache values). CRDTs avoid conflict resolution complexity at the cost of more complex data structure and merge logic. Riak pioneered CRDTs in KV stores.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a Write-Ahead Log (WAL) in a KV store?
options: To ensure durability: if node crashes, WAL allows recovery of in-memory data that wasn't yet flushed to disk
quizBank.answer: To ensure durability: if node crashes, WAL allows recovery of in-memory data that wasn't yet flushed to disk
quizBank.explanation: Without WAL: data in Memtable is lost on crash. With WAL: every write first goes to append-only WAL on disk (fast sequential write). On crash recovery: replay WAL to reconstruct Memtable. Once SSTable flushed, corresponding WAL entries can be truncated. WAL is why LSM-tree can claim durability despite in-memory writes.

quizBank.question: Your KV store cluster needs to add 10 new nodes to increase capacity. How do you do this without downtime?
quizBank.answer: Rolling addition: (1) Add new node to gossip ring — it announces itself. (2) Token assignment: assign vnodes (virtual positions) to new node. Approximately K/N keys will migrate from existing nodes. (3) Data streaming: each source node streams keys in new node's range. New node receives data while serving reads from source nodes (no downtime). (4) Verification: new node confirms data received (Merkle tree comparison). (5) Switchover: once data verified, new node starts serving reads/writes for its token range. Source nodes remove migrated keys (or allow expiry). (6) Repeat for each new node. Monitor: rebalancing I/O, ensure it doesn't impact production latency (throttle if needed). Total time: proportional to data volume and network bandwidth.

quizBank.question: When would you choose a KV store over a relational database? Give three specific scenarios.
quizBank.answer: Scenario 1 — Session storage: store user session data (session_id → session_data). Simple lookup by key, no joins needed, TTL (session expiry), high throughput, horizontal scale. Redis is perfect. Scenario 2 — Leaderboard/counters: game scores (player_id → score). Need INCR, ZREVRANGE for top N. Redis Sorted Sets purpose-built for this. Relational DB would struggle at 1M score updates/sec. Scenario 3 — Content caching: cache HTML pages, API responses (url_hash → rendered_html). Pure key-value access, cache semantics (TTL, eviction), millisecond latency required. No relational structure needed. When to use RDBMS instead: complex queries with JOINs, ACID transactions across multiple entities, foreign key constraints, structured reporting.

quizBank.question: What problem does Merkle tree anti-entropy solve in a distributed KV store?
options: Efficient detection of data inconsistencies between replicas without transferring all data
quizBank.answer: Efficient detection of data inconsistencies between replicas without transferring all data
quizBank.explanation: Anti-entropy without Merkle: compare all key-value pairs between replicas → O(N) data transfer. Merkle tree: tree of hashes. Root hash = hash of all data. If root hashes match → replicas identical. If differ → recurse down tree to find differing leaf (specific key range with inconsistency). Only transfer: tree hashes + specific differing values. Reduces data transfer from O(N) to O(inconsistencies x log N).

quizBank.question: A client reads a key and gets an old value because it hit a stale replica. How do you detect and fix this?
quizBank.answer: Detection: vector clocks or timestamps on values. Client reads value with timestamp T1 from replica A. Client does another read and gets value with timestamp T2 < T1 from replica B — stale! Fix options: (1) Read repair: on QUORUM read, coordinator compares all replica responses. Replica with older version receives updated value from coordinator. Passive, no extra round trip needed. (2) Monotonic reads: client sends last_seen_version with each read. Server guarantees version ≥ last_seen (route to nodes that have that version). Useful for stateful clients. (3) Strong consistency mode: use QUORUM reads always. Client always gets latest value. Higher latency trade-off.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between a cache (like Redis) and a persistent KV store (like DynamoDB)? When do you use each?
quizBank.answer: Cache (Redis): data stored in RAM. Extremely fast (sub-millisecond). Limited by RAM size. Data can be evicted (LRU) or expires (TTL). Not primary source of truth. Restart loses data (without persistence enabled). Use for: session state, computed results, rate limit counters, leaderboards, pub/sub. Persistent KV store (DynamoDB, Cassandra): data persisted to disk. Can store petabytes. Slower than RAM (SSDs, but still fast). Always durable. Primary source of truth. Use for: user profiles, order history, product catalog, anything you can't afford to lose. In practice: often use both — DynamoDB as primary store, Redis as cache in front. Cache-aside pattern: check Redis first, fall back to DynamoDB on miss, populate Redis.

quizBank.question: In the Raft consensus algorithm used by etcd, what is a 'leader election'?
options: Process where nodes vote to elect one leader for handling all writes, ensuring linearizable consistency
quizBank.answer: Process where nodes vote to elect one leader for handling all writes, ensuring linearizable consistency
quizBank.explanation: Raft: CP (consistent) distributed consensus. One leader handles all writes, replicates to followers. If leader fails, followers hold election: candidate requests votes, wins majority → new leader. Guarantees linearizability (reads always see latest write) at cost of availability during elections. Used by etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV. Contrast with Dynamo/Cassandra (leaderless, AP, eventual consistency).

quizBank.question: Design a geo-distributed KV store where users in Europe must see EU data, and US users see US data (data residency requirements).
quizBank.answer: Regional partitioning: partition data by user's region at the key level or routing level. Key scheme: {region}:{user_id}:{key}. Routing: region detected from user's IP or account profile. EU requests route to EU cluster. US requests route to US cluster. Cross-region replication: for EU/US shared data (e.g., product catalog): replicate to both regions. For user-specific data: store only in home region. Enforcement: API gateway validates user's region matches data region. Reject cross-region reads for regulated data. Challenge: user traveling (EU user in US): must still read EU cluster (GDPR compliance). Latency penalty accepted. Global services (authentication, routing): must be available in all regions. Key management: region-specific encryption keys (BYOK in EU for GDPR).

quizBank.question: How do you handle a 'hot key' problem where one key (e.g., a celebrity's profile) gets 1 million reads per second?
quizBank.answer: Hot key (celebrity problem): 1M reads/sec to single key overwhelms the responsible node. Solutions: (1) Client-side caching: SDK caches hot keys in process memory (e.g., 100ms TTL). Reduces server load by 100x. (2) Read replicas: create extra replicas specifically for hot keys. Route to replicas, not just primary+replicas. (3) Key splitting: break one key into K shards: celeb_profile_0 through celeb_profile_9. Each shard gets 100K reads/sec. Client randomly selects shard. Works for read-only data. Writes: update all shards. (4) Local caching tier: L1 cache in each app server (in-process, 1ms TTL). Hot keys served without touching KV cluster. (5) CDN: for immutable data, cache at CDN edge. (6) Detection: monitor key access frequency, automatically promote hot keys to L1 cache.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Distributed Key-Value Store (Redis/DynamoDB)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design a Ride-Sharing Service (Uber/Lyft)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Rider requests a ride from location A to B
- System matches rider with nearby available driver
- Real-time driver location tracking (live map)
- ETA calculation and route navigation
- Dynamic pricing (surge pricing)
- Payment processing at ride completion
- Rating system (rider ↔ driver)
- Trip history
- Driver app: accept/reject rides, go online/offline

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Low latency matching: driver matched in <5 seconds
- Real-time tracking: driver location updated every 5 seconds
- High availability: 99.99% (can't drop rides)
- Scale: 25M trips/day, 5M drivers worldwide
- Geospatial accuracy: within 50 meters

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- Trips: 25M/day = ~290 trips/sec peak
- Drivers online: assume 500K at peak (20% of 5M driving at any time)
- Driver location updates: 500K drivers x 1 update/5s = 100K location updates/sec
- Ride requests: 290 requests/sec
- ETA computations: ~10 nearby drivers evaluated per request = 2,900 ETA/sec
- Location data storage: 500K drivers x 1 update/5s = 864M updates/day x 50 bytes = 43GB/day

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DriverApp([Driver App])
    RiderApp([Rider App])
    AG[API Gateway]
    LS[Location Service]
    MS[Matching Service]
    TS[Trip Service]
    PS[Pricing Service]
    RS[Routing / ETA Service]
    PAY[Payment Service]
    NS[Notification Service]
    GeoCache[Geospatial Index - Redis]
    LocationDB[(Location History - Cassandra)]
    TripDB[(Trip DB - MySQL Sharded)]
    MapSvc[Mapping Service - OSRM/Google Maps]
    Kafka[Event Bus]

    DriverApp -->|Location update| AG
    RiderApp -->|Ride request| AG
    AG --> LS
    AG --> MS
    AG --> TS
    LS --> GeoCache
    LS --> LocationDB
    MS --> GeoCache
    MS --> RS
    MS --> PS
    RS --> MapSvc
    TS --> TripDB
    TS --> Kafka
    Kafka --> PAY
    Kafka --> NS
```

## 4. Geospatial Indexing

### Problem
- 500K driver locations changing every 5 seconds
- Need to find all drivers within 2km of rider in <50ms
- Naive: scan all 500K driver locations → too slow

### Geohash
```
Geohash: encode lat/lng into a string prefix
Higher precision = smaller area
- Length 6: ~1.2km x 0.6km (for finding nearby drivers)
- Length 5: ~5km x 5km

Algorithm:
1. Encode rider's location to geohash: 'u4pruydqqvj' (length 6)
2. Query Redis: SMEMBERS drivers:{geohash}
3. Also query 8 neighboring cells (to handle boundary cases)
4. Return driver IDs within cells
5. Filter by exact distance (haversine formula)

Redis structure:
Key: drivers:{geohash6}  (e.g., drivers:u4pruyd)
Type: Hash
Value: {driver_id → {lat, lng, car_type, timestamp}}
```

### Alternative: S2 Geometry (Google's approach)
- Hierarchical cell-based geospatial index
- Better coverage of sphere (no edge cases at poles)
- Used by Google, Lyft
- S2 cell level 13 ≈ 1km² area

## 5. Driver Matching Algorithm

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Rider App
    participant MS as Matching Service
    participant GI as Geospatial Index
    participant RS as Routing Service
    participant D as Driver App
    participant NS as Notification Service

    R->>MS: Ride request {pickup, dropoff, car_type}
    MS->>GI: Find drivers in pickup geohash + neighbors
    GI-->>MS: [driver_1, driver_2, driver_3, driver_4, driver_5]
    MS->>RS: Calculate ETA for each driver (parallel)
    RS->>RS: Road-network routing (OSRM/Mapbox)
    RS-->>MS: ETAs: [driver_1: 2min, driver_2: 4min, driver_3: 1min]
    MS->>MS: Rank by score: ETA + rating + acceptance_rate
    MS->>NS: Send ride offer to driver_3 (best match)
    D->>MS: Accept offer
    MS->>R: Driver assigned: {driver_3, ETA: 1min, car: Toyota Prius}
    Note over MS: If driver rejects: offer to driver_1 (next best)
```

### Matching Scoring
```
match_score = w1*(1/eta_minutes) + w2*driver_rating + w3*acceptance_rate
  - Minimize ETA (primary factor)
  - Prefer higher-rated drivers
  - Prefer drivers with high acceptance rates (fairness)
  - Car type filter: Pool vs X vs Black
```

## 6. Real-Time Location Tracking

### Driver Location Pipeline
```
Driver app → Location Service → Redis GeoHash Index
                             → Kafka (async) → Cassandra (history)

Driver update rate: every 5 seconds
Redis update: HSET drivers:{geohash} driver_id {lat, lng, ts}
Rider tracking: WebSocket to rider showing driver's live position

Kafka topic: driver_locations (partition by driver_id)
Cassandra: store all location history for trip replay, analytics
```

### Rider Tracking WebSocket
- Rider opens WebSocket connection to Track Service
- Track Service subscribes to Redis Pub/Sub: driver:{driver_id}:location
- Every time driver updates location → published to Redis → pushed to rider via WebSocket
- Rider app shows live moving marker on map

## 7. ETA Calculation

### Road Network Routing
- Simple Euclidean distance (as-the-crow-flies) is inaccurate
- Need road-network distance: traffic, one-way streets, turns
- Dijkstra's algorithm on road graph
- A* with heuristic for faster routing
- Production: pre-compute route tables (Contraction Hierarchies)
- Services: Google Maps API, Mapbox, OSRM (open-source)

### Traffic Integration
- Historical traffic: time-of-day patterns
- Real-time traffic: data from GPS of moving vehicles
- ETA = baseline_time * traffic_multiplier
- Update ETA every minute during trip as traffic changes

## 8. Surge Pricing

```
Surge multiplier = f(supply, demand)

Inputs:
- Demand: ride requests in area per minute
- Supply: available drivers in area
- Supply/Demand ratio < 0.5: high surge
- Supply/Demand ratio > 2: no surge

Formula (simplified):
if demand > supply_threshold: surge = 1.0 + (demand_rate * demand_factor)

Geographic granularity:
- Compute surge per S2/geohash cell
- Adjacent cells smoothed to avoid cliff edges
- Update every 1 minute
- Display heatmap to drivers: high surge areas attract them

Communicate to rider:
- Show surge multiplier before confirming
- Require explicit acceptance
- Log surge at time of acceptance
```

## 9. API Design

```
Rider APIs:
POST /api/v1/rides/estimate     — estimate price and ETA before booking
POST /api/v1/rides              — request ride {pickup, dropoff, ride_type}
GET  /api/v1/rides/{ride_id}    — ride status
DEL  /api/v1/rides/{ride_id}    — cancel ride
GET  /api/v1/rides/history      — past trips

Driver APIs:
PUT  /api/v1/driver/location    — update location {lat, lng}
PUT  /api/v1/driver/status      — go online/offline
POST /api/v1/driver/rides/{id}/accept  — accept ride offer
POST /api/v1/driver/rides/{id}/reject  — reject offer
POST /api/v1/driver/rides/{id}/start   — start trip
POST /api/v1/driver/rides/{id}/complete — end trip

Tracking API:
GET  /api/v1/rides/{id}/track   — WebSocket for live driver location
```

## 10. Data Models

### Trips Table (MySQL Sharded by rider_id)
```sql
CREATE TABLE trips (
    trip_id         VARCHAR(36) PRIMARY KEY,
    rider_id        BIGINT NOT NULL,
    driver_id       BIGINT,
    status          ENUM('requested','matched','driver_arriving',
                         'in_progress','completed','cancelled'),
    pickup_lat      DECIMAL(10,8),
    pickup_lng      DECIMAL(11,8),
    dropoff_lat     DECIMAL(10,8),
    dropoff_lng     DECIMAL(11,8),
    pickup_address  TEXT,
    dropoff_address TEXT,
    fare_amount     DECIMAL(10,2),
    surge_multiplier DECIMAL(4,2) DEFAULT 1.0,
    distance_km     DECIMAL(6,2),
    duration_secs   INT,
    car_type        VARCHAR(20),
    requested_at    TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    started_at      TIMESTAMP,
    completed_at    TIMESTAMP
);
```

### Driver Location History (Cassandra)
```sql
CREATE TABLE driver_locations (
    driver_id   BIGINT,
    recorded_at TIMESTAMP,
    lat         DECIMAL(10,8),
    lng         DECIMAL(11,8),
    speed_kmh   FLOAT,
    bearing     FLOAT,
    trip_id     VARCHAR(36),
    PRIMARY KEY (driver_id, recorded_at)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (recorded_at DESC);
```

## 11. Scalability & Edge Cases

- **Location service bottleneck**: 100K updates/sec to Redis. Solution: Redis Cluster, partition by geohash prefix. Each shard handles subset of geographic areas.
- **No drivers nearby**: expand search radius incrementally (2km → 5km → 10km). Or queue request and retry.
- **Driver doesn't respond to offer**: timeout after 15 seconds, offer to next driver.
- **Trip in progress, location update gaps**: client reconnects, GPS interpolation for gaps.
- **Surge during trip**: fare locked at booking-time surge multiplier.
- **Multi-city**: different geohash indexes per city (isolated). No cross-city matching.

## 12. Monitoring
- Match success rate: % of requests successfully matched in <5 min
- Match latency: time from request to driver assigned (p99 target: <5s)
- Driver ETA accuracy: predicted vs actual pickup time
- Driver location update freshness (p99: <10s)
- Surge pricing accuracy: supply/demand prediction vs actual
- Payment success rate
- Trip completion rate (vs cancellation)

quizBank.question: Why is Euclidean (straight-line) distance insufficient for ETA calculation in a ride-sharing app?
options: It ignores roads, traffic, one-way streets — actual driving distance is always longer than straight-line
It doesn't work with GPS coordinates
It can only calculate distances under 10km
quizBank.answer: It ignores roads, traffic, one-way streets — actual driving distance is always longer than straight-line distance
quizBank.explanation: Two points 1km apart may require 3km of driving due to roads, barriers, traffic patterns. ETA based on Euclidean distance would be significantly underestimated. Road-network routing (Dijkstra on road graph) gives accurate driving distance and time.

quizBank.question: Why must neighboring geohash cells also be queried when searching for nearby drivers?
options: A driver just outside the primary cell boundary would be missed without neighbor queries
quizBank.answer: A driver just outside the primary cell boundary would be missed without neighbor queries
quizBank.explanation: Geohash cells have sharp boundaries. A rider at cell center and a driver 100m away but in an adjacent cell would not match without neighbor queries. Query the 8 surrounding cells to handle boundary cases. Then filter by actual distance using haversine formula.

quizBank.question: A driver's phone GPS has a 30-second outage. What happens to ongoing trip tracking?
quizBank.answer: GPS outage handling: (1) Last known position retained in Redis for driver. (2) Rider app: interpolate driver position based on last known position + bearing + speed. Show estimated position. (3) After 30 seconds: show 'signal lost' indicator on rider map. (4) Driver app: on GPS recovery, send buffered location updates. Redis updated to current position. (5) Rider map: snap to real position on recovery. (6) Matching: during GPS outage, driver removed from available pool (no location update > 30s = stale). Prevents wrong matching. (7) Trip continues: GPS outage mid-trip doesn't cancel trip. Driver reconnects, continues.

quizBank.question: What Redis data structure is used to store driver locations by geographic cell?
quizBank.answer: Hash (geohash key → driver data)
quizBank.explanation: Redis Hash: HSET drivers:{geohash6} driver_id JSON_payload. HGETALL drivers:u4pruyd returns all drivers in that cell. HDEL removes driver when they leave cell or go offline. Fast O(1) operations. Alternative: Redis GEO commands (GEOADD, GEORADIUS) which use sorted sets internally — more elegant but similar performance.

quizBank.question: How does surge pricing work and how do you ensure it's fair to riders?
quizBank.answer: Surge pricing (dynamic pricing): price = base_fare * surge_multiplier. Surge driven by supply/demand imbalance in geographic area. Supply = available drivers. Demand = ride requests per minute. Low supply or high demand → high multiplier. Fairness: (1) Show multiplier clearly BEFORE confirmation. (2) Require explicit acceptance (Uber's pin entry for high surge). (3) Log surge_multiplier at booking time → fare doesn't increase mid-trip even if surge increases. (4) Cap: maximum multiplier limit (e.g., 8x). (5) Transparency: explain why surge is happening ('High demand due to event nearby'). (6) Cooling off: if rider waits, surge may decrease (alerts when it drops). Legality: some jurisdictions regulate surge pricing during emergencies — must disable.

quizBank.question: The system matches 10 drivers to the same rider request due to a bug. How do you prevent this?
quizBank.answer: Race condition: multiple matching service instances all see the same rider request and simultaneously offer to multiple drivers. Prevention: (1) Distributed lock: before sending offers, acquire Redis lock: SET ride_request:{request_id}:matching NX EX 30. Only one instance gets the lock and proceeds with matching. (2) Idempotent request: request has unique request_id. Matching service records request_id in DB before offering. Other instances check if request already being processed. (3) State machine: ride request starts as 'pending'. Atomic CAS: UPDATE requests SET status='matching' WHERE status='pending' AND id=?. Only succeeds once. (4) Kafka consumer group: single partition for a ride request → single consumer processes it.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a rider requesting a ride in a very dense area (airport) with 100 simultaneous requests?
quizBank.answer: Airport surge scenario: 100 riders, 10 drivers. All 100 request rides simultaneously. Matching service must fairly allocate 10 drivers. Approach: (1) Queue-based matching: queue all requests, process FIFO. First 10 requests get matched. Others notified: 'waiting for driver'. (2) Price-based: highest surge multiplier selection (market clearing). Controversial — effectively prioritizes wealthier riders. (3) Time-ordered + priority: Uber Comfort/Black get priority pool. (4) Driver attraction: surge pricing signal attracts more drivers to airport. (5) Pre-positioning: ML predicts flight arrivals, incentivizes drivers to go to airport before demand spike. (6) Pool rides: reduce individual driver need by combining riders going same direction.

quizBank.question: How do you scale the location service to handle 500,000 driver updates per second?
options: Single Redis server with fast hardware
Redis Cluster partitioned by geohash prefix, with each shard handling a geographic region
Store all locations in a SQL database with geospatial index
quizBank.answer: Redis Cluster partitioned by geohash prefix, with each shard handling a geographic region
quizBank.explanation: 500K updates/sec on single Redis: possible (Redis handles 1M ops/sec) but risky (single point of failure, memory limits). Redis Cluster: partition by geohash prefix (first 2-3 chars). North America on shard 1, Europe on shard 2, etc. Each shard handles ~100K updates/sec. Geographic partitioning: matching queries for one city don't cross shards.

quizBank.question: What communication protocol is used to push driver location updates to the rider app in real time?
quizBank.answer: WebSocket bidirectional connection
quizBank.explanation: WebSocket: persistent bidirectional connection. Server pushes driver location updates to rider without rider polling. Low latency (sub-100ms), low overhead (no HTTP headers per update). Rider app receives new driver lat/lng every 5 seconds (when driver sends update) → updates map marker animation.

quizBank.question: How do you detect and prevent GPS spoofing (a driver faking their location)?
quizBank.answer: GPS spoofing: driver uses app to fake GPS coordinates (e.g., appear at pickup while actually elsewhere). Detection: (1) Speed analysis: if driver's reported location jumps 5km in 1 second → physically impossible → flag. (2) Consistency check: driver's path should follow valid roads (road-snap check). If location is in middle of a river → flag. (3) Trip distance vs reported path: actual distance driven (odometer) vs GPS path length. Significant discrepancy → flag. (4) Device sensor fusion: accelerometer + gyroscope data should correlate with GPS movement. (5) ML model: trained on known spoofer patterns. (6) Action: flag for human review, suspend driver on confirmed spoofing. Consequence: driver charged for actual distance, not spoofed distance.

quizBank.question: How does the app show an accurate ETA for driver arrival that updates in real time?
quizBank.answer: Initial ETA: at match time, calculate route from driver's current location to rider's pickup. OSRM/Mapbox returns duration_seconds. Display 'X minutes away'. Updates: every 30 seconds (or every driver location update), recalculate remaining route from driver's latest GPS position. If driver is stuck in traffic: ETA increases. If driver takes faster route: ETA decreases. Rider app receives ETA updates via WebSocket. Map shows route overlay — rider sees driver position on route. Accuracy: compare predicted vs actual arrival times historically. Use ML to apply correction factor per region/time-of-day. Edge case: driver takes wrong turn → reroute, recalculate ETA. Notification: '2 minutes away' push notification triggers when ETA < 2 minutes.

quizBank.question: A driver rejects a ride offer. What happens next?
options: System waits for driver to reconsider
System offers to next best matching driver, with a 15-second timeout
quizBank.answer: System offers to next best matching driver, with a 15-second timeout
quizBank.explanation: Rejection cascade: (1) Driver rejects → mark driver as 'unavailable' for this request. (2) Next driver in ranked list receives offer. (3) 15-second timeout: if driver doesn't respond, treat as reject, move to next. (4) Track rejection rate: drivers who frequently reject face lower match priority. (5) After exhausting all nearby drivers: expand search radius, retry with new set. (6) If no match in 5 minutes: notify rider, suggest trying again.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'share my trip' safety feature that lets a rider share live location with a contact?
quizBank.answer: Share trip feature: rider generates shareable trip link. Contact opens link → sees real-time map with rider's location and driver info. Architecture: (1) Generate unique share_token (UUID) for trip. (2) Store token → trip_id mapping in Redis with TTL = trip duration + 2 hours. (3) Contact opens link → web page with map. Connects to WebSocket using share_token. (4) Track Service: authenticates share_token, subscribes to rider's trip location stream. (5) Rider location = driver's current location (they're in the same car after pickup). (6) Show: driver name, car info, real-time position on map, ETA to destination. (7) Terminate: link expires when trip completes. Privacy: share_token is opaque, doesn't expose personal info. Rider controls sharing (can revoke).

quizBank.question: How would you design the ML system for proactive driver positioning (predicting where demand will be)?
quizBank.answer: Demand prediction model: forecast ride demand per geographic cell (geohash/H3) per 15-minute time slot. Features: day of week, time of day, weather, local events (concerts, sports), historical demand patterns, holidays. Training: historical trip request data, labeled by origin geohash and time. Model: gradient boosting (LightGBM) or LSTM for time-series patterns. Output: demand heatmap for next 30 minutes. Driver incentives: show predicted surge areas on driver app. 'Head toward downtown — predicted surge in 15 minutes.' Surge pricing pre-announcement: adjust surge earlier when demand predicted. Evaluation: predicted demand vs actual demand per cell. Business impact: reduced wait times, higher driver earnings. Retrain: weekly with new data, deploy via ML pipeline.

quizBank.question: What geospatial precision (geohash length) should be used for finding drivers within 2km?
quizBank.answer: Length 6 (~1.2km x 0.6km)
quizBank.explanation: Geohash length 6 covers ~1.2km x 0.6km area. For 2km radius: query primary cell + 8 neighbors ≈ 3.6km x 1.8km coverage. Then filter by exact haversine distance ≤ 2km. Length 8 is too precise (must query too many cells for 2km). Length 4 is too coarse (returns drivers 20km away — too many to evaluate).

quizBank.question: How do you handle a trip that goes through a tunnel with no GPS signal?
quizBank.answer: Tunnel handling: driver loses GPS signal. (1) Dead reckoning: use last known position + speed + bearing + time elapsed to estimate current position. Accelerometer/gyroscope data helps maintain accuracy. (2) Map matching: match estimated position to known tunnel geometry. Known tunnels in map database — snap position to tunnel path. (3) Rider map: show car moving through tunnel at estimated speed. No jarring jump. (4) On exit: GPS reacquires. Update to actual position. Correct any accumulated dead-reckoning error. (5) Distance calculation: use road distance (from route geometry) not GPS track for fare computation — GPS gaps don't undercount distance. (6) ETA: recalculate on GPS recovery.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between H3 and Geohash for geospatial indexing? When would you use each?
quizBank.answer: Geohash: encodes lat/lng to base32 string. Rectangular grid. Simple, widely supported. Issues: cells are rectangles (different size at different latitudes), neighbor queries slightly complex (8 neighbors, but shape varies). Use for: simple nearby search, most ride-sharing use cases. H3 (Uber's hexagonal grid): divides earth into hexagonal cells. Advantages: (1) Hexagons have equal distance to all 6 neighbors (no diagonal vs edge issue). (2) More uniform cell sizes. (3) Hierarchical: zoom levels 0-15. (4) Better for hexagonal neighbor queries (only 6 neighbors, all equidistant). Use for: demand heatmaps, surge pricing visualization (hexagons look better on maps), advanced spatial analytics. Production: Uber uses H3, Lyft uses S2, most others use Geohash. All work well for basic proximity search.

quizBank.question: How does the system ensure a driver is charged the correct distance even if their GPS has gaps?
quizBank.answer: Use road-snap and interpolation for gaps, compare to road-network distance of planned route
options: Accept whatever GPS distance is reported
Use road-snap and interpolation for gaps, compare to road-network distance of planned route
Ask driver manually at trip end
quizBank.explanation: GPS gap handling: snap recorded GPS points to road network. Interpolate missing sections using road geometry. Compare GPS-computed distance to planned route distance. If GPS shows much shorter than planned: likely GPS gaps. Use planned route distance as floor. Driver incentive to take shortest route maintained. Anti-fraud: if GPS distance >> planned route: flag for review (driver took detour).

quizBank.question: How would you design a pool/shared ride matching system where two riders going the same direction share a car?
quizBank.answer: Pool matching complexity: must find two riders with similar origin AND destination within time and detour constraints. Algorithm: (1) Rider A requests pool → enter pool queue. (2) For rider B's request: find pool requests where route from B's pickup, through A's pickup, to A's destination, to B's destination is feasible (total detour < max_detour_minutes). (3) Calculate: route AB = pickup_B → pickup_A → dropoff_A → dropoff_B. If total_time(AB) < individual_times * threshold: match. (4) Driver assignment: single driver for combined route. (5) ETA shown: includes detour time for each rider. Complexity: matching is computationally expensive. Practical: only try to match within 2-minute window. Use spatial pre-filtering before route computation. Limit: match at most 2 riders per vehicle (Pool, not Bus).

quizBank.question: How do you build the driver rating system to prevent gaming?
quizBank.answer: Rating system: after each trip, rider rates driver (1-5 stars), driver rates rider (1-5 stars). Gaming prevention: (1) Mandatory rating: must rate before booking next trip (reduces skipping). (2) Mutual rating delay: both ratings submitted before either sees the other (prevents retaliation). (3) Threshold for action: driver suspended only if average < 4.0 AND minimum N ratings. (4) Context-aware: very short ratings window (1 day) heavily discounted vs established pattern. (5) Outlier detection: if driver suddenly drops from 4.9 to 2.0: flag for review. Could be unfair ratings. (6) Appeal process: drivers can flag unfair 1-star ratings. (7) Display: show rolling 6-month average, not all-time, to allow recovery. (8) Geographic normalization: some regions consistently rate lower — adjust baseline by city.

quizBank.question: What database is most appropriate for storing driver location history for audit and analytics purposes?
options: Cassandra partitioned by driver_id, clustered by timestamp
quizBank.answer: Cassandra partitioned by driver_id, clustered by timestamp
quizBank.explanation: Location history is time-series, append-only. Access patterns: 'get all locations for driver X during trip Y' (range query by time). Cassandra partition by driver_id: all driver's history on same node. Cluster by recorded_at DESC: range scans by time. High write throughput (100K updates/sec). TTL: auto-delete history older than 1 year. Cassandra excels at exactly this pattern.

quizBank.question: A major event (concert) ends and 50,000 people simultaneously request rides. How does the system handle this?
quizBank.answer: Concert end event: 50,000 ride requests in 60 seconds. System stress: (1) Request queue: API layer queues requests, prevents overload on matching service. (2) Auto-scaling: K8s scales matching service instances based on queue depth. (3) Surge pricing: extreme surge multiplier. Riders see '3x surge'. Many cancel or choose cheaper alternatives. Demand self-regulates. (4) Driver attraction: drivers see high surge zone on app, drive to venue. (5) Staggered release: show 'estimating wait time' while system catches up. (6) Pool optimization: aggressively match pool rides for same destination (all going downtown). (7) Capacity: position extra drivers near venue 15 minutes before event end (demand prediction). (8) Graceful degradation: if queue too long, show estimated wait time rather than failing immediately.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Ride-Sharing Service (Uber/Lyft)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design a Social Media Platform (Instagram)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- User profiles: bio, profile photo, follower/following counts
- Image/video upload and display
- Feed: posts from followed accounts, ranked
- Stories: 24-hour ephemeral content
- Explore/Discovery: find new content and users
- Hashtags: browse content by tag
- Likes, comments, shares
- Direct Messages (DM)
- Notifications

### Non-Functional Requirements
- High availability: 99.99%
- Low latency: feed load <200ms, image display <1s
- Scale: 2B MAU, 100M photos/day uploaded, 4.2B likes/day
- Durability: photos and videos stored permanently

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- Photo uploads: 100M/day = ~1,160/sec
- Photo size (compressed): avg 500KB → 100M x 500KB = 50TB/day
- Video: assume 10% of uploads are videos, 5MB avg → 10M x 5MB = 50TB/day
- Total storage: 100TB/day = 36.5PB/year (need tiered storage)
- Feed reads: 2B users x 5 feed loads/day = 10B/day = ~116,000/sec
- Likes: 4.2B/day = ~49,000/sec
- Following graph: 2B users x avg 200 follows = 400B edges

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([Mobile/Web Client])
    CDN[CDN - CloudFront]
    AG[API Gateway]
    US[Upload Service]
    FS[Feed Service]
    PS[Profile Service]
    StS[Stories Service]
    EX[Explore Service]
    DM[DM Service]
    NS[Notification Service]
    IG[Image Processing Service]
    GS[Graph Service - Follower/Following]
    MediaStore[S3 - Media Storage]
    FCache[Redis - Feed Cache]
    PDB[(Profile DB - PostgreSQL)]
    GDB[(Graph DB - Cassandra)]
    MDB[(Media Metadata - Cassandra)]
    Kafka[Event Bus]

    Client --> CDN
    CDN --> AG
    AG --> US
    AG --> FS
    AG --> PS
    AG --> StS
    AG --> EX
    AG --> DM
    US --> IG
    IG --> MediaStore
    IG --> MDB
    IG --> Kafka
    Kafka --> FS
    Kafka --> NS
    FS --> FCache
    FS --> GS
    GS --> GDB
    MediaStore --> CDN
```

## 4. Image Upload & Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant US as Upload Service
    participant S3 as Object Store
    participant IP as Image Processor
    participant CDN
    participant Kafka

    U->>US: POST /upload (multipart or presigned URL request)
    US-->>U: Presigned S3 URL + media_id
    U->>S3: PUT image directly (bypasses servers)
    S3->>IP: S3 trigger event
    IP->>IP: Resize to multiple sizes (thumb, medium, large)
    IP->>IP: Apply filters if requested
    IP->>IP: Generate perceptual hash (for dedup / moderation)
    IP->>S3: Store all variants
    IP->>Kafka: media_ready event
    Kafka->>US: Update post status: published
    US->>CDN: Warm CDN for popular content
    US-->>U: Post published notification
```

### Image Variants Generated
```
Original:    Stored as-is (for download)
Large:       1080px width (feed display)
Medium:      640px width (profile grid)
Thumbnail:   150x150px (search results, comments)
Story:       Optimized for 9:16 aspect ratio
```

## 5. Follow Graph

### Data Model (Cassandra)
```sql
-- Who does user X follow?
CREATE TABLE following (
    follower_id   BIGINT,
    followee_id   BIGINT,
    created_at    TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id)
);

-- Who follows user X? (for fan-out)
CREATE TABLE followers (
    followee_id   BIGINT,
    follower_id   BIGINT,
    created_at    TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (followee_id, follower_id)
);
```

### Scaling the Graph
- 400B edges: Cassandra handles with partition by user_id
- Celebrity problem: Selena Gomez has 400M followers
- Fan-out on write: would create 400M feed updates per post
- Solution: hybrid fan-out (same as news feed problem)

## 6. Feed Generation

### Hybrid Fan-out Strategy
- Normal users (<100K followers): fan-out on write
- Celebrity (>100K followers): fan-out on read
- Inactive users (>30 days): no fan-out, compute on next login

### Feed Ranking ML Pipeline
```
Signals:
- Affinity: how much does this user interact with poster?
- Recency: newer posts scored higher
- Content type: video > carousel > single image
- Engagement velocity: fast-growing likes boost visibility
- User interests: topics user engages with

Pipeline:
1. Candidate generation: 1000 posts from feed cache
2. ML ranking: score each candidate
3. Diversity filter: max 3 consecutive posts from same user
4. Ad insertion: every 5th post
5. Return top 20 posts
```

## 7. Stories Architecture

### Ephemeral Design
- Stories expire after 24 hours
- Separate from main feed (different UX)
- Viewed story tracked per user
- Story ring: avatars of friends who posted stories

### Data Model
```sql
CREATE TABLE stories (
    story_id    UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id     BIGINT,
    media_url   TEXT,
    story_type  TEXT,  -- image, video, boomerang
    duration_ms INT,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP,
    expires_at  TIMESTAMP,  -- created_at + 24 hours
    view_count  INT DEFAULT 0
) WITH default_time_to_live = 86400;  -- Cassandra TTL: 24 hours

CREATE TABLE story_views (
    story_id   UUID,
    viewer_id  BIGINT,
    viewed_at  TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (story_id, viewer_id)
) WITH default_time_to_live = 86400;
```

### Story Ring Generation
- Fetch: who does user follow? (from followers table)
- For each followee: any active stories (created_at > now - 24h)?
- Sorted by: unviewed first, then by recency
- Cache: store story ring in Redis with 1-hour TTL

## 8. Explore / Discovery

### How Instagram Explore Works
- Collaborative filtering: users with similar tastes like same posts
- Identify 'seeds' (posts user recently engaged with)
- Find other users who also engaged with seeds → see what else they like
- Content-based: posts with similar visual features (ML embedding)
- Trending: posts with fast engagement velocity in user's interest graph
- Location-based: posts near user's location

### Hashtag System
```sql
CREATE TABLE hashtag_posts (
    hashtag    TEXT,
    post_id    BIGINT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (hashtag, created_at, post_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (created_at DESC);

-- Query: latest posts with #sunset
SELECT post_id FROM hashtag_posts
WHERE hashtag = 'sunset'
AND created_at > (now - 7 days)
ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50;
```

## 9. API Design

```
Media:
POST /api/v1/media/upload/initiate   — get presigned URL + media_id
POST /api/v1/posts                   — create post with media_ids
GET  /api/v1/posts/{post_id}         — post details
DEL  /api/v1/posts/{post_id}         — delete post

Feed:
GET  /api/v1/feed?cursor={post_id}&limit=20  — paginated feed
GET  /api/v1/users/{id}/posts        — user's profile grid
GET  /api/v1/explore                 — explore feed
GET  /api/v1/hashtags/{tag}          — hashtag feed

Social:
POST /api/v1/posts/{id}/like         — like/unlike
POST /api/v1/users/{id}/follow       — follow/unfollow
GET  /api/v1/users/{id}/followers    — follower list
GET  /api/v1/users/{id}/following    — following list

Stories:
POST /api/v1/stories                 — create story
GET  /api/v1/stories/feed            — story ring
POST /api/v1/stories/{id}/view       — mark as viewed
```

## 10. Like Counter at Scale

- 49,000 likes/sec: too many to write directly to post DB
- Redis INCR: like:{post_id} → atomic in-memory counter
- Batch write to DB every 5 seconds
- Display: read from Redis (real-time count)
- Idempotency: maintain SET liked_by:{post_id} per user → prevent double-like
- Problem: liked_by set for viral post (10M likes) → 10M entries in Redis set
- Solution: store likes in Cassandra, check with Bloom filter for quick 'did I like this?' check

## 11. Media Storage & CDN

- Object store: S3 (origin)
- CDN: Akamai/CloudFront (edge delivery)
- Image URL structure: `cdn.instagram.com/{user_id}/{media_id}/{size}.jpg`
- Cache TTL: images immutable → 1 year
- Stories: shorter TTL (24h) since they expire
- Private accounts: signed URLs with expiry
- Deduplication: perceptual hash (pHash) → detect near-identical uploads

## 12. Monitoring
- Feed load latency p99 (target <200ms)
- Image upload success rate
- Story TTL cleanup job lag
- Fan-out queue depth (Kafka lag)
- CDN hit rate for media
- Like/comment event processing latency
- Explore recommendation CTR

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows Instagram as photo sharing app. Can describe upload → share → view flow.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Designs upload pipeline with S3. Knows about CDN for image delivery. Can describe follow graph.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Designs image processing pipeline, hybrid fan-out feed, Redis like counters, stories with TTL, hashtag system.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Implements explore/discovery system, ML feed ranking, story ring with view tracking, DM system, content moderation.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs multi-region photo storage, visual search (search by photo), reels recommendation, creator monetization, and GDPR data handling.
