reviewQuestions: Explain Requirements & Scale in simple terms to a non-technical person:::Requirements & Scale is a fundamental concept in Design: Social Media Platform (Instagram). At its core, it solves the problem of [specific challenge] by [approach]. Think of it like [real-world analogy].

What are the trade-offs of using Requirements & Scale?:::Every approach has trade-offs. Requirements & Scale offers [advantages] but comes with [disadvantages]. In an interview, always discuss both sides and when you would choose alternatives.

How would you implement Requirements & Scale in a production system?:::In production, you need to consider: 1) Scalability — how does it handle growth? 2) Fault tolerance — what happens when components fail? 3) Monitoring — how do you observe its health? 4) Testing — how do you verify correctness?

quizBank.question: Why are multiple image size variants (thumbnail, medium, large) generated for each uploaded photo?
options: Different screen sizes and contexts need different resolutions to save bandwidth and improve performance
quizBank.answer: Different screen sizes and contexts need different resolutions to save bandwidth and improve performance
quizBank.explanation: Profile grid: 150px thumbnails. Feed: 640px medium. Full view: 1080px. Sending 1080px everywhere wastes bandwidth on profile grid (3 images visible = 3x1080px = wasteful). Sending 150px everywhere: blurry in full view. Optimizing by context: reduces page load by 5-10x.

quizBank.question: How does Instagram handle the 'celebrity problem' where Selena Gomez (400M followers) posts a photo?
options: Fan-out on write to all 400M followers
Store post once; celebrities' posts fetched on read; normal users get fan-out on write
Delay celebrity posts until off-peak hours
quizBank.answer: Store post once; celebrities' posts fetched on read; normal users get fan-out on write
quizBank.explanation: Hybrid fan-out: celebrities (>100K followers) use fan-out on read. When user opens feed: normal followees' posts come from pre-built feed cache. Celebrity's posts fetched from their post timeline and merged. This avoids 400M fan-out operations per celebrity post.

quizBank.question: How are Instagram Stories automatically deleted after 24 hours?
options: Background job scans and deletes expired stories every minute
Cassandra default_time_to_live (TTL) automatically expires rows after 24 hours
Stories are moved to archive after 24 hours
quizBank.answer: Cassandra default_time_to_live (TTL) automatically expires rows after 24 hours
quizBank.explanation: Cassandra TTL: set default_time_to_live = 86400 (seconds) on stories table. Rows automatically deleted after 24 hours with no application logic needed. Efficient: Cassandra handles TTL cleanup during compaction. CDN cache for story media: set max-age=86400 so cached media also expires naturally.

quizBank.question: A photo uploaded by a user is found to violate content policy 1 hour after posting. How do you handle this?
quizBank.answer: Immediate response: (1) Soft delete: mark post as is_deleted=true in post DB. Feed service filters deleted posts. (2) CDN purge: proactively purge CDN cache for all image variants (expensive but necessary for policy violations). (3) Object store: media files remain (for audit/legal) but access blocked via signed URL invalidation or storage policy. (4) User notification: inform user of policy violation, link to policy. (5) Appeal: user can appeal within 30 days. (6) Hash blacklist: add perceptual hash of image to blocklist — prevent re-upload. (7) Account action: first offense warning, repeated violations → suspension.

quizBank.question: How do you implement the 'story ring' — showing circles of friends who have active stories?
quizBank.answer: Story ring generation: (1) Fetch user's following list from graph DB (Cassandra following table). (2) For each followee: check if they have active stories (created_at > now - 24h). Query: SELECT story_id FROM stories WHERE user_id=? AND expires_at > now(). (3) Sorting: unviewed stories first (check story_views table for viewer_id=current_user). Then by most recent story. (4) Avatars: profile photo from user profile cache. (5) Caching: store story ring result in Redis per user. TTL: 5 minutes (acceptable staleness). On follow/unfollow: invalidate cache. (6) Real-time: when someone posts a new story, push notification to followers → invalidate story ring cache.

quizBank.question: How is the like count for a photo stored and retrieved at 49,000 likes/sec?
options: Directly in SQL posts table, updated per like
Redis INCR for fast atomic increment, batch-written to SQL DB every 5 seconds
Counted by querying likes table at read time
Stored in Elasticsearch for fast aggregation
quizBank.answer: Redis INCR for fast atomic increment, batch-written to SQL DB every 5 seconds
quizBank.explanation: 49K likes/sec directly to MySQL: overloads single row. Redis INCR like:{post_id}: atomic, in-memory, handles 100K+ ops/sec. Background job reads Redis counters every 5 seconds, batch-writes to MySQL. Display: read from Redis (live count). DB is eventual source of truth (5s lag acceptable). Viral post: only Redis counter hot, not DB.

quizBank.question: How do you detect and prevent upload of child sexual abuse material (CSAM)?
quizBank.answer: CSAM detection is both legal requirement and ethical imperative. Multi-layer: (1) PhotoDNA (Microsoft): perceptual hash matching against known CSAM database (maintained by NCMEC). Every uploaded image hashed and compared. Match = immediate block, no human review needed. (2) ML classifier: detect new/unknown CSAM that isn't in hash database. Computer vision model trained to detect content. (3) User reports: report mechanism → immediate human review queue. Content reviewed within hours. (4) NCMEC reporting: legally required to report confirmed CSAM to NCMEC within 24 hours. Provide user data to law enforcement. (5) Account suspension: immediate permanent ban for any confirmed CSAM upload. (6) Data retention: preserve evidence for law enforcement. Never retain CSAM on servers — hash only.

quizBank.question: How would you design the 'close friends' feature where stories are shared with a select group?
quizBank.answer: Close friends list: user maintains a close_friends list (subset of following). Stories marked as close_friends_only=true. Access control: (1) Story ring: only show close-friends-only story ring to users in close_friends list. (2) Story viewer list: only close friends can view the story. (3) Backend: when fetching story ring: check if current user is in story poster's close friends list. SELECT 1 FROM close_friends WHERE user_id=poster AND friend_id=viewer. (4) Data model: close_friends table (user_id, friend_id). Partitioned by user_id. (5) Privacy: story URLs must be signed — non-close-friends can't access even with URL. (6) Notifications: close friends story has green ring indicator (different from regular story).

quizBank.question: How do you store the follower graph for 2 billion users with an average of 200 follows each?
options: Two Cassandra tables: following (follower_id → followees) and followers (followee_id → followers)
Redis sorted sets for all edges
quizBank.answer: Two Cassandra tables: following (follower_id → followees) and followers (followee_id → followers)
quizBank.explanation: 400B edges total. Two complementary tables: (1) following: partition by follower_id → 'who does user X follow?' for feed generation. (2) followers: partition by followee_id → 'who follows user Y?' for fan-out notifications. Denormalized for query efficiency — each follow creates 2 rows. Cassandra handles 400B rows across nodes. Alternative: MySQL is too slow for full table scans at this scale.

quizBank.question: How would you design the Instagram Explore page recommendation system?
quizBank.answer: Multi-signal collaborative filtering: (1) Seed selection: pick user's recent high-engagement posts (liked, saved, commented). (2) User-user similarity: find users who engaged with same seeds. These are 'taste twins'. (3) Candidate generation: what else do taste twins engage with? Those posts are candidates. (4) Content-based: ML visual embeddings of posts user liked → find visually similar posts (CLIP model). (5) Ranking: ML model scores candidates. Features: affinity to author, content quality (engagement rate), diversity (topic mix), freshness. (6) Filtering: remove already-seen posts, blocked users, low-quality content. (7) Diversity: max 3 posts from same creator. (8) Infrastructure: offline training (Spark), daily candidate generation, online ranking per request. (9) Personalization: separate explore tab per user, computed from their unique engagement history.

quizBank.question: What is a perceptual hash (pHash) used for in image upload processing?
options: To encrypt the image for storage
To detect near-duplicate or visually similar images (same image re-uploaded or slightly modified)
To compress the image for CDN delivery
To categorize image by color palette
quizBank.answer: To detect near-duplicate or visually similar images (same image re-uploaded or slightly modified)
quizBank.explanation: pHash: generates a 64-bit fingerprint based on image visual content. Similar images produce similar hashes (small Hamming distance). Use cases: (1) Deduplication: don't store same image twice. (2) Copyright detection: artist's image reposted. (3) CSAM detection: PhotoDNA uses pHash to match known illegal content. (4) Content moderation: blocklisted images can be detected even after cropping or filter application.

quizBank.question: A user with 10M followers posts a photo. How does it appear in followers' feeds?
quizBank.answer: Hybrid fan-out: 10M followers exceeds normal user threshold (say 100K). Handle as celebrity. Post creation: (1) Write post to post DB (single write). (2) Publish post_created event to Kafka. (3) Fan-out worker: for celebrity posts, skip fan-out to followers' feed caches. (4) Feed service: when follower requests feed — fetch their pre-built feed cache (from normal followees) + celebrity posts (fetched on-demand from post DB). (5) Merge and rank: celebrity's posts merged with feed cache, ML ranking applied. (6) Caching celebrity's post: celebrity's timeline cached in Redis (recent 500 posts) → read is still fast. (7) Result: follower sees celebrity's photo within a few seconds of posting.

quizBank.question: Why does Instagram use Cassandra for storing media metadata instead of MySQL?
quizBank.answer: Media metadata (post table) access patterns: insert on upload (high write volume: 1,160/sec), lookup by post_id (key-value), lookup by user_id (user's profile grid). Scale: 100M posts/day, years of history = billions of rows. Cassandra advantages: (1) Linear horizontal scalability — add nodes to handle more throughput. (2) High write throughput — LSM-tree storage optimized for writes. (3) Key-value access pattern matches Cassandra's partition key model. (4) No complex joins needed — post data is self-contained. MySQL would require: careful sharding, complex cluster management, limited write throughput per shard. Cassandra limitations: no ad-hoc queries, no ACID across rows, schema must match access patterns.

quizBank.question: How do you implement 'suggested users to follow' based on social graph connections?
quizBank.answer: Friend-of-friend (FoF) algorithm: (1) Get user's current following list (set A). (2) For each user in A: get their following list. (3) Collect all users that A's followees follow (set B). Remove users already in A. Remove current user. (4) Count: how many of current user's followees follow each candidate in B. (5) Higher count = stronger suggestion (mutual connections). (6) Filter: remove blocked users, private accounts (without follow request). (7) Rank: FoF count + account type + content interest alignment. (8) Cache: compute suggestions daily, cache in Redis. Refresh on follow/unfollow. (9) Alternative signals: common hashtag engagement, similar profile (bio analysis), location-based (shared city). (10) ML enhancement: train model on which suggestions users actually follow → predict follow probability.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of cursor-based pagination in Instagram's feed vs offset pagination?
options: Cursor pagination is faster to implement
Cursor pagination is stable when new posts are added at top; offset can cause duplicates or missing posts
Cursor pagination requires less database storage
quizBank.answer: Cursor pagination is stable when new posts are added at top; offset can cause duplicates or missing posts
quizBank.explanation: Offset: LIMIT 20 OFFSET 20 → if new post inserted: page 2 includes last item of page 1 (duplicate) or skips first of old page 2. Cursor: anchor on last_seen_post_id → return posts where post_id < cursor. Stable regardless of new posts. Instagram feed: cursor = lowest post_id in current page. Next page: load more before that ID.

quizBank.question: How do you handle video upload and playback at scale (Reels)?
quizBank.answer: Video pipeline differs from photos: (1) Size: videos up to 90 seconds = ~50MB. Multipart upload to S3 (10MB chunks). (2) Transcoding: similar to YouTube — FFmpeg workers transcode to H.264 MP4. Multiple resolutions: 1080p, 720p, 480p. Multiple frame rates. (3) HLS output: segment into 2-second chunks for adaptive bitrate. (4) Thumbnail: extract cover frame. Allow user to choose. (5) CDN: video segments served from CDN. Long TTL (immutable). (6) Autoplay: load first 5 seconds while scrolling for smooth autoplay. (7) Audio: mute by default, user unmutes. (8) Transcription: captions generated via ASR for accessibility. (9) Recommendation: separate Reels recommendation model — optimize for watch-through rate, rewatches. (10) Analytics: track per-second viewer retention.

quizBank.question: How would you design the 'tagged in a photo' feature and make it searchable?
quizBank.answer: Tag creation: when user creates post, they can tag other users (search by username → select user_id). Tags stored in photo_tags table (post_id, tagged_user_id, x_position, y_position, created_at). Notification: tagged user gets notified immediately via notification service. Privacy: (1) Tagged user can remove themselves from tag (delete from photo_tags). (2) Private accounts: can restrict who can tag them (all, following only, nobody). (3) Approval required: option to review tags before they appear on profile. Searchability: (1) Reverse index: tagged_user_id → [post_ids]. Query for user's tagged photos. (2) Cache: list of tag_post_ids per user in Redis (recently tagged, up to 500). (3) Profile page: 'Tagged' tab shows all approved tags. (4) Index: Elasticsearch for full tag graph search.

quizBank.question: How does Instagram ensure images load quickly for users in slow network regions?
options: Send full resolution images with compression
Use CDN edge nodes globally + serve smallest usable resolution + progressive JPEG loading
Store images near each user's home region
Pre-download all feed images when on WiFi
quizBank.answer: Use CDN edge nodes globally + serve smallest usable resolution + progressive JPEG loading
quizBank.explanation: Multi-strategy: (1) CDN edge (200+ locations): serve from nearest POP, <10ms network latency. (2) Adaptive quality: detect connection speed, serve 480px instead of 1080px on slow connection. (3) Progressive JPEG: displays low-quality preview immediately, progressively sharpens as bytes arrive. (4) WebP format: 25-35% smaller than JPEG at same quality. (5) Lazy loading: images below viewport load only when scrolling approaches. (6) Pre-fetch: next 2-3 images in feed preloaded while reading current.

quizBank.question: A user deletes their Instagram account. What needs to happen to their data?
quizBank.answer: Account deletion pipeline: (1) Immediate: mark account as deleted/inactive. Remove from search results. Other users can't find them. (2) Content removal: posts, stories, comments → soft delete (hide from feed, mark is_deleted=true). (3) Follow relationships: remove from all followers' following lists, remove all their followers' graph entries. Kafka fan-out to update affected users' feed caches. (4) DMs: delete their messages (GDPR right to erasure). Recipient still has thread but sender's messages replaced with 'deleted message'. (5) Media files: S3 deletion scheduled (can take 30 days per GDPR). CDN purge. (6) Likes/comments on others' posts: anonymize or remove. (7) Data export: before deletion, provide data export (GDPR right to data portability). (8) Reactivation window: 30-day recovery period before permanent deletion. (9) Audit log: retain minimal log for legal compliance.

quizBank.question: How would you design a visual search feature ('search by image')?
quizBank.answer: Visual search: user uploads photo or long-presses any image in feed → find visually similar posts. Architecture: (1) Image embeddings: ML model (CLIP, EfficientNet) converts each image to 512-dim vector embedding. Computed at upload time, stored in vector DB. (2) Vector DB: FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) or Pinecone — optimized for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. (3) Query: user's image → embedding → ANN query in vector DB → top-K similar embeddings → post IDs. (4) Ranking: re-rank by combined score: visual similarity + engagement quality. (5) Scale: 100M posts/day × 512 floats = 200GB/day of embeddings. Use quantization (INT8) to reduce by 4x. (6) Index: FAISS IVF index for billion-scale ANN. Trade-off: approximate (may miss some similar images) vs exact (too slow). (7) Pipeline: upload → resize → embedding model → vector DB write (async).

quizBank.question: What technique prevents the same user from liking a photo multiple times?
options: Redis SET membership check before incrementing counter
Both A and B are valid approaches
quizBank.answer: Both A and B are valid approaches
quizBank.explanation: Two-phase approach: (1) Redis SET liked_by:{post_id}: SADD before INCR. SADD returns 0 if already member → skip increment. Fast, in-memory. (2) DB unique constraint: INSERT INTO likes (user_id, post_id) → unique constraint violation if duplicate. Two layers: Redis for speed, DB for truth. For large posts (10M likes): Redis set too large. Alternative: Cassandra likes table with (user_id, post_id) as PK → idempotent INSERT.

quizBank.question: How would you design the hashtag trending system for Instagram?
quizBank.answer: Trending hashtag pipeline: (1) Event collection: every post creation with hashtags → Kafka post_created event containing hashtag list. (2) Real-time aggregation: Kafka Streams or Flink: sliding window count per hashtag (last 60 minutes). (3) Baseline comparison: compare current count to same window last week. Trending_score = current_count / historical_baseline. (4) Velocity filter: require minimum absolute count (e.g., 1000 posts/hour) to trend. (5) Geographic trending: separate trending per country/city. Geo-tag of posts used. (6) Storage: trending_hashtags table in Redis sorted set: ZADD trending:{country} score hashtag. Top 30 per country. (7) Update frequency: recalculate every 5 minutes. (8) Display: Explore page 'Trending' section, hashtag search result header. (9) Manipulation prevention: rate limit new accounts' hashtag posts. Detect coordinated hashtag campaigns.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Social Media Platform (Instagram)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design a Payment System (Stripe/PayPal)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Accept payments: credit/debit card, bank transfer, wallet
- Payouts: transfer money to merchants/users
- Idempotent API: safe to retry without double-charging
- Webhook notifications: notify merchants of payment events
- Refunds: full and partial
- Fraud detection: block suspicious transactions
- Multi-currency support
- PCI DSS compliance
- Reconciliation: balance books at end of day

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Exactly-once processing: never charge twice, never miss a charge
- High availability: 99.999%
- Low latency: authorization <500ms, capture <2s
- Strong consistency: financial data must be accurate
- Auditability: full audit trail for every transaction
- Scale: 100M transactions/day = ~1,160/sec average, 10,000/sec peak

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- 100M transactions/day = 1,160/sec average
- Peak (Black Friday): 10,000/sec
- Transaction record: ~1KB
- 100M/day x 1KB = 100GB/day
- 5 years: 100GB x 365 x 5 = 182.5TB
- Ledger entries: 2 per transaction (debit + credit) = 200M entries/day
- Webhooks: 100M events/day = 1,160/sec

## 3. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Merchant([Merchant / Client])
    AG[API Gateway]
    PS[Payment Service]
    IS[Idempotency Service]
    FD[Fraud Detection Service]
    PG[Payment Gateway Adapter]
    LS[Ledger Service]
    WS[Webhook Service]
    RecS[Reconciliation Service]
    VisaNet[Visa / Mastercard Network]
    AcqBank[Acquiring Bank]
    DB[(Payment DB - PostgreSQL)]
    LDB[(Ledger DB - PostgreSQL Double-Entry)]
    Kafka[Event Bus]
    IdempDB[(Idempotency Store - Redis)]

    Merchant -->|POST /charges| AG
    AG --> PS
    PS --> IS
    IS --> IdempDB
    PS --> FD
    FD --> PS
    PS --> PG
    PG --> AcqBank
    AcqBank --> VisaNet
    VisaNet --> AcqBank
    AcqBank --> PG
    PG --> PS
    PS --> LS
    PS --> DB
    PS --> Kafka
    Kafka --> WS
    Kafka --> RecS
    WS --> Merchant
    LS --> LDB
```

## 4. Idempotency — The Core Guarantee

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Merchant
    participant PS as Payment Service
    participant IS as Idempotency Store Redis
    participant PG as Payment Gateway
    participant DB as Database

    M->>PS: POST /charges {idempotency_key: uuid-123, amount: 100}
    PS->>IS: GET idempotency:uuid-123
    IS-->>PS: null (first time)
    PS->>IS: SET idempotency:uuid-123 = PROCESSING EX 600
    PS->>FD: Fraud check
    FD-->>PS: PASS
    PS->>PG: Authorize $100
    PG-->>PS: authorized (charge_id: ch_abc)
    PS->>DB: INSERT payment (idempotency_key: uuid-123, charge_id: ch_abc)
    PS->>IS: SET idempotency:uuid-123 = {charge_id: ch_abc, status: success}
    PS-->>M: 200 {charge_id: ch_abc}

    Note over M,PS: Network timeout — Merchant retries
    M->>PS: POST /charges {idempotency_key: uuid-123, amount: 100}
    PS->>IS: GET idempotency:uuid-123
    IS-->>PS: {charge_id: ch_abc, status: success}
    PS-->>M: 200 {charge_id: ch_abc} (same response, no double charge)
```

### Idempotency Key Rules
- Client generates unique UUID per payment attempt
- Key stored with TTL (e.g., 24 hours)
- If key found: return cached response immediately
- If key in PROCESSING state and request times out: client retries, must wait for processing to complete
- Key must be included in payment form, not regenerated on retry

## 5. Double-Entry Bookkeeping (Ledger)

```
Every financial transaction creates exactly two ledger entries:
  Debit one account + Credit another account
  Sum of all debits = Sum of all credits (always balanced)

Example: Merchant receives $100 payment
  DEBIT  customer_payable:customer_123    $100  (customer owes us)
  CREDIT merchant_receivable:merchant_456 $100  (we owe merchant)

Example: Payout to merchant
  DEBIT  merchant_receivable:merchant_456 $100  (reduce what we owe)
  CREDIT bank_account:wells_fargo         $100  (money leaves bank)

Ledger DB schema:
CREATE TABLE ledger_entries (
    id              BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    transaction_id  UUID NOT NULL,
    account_id      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    entry_type      CHAR(1) NOT NULL,  -- D=Debit, C=Credit
    amount          BIGINT NOT NULL,   -- in cents (no floating point!)
    currency        CHAR(3) NOT NULL,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    description     TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_account ON ledger_entries (account_id, created_at DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_transaction ON ledger_entries (transaction_id);
```

### Why Store in Cents (Integer)?
- Floating point: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004 (incorrect!)
- Integer cents: 10 + 20 = 30 (exactly correct)
- Never use FLOAT or DOUBLE for money — use BIGINT cents or NUMERIC(19,4)

## 6. Payment Flow: Authorization and Capture

```
Two-phase payment (common for e-commerce):

Phase 1 - Authorization:
  Merchant → Acquiring Bank → Card Network → Issuing Bank
  Issuing Bank: check funds available, fraud, credit limit
  Response: authorized (hold placed on funds)
  Latency: 200-500ms

Phase 2 - Capture (typically at shipment):
  Merchant initiates capture (up to 7 days after auth)
  Funds transferred from customer to merchant
  If not captured: authorization expires

Single-step (Charge):
  Authorize + Capture in one step
  For immediate digital goods delivery

Refund flow:
  POST /refunds {charge_id: ch_abc, amount: 100 (optional)}
  Partial refund: amount < original charge
  Full refund: amount = original charge
  Creates reverse ledger entries
```

## 7. Fraud Detection

```
Real-time fraud signals (checked before authorization):
- Velocity: same card used >5 times in 1 minute
- Amount anomaly: unusual amount for merchant category
- Geographic: card used in two countries within 1 hour
- Device fingerprint: known bad device
- IP reputation: known fraud IP
- Card BIN: card from high-risk country
- 3DS authentication: require 3D Secure for high-risk

ML model:
- Features: above signals + historical behavior
- Output: fraud_score (0-1)
- Threshold: score > 0.7 → decline, 0.4-0.7 → 3DS challenge, <0.4 → approve
- Latency requirement: <50ms (must not add significant latency to checkout)

Post-authorization monitoring:
- Chargebacks: card holder disputes → chargeback process
- High chargeback rate merchant → suspend account
```

## 8. Webhook System

```
Webhook events published to Kafka:
  payment.succeeded
  payment.failed
  refund.created
  dispute.created
  payout.paid

Webhook Worker:
1. Consume from Kafka
2. Find merchant's webhook endpoints
3. POST event payload to merchant URL
4. Sign payload with HMAC-SHA256 (merchant verifies signature)
5. Expect 200 response within 30 seconds
6. On failure/timeout: retry with exponential backoff (5s, 30s, 2m, 10m, 30m)
7. After 5 retries: move to DLQ, alert merchant dashboard

Idempotency for webhooks:
- Include event_id in payload
- Merchant should: check event_id in their DB, skip if already processed

Security:
X-Stripe-Signature: t=timestamp,v1=hmac_signature
Merchant verifies: HMAC-SHA256(timestamp.payload, webhook_secret)
```

## 9. API Design

```
Payment Intents (Stripe-style):
POST /v1/payment_intents
Body: { amount: 2000, currency: "usd", payment_method: "pm_card_visa" }
Headers: Idempotency-Key: uuid-123
Response 200: { id: "pi_abc", status: "requires_confirmation", client_secret: "..." }

POST /v1/payment_intents/{id}/confirm
Response 200: { status: "succeeded", charge: { id: "ch_xyz", amount: 2000 } }

POST /v1/refunds
Body: { charge: "ch_xyz", amount: 1000 }  -- partial refund
Response 200: { id: "re_abc", status: "pending", amount: 1000 }

GET /v1/charges/{id}          — charge details
GET /v1/balance               — merchant balance
GET /v1/payouts               — payout history
POST /v1/webhooks             — register webhook endpoint

All requests: require API key in Authorization header
All amounts: in smallest currency unit (cents for USD)
```

## 10. Reconciliation

```
Daily reconciliation process:
1. Download settlement file from acquiring bank (all captured charges)
2. Compare to internal ledger:
   - For each bank entry: find matching internal payment record
   - Match by: amount, card last4, timestamp
3. Exceptions:
   - In bank but not internal: missed capture → investigate
   - In internal but not bank: fraud, timing issue → investigate
4. Adjustments:
   - Bank fees deducted → post fee entries to ledger
   - Chargebacks received → reverse ledger entries
5. Daily balance report: sum all ledger entries → verify equals bank balance
6. Tool: runs as nightly batch job (Apache Airflow)
```

## 11. PCI DSS Compliance

```
PCI DSS Level 1 requirements:
- Never store raw card numbers (PAN) in your systems
- Tokenization: card details entered in Stripe.js/Braintree hosted fields
  → never touch your servers → only token returned to you
- Encryption: TLS 1.2+ for all card data in transit
- Access control: minimal access to cardholder data, audit logs
- Vulnerability scanning: quarterly ASV scans
- Penetration testing: annual
- Network segmentation: payment processing isolated from rest of network
- Audit logs: all access to cardholder data environment logged
```

## 12. Data Model Summary

```sql
CREATE TABLE payments (
    payment_id      UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    merchant_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    customer_id     BIGINT,
    idempotency_key VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    amount          BIGINT NOT NULL,   -- cents
    currency        CHAR(3) NOT NULL,
    status          VARCHAR(30),       -- pending, authorized, captured, failed, refunded
    payment_method  VARCHAR(50),       -- card, bank_transfer, wallet
    gateway_ref     VARCHAR(100),      -- external charge ID from processor
    fraud_score     DECIMAL(4,3),
    failure_reason  TEXT,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    updated_at      TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE refunds (
    refund_id    UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    payment_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES payments(payment_id),
    amount       BIGINT NOT NULL,
    status       VARCHAR(20),   -- pending, succeeded, failed
    reason       TEXT,
    created_at   TIMESTAMP NOT NULL
);
```

## 13. Monitoring
- Payment success rate (target >99.5%)
- Authorization latency p99 (target <500ms)
- Fraud decline rate (balance: too high = friction, too low = fraud loss)
- Webhook delivery success rate and retry rate
- Reconciliation exception count (target: 0)
- Chargeback rate per merchant (alert if >1%)
- Ledger balance drift (must always be $0)

quizBank.question: What is an idempotency key in payment processing?
options: A key that encrypts payment data
A unique client-generated ID sent with each payment request to ensure retries don't cause duplicate charges
A key for PCI DSS tokenization
A key that identifies the payment gateway
quizBank.answer: A unique client-generated ID sent with each payment request to ensure retries don't cause duplicate charges
quizBank.explanation: Network timeouts cause merchants to retry. Without idempotency key: same payment processed twice → customer charged twice. With idempotency key: second request with same key returns cached response from first attempt. Stripe stores idempotency keys for 24 hours.

quizBank.question: Why should monetary amounts be stored as integers (cents) rather than floats?
options: Floating-point arithmetic has precision errors that cause incorrect financial calculations
quizBank.answer: Floating-point arithmetic has precision errors that cause incorrect financial calculations
quizBank.explanation: IEEE 754 float: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004. Over millions of transactions, these errors accumulate. Store as integer cents: $1.99 = 199. Never use FLOAT or DOUBLE for money. Use BIGINT (cents) or NUMERIC(19,4) in SQL.

quizBank.question: What is double-entry bookkeeping and why is it used in payment systems?
options: Recording each transaction twice for redundancy
Every transaction creates a debit entry and a credit entry ensuring the ledger always balances
quizBank.answer: Every transaction creates a debit entry and a credit entry ensuring the ledger always balances
quizBank.explanation: Double-entry: every transaction affects at least two accounts. Sum of all debits = Sum of all credits always. This invariant makes errors immediately detectable (ledger won't balance). Accountants have used this for 500+ years. Any discrepancy in balance → data corruption or bug.

quizBank.question: The payment gateway times out after 10 seconds. You don't know if the charge succeeded. What do you do?
quizBank.answer: Unknown outcome is the most dangerous payment state. Strategy: (1) Query the gateway: call the gateway's GET /charges/{id} endpoint to check status. Many gateways support this. If succeeded: update internal status, don't retry charge. (2) If gateway unreachable: hold payment in PENDING state. Retry status check with exponential backoff. (3) Idempotency: if you must retry the charge itself, use same idempotency key → gateway will return same result without double charging. (4) Customer communication: if held too long → show 'payment processing' state to customer. Don't claim success OR failure. (5) Timeout SLA: after 30 minutes without resolution → fail the payment, release any holds. Customer can retry.

quizBank.question: What does PCI DSS require regarding storage of credit card numbers?
options: Never store the full PAN (Primary Account Number) unless absolutely necessary, with strict controls if you do
quizBank.answer: Never store the full PAN (Primary Account Number) unless absolutely necessary, with strict controls if you do
quizBank.explanation: PCI DSS: storing PANs requires Level 1 compliance (most stringent). Best practice: never store cards — use tokenization (Stripe/Braintree stores card, gives you a token). Breach of tokens: useless to attacker. Breach of PANs: enormous liability, fines, brand damage.

quizBank.question: How do you design webhook delivery to guarantee at-least-once delivery to merchants?
quizBank.answer: Durable queue: publish payment events to Kafka (persisted). Webhook worker consumes from Kafka. For each event: (1) Find merchant's registered webhook URL(s). (2) POST event payload with HMAC-SHA256 signature. (3) Wait up to 30 seconds for 200 response. (4) On failure (non-200, timeout, connection error): publish to retry queue with exponential backoff metadata. (5) Retry schedule: 5s, 30s, 2m, 10m, 30m, 2h, 8h — up to 5 attempts. (6) After 5 failures: dead letter queue. Alert merchant dashboard. (7) At-least-once: same event may be delivered multiple times. Merchants must implement idempotency: check event_id in their DB, skip if already processed. (8) Event ordering: Kafka partitions by merchant_id ensures per-merchant ordering.

quizBank.question: A merchant has a chargeback rate of 2%. What action should the payment system take?
options: Automatically suspend the merchant account and notify them
Block individual customers who filed chargebacks
quizBank.answer: Automatically suspend the merchant account and notify them
quizBank.explanation: Visa/Mastercard require processors to suspend merchants exceeding 1% chargeback rate. 2% is above threshold. Consequences: card networks can fine the acquirer and terminate processing agreement. Action: immediate suspension, notification, investigation. Merchant can dispute if chargebacks are fraud (identity theft, not their fault).

quizBank.question: Your payment processing system needs to handle a payment gateway outage. How do you design failover?
quizBank.answer: Multi-gateway strategy: (1) Primary gateway: Stripe. (2) Secondary gateway: Braintree or Adyen. (3) Circuit breaker: monitor Stripe error rate. If >10% errors in 60 seconds: open circuit breaker, route to secondary. (4) Routing logic: payment service selects gateway based on: health, card type (some gateways better for certain cards), cost (negotiate rates), geographic optimization. (5) Retry on failover: new request to secondary with same idempotency key. Since different gateway: cannot reuse gateway-specific key. Generate new internal attempt_id. (6) Customer impact: slight latency increase during failover. No customer-visible failure if failover completes within 30 seconds. (7) Reconciliation: track which gateway processed each payment. Run separate reconciliation per gateway.

quizBank.question: Explain how a refund is processed end-to-end, including ledger entries.
quizBank.answer: Refund flow: (1) Merchant POST /refunds {charge_id, amount}. (2) Payment service: validate charge exists, status=captured, refund amount ≤ original amount. (3) Call gateway refund API (Stripe: POST /refunds with charge_id). (4) Gateway processes: funds returned from merchant's bank to customer's card. (5) Internal state: create refund record. Update payment status: refunded or partially_refunded. (6) Ledger entries (reverse of original): DEBIT merchant_receivable:merchant_456 $100 / CREDIT customer_payable:customer_123 $100. (7) Balance impact: merchant's available balance decreases. (8) Kafka event: refund.created. (9) Webhook: notify merchant of refund event. (10) Customer: card statement shows credit within 5-10 business days (depends on card network).

quizBank.question: What is a payment 'authorization' vs 'capture'?
options: Authorization reserves funds on customer's card; capture actually moves the money to the merchant
Authorization is for credit cards, capture for debit
Authorization is optional, capture is mandatory
quizBank.answer: Authorization reserves funds on customer's card; capture actually moves the money to the merchant
quizBank.explanation: Two-phase: Authorization places a hold on customer funds (verifies card, checks fraud, reserves amount). No money moves yet. Capture (later, up to 7 days): actually moves money from customer to merchant. Used by e-commerce to authorize at order, capture only when item ships. If not captured: hold expires, no charge. If cancelled before capture: release authorization.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a situation where your internal ledger shows a different balance than the bank?
quizBank.answer: Reconciliation discrepancy — potentially serious. Process: (1) Don't panic: small discrepancies common due to timing (payments processing overnight). (2) Categorize: which transactions are in bank but not ledger? Which in ledger but not bank? (3) Bank has it, ledger doesn't: missed capture? Gateway sent settlement file but internal event lost. Replay Kafka event or manually post ledger entry after investigation. (4) Ledger has it, bank doesn't: timing issue (payment authorized but not yet settled). Check again in 24 hours. If still missing after 3 days: potential fraud or error. (5) All discrepancies require manual review and documented resolution. (6) Root cause: identify and fix the bug causing discrepancy. (7) Controls: automated alert if discrepancy > $100 or >0.01% of daily volume.

quizBank.question: What is HMAC-SHA256 signature verification used for in webhook delivery?
options: To allow merchants to verify that webhooks are genuinely sent by the payment processor and not forged
quizBank.answer: To allow merchants to verify that webhooks are genuinely sent by the payment processor and not forged
quizBank.explanation: Without verification: attacker sends fake webhook 'payment succeeded' to merchant's endpoint. Merchant ships goods without actual payment. With HMAC: payment processor signs payload with merchant's secret key. Merchant verifies signature. Only the processor and merchant know the secret. Forged webhooks have invalid signatures → rejected.

quizBank.question: How would you design a real-time fraud detection system that operates in <50ms?
quizBank.answer: 50ms budget requires fully in-memory processing. Architecture: (1) Pre-computed risk signals in Redis: card velocity (INCR card:{card_hash}:count EX 60), IP velocity, device fingerprint score. (2) ML model: lightweight gradient boosting (XGBoost) or neural network optimized for inference. Served via TensorFlow Serving or custom C++ service. Model size: <10MB (fits in L3 cache). (3) Feature computation: parallel Redis lookups (pipeline), sub-10ms. (4) Inference: model scoring with pre-computed features, <5ms. (5) Decision: threshold comparison, <1ms. (6) Total: 10ms feature fetch + 5ms inference + overhead = ~20ms. (7) Async enrichment: for borderline cases (score 0.4-0.7): proceed but log for async deeper analysis. (8) Model updates: retrain nightly on new fraud labels. Deploy new model with canary (5% traffic) before full rollout.

quizBank.question: Two concurrent requests with the same idempotency key arrive simultaneously. How do you handle this?
options: Process both, take the first result
Use Redis SETNX to acquire a lock — only one request processes, the other waits and returns the cached result
quizBank.answer: Use Redis SETNX to acquire a lock — only one request processes, the other waits and returns the cached result
quizBank.explanation: Race condition: both requests check idempotency store simultaneously, both see null, both start processing → double charge possible. Solution: atomic Redis SETNX (SET if Not eXists) to create a processing lock. Only one succeeds. The other: polls until result available (with timeout). Fencing token prevents stale lock from causing issues on node failure.

quizBank.question: A merchant wants to implement a subscription billing system. How do you design this?
quizBank.answer: Subscription billing on top of payment infrastructure: (1) Subscription object: stores {plan, interval, customer, payment_method, status, next_billing_date}. (2) Scheduler: cron job runs daily. Finds subscriptions where next_billing_date <= today AND status=active. (3) Charge: create charge with stored payment method token. Idempotency key: {subscription_id}:{billing_period}. (4) Success: update next_billing_date += interval. Send receipt. (5) Failure handling: smart retry — retry failed charge 3x over 7 days (sometimes cards temporarily decline). Send dunning emails. After 7 days of failure: pause subscription, notify customer to update payment. (6) Proration: mid-cycle upgrades/downgrades: credit unused time, charge new plan prorated. (7) Trials: no charge until trial ends. Webhook: subscription.trial_will_end 3 days before.

quizBank.question: How do you support multiple currencies in a payment system?
quizBank.answer: Multi-currency design: (1) All amounts stored in minor units of their respective currency (cents for USD, pence for GBP, yen for JPY — note JPY has no minor unit, so 1 yen = 1 unit). (2) Explicit currency on every amount: {amount: 500, currency: 'usd'} = $5.00. (3) FX rates: fetch daily rates from provider (Open Exchange Rates, XE). Cache in Redis. Lock rate at payment creation time. (4) Settlement currency: merchants have a settlement currency. FX conversion at settlement: use rate locked at payment time. (5) Ledger: all entries include currency. Cross-currency: two entries in different currencies. FX gain/loss tracking for accounting. (6) Display: client formats amount per locale (en-US: $5.00, de-DE: 5,00 $). Use Intl.NumberFormat. (7) Rounding: round half-up, always. Document rounding strategy in API docs.

quizBank.question: What is a chargeback?
options: A fee charged by payment processors
When a customer disputes a charge with their bank, and the bank forcibly reverses the payment
A refund initiated by the merchant
quizBank.answer: When a customer disputes a charge with their bank, and the bank forcibly reverses the payment
quizBank.explanation: Chargeback: customer contacts their issuing bank claiming unauthorized charge or non-delivered goods. Bank reverses the charge without merchant consent. Merchant must provide evidence to dispute. Chargebacks result in: lost revenue, chargeback fee ($15-100), and merchant reputation risk with card networks. Merchants with >1% chargeback rate face penalties.

quizBank.question: How would you design instant payouts (money in merchant's bank within 30 seconds)?
quizBank.answer: Instant payout infrastructure: traditional payouts take 1-3 business days (ACH). Instant options: (1) Push-to-debit: Visa Direct / Mastercard Send — push funds to merchant's debit card instantly. 24/7/365. Requires knowing merchant's debit card. (2) RTP (Real-Time Payments, US): Federal Reserve's FedNow or TCH RTP network. Account-to-account transfer in seconds. Requires bank to bank connectivity. (3) Prefunding: Stripe/PayPal pre-funds a pool of money at receiving banks. Payout debits the pool, immediately credits merchant. Reconciled with actual payments later. (4) Risk: instant means no reversal. Must verify merchant identity, hold period for new merchants, fraud detection before releasing payout. (5) Cost: instant payout fee (0.25-1% or flat fee) passed to merchant. (6) Architecture: payout service → determine method (RTP/Visa Direct based on merchant's bank) → bank API call → status callback.

quizBank.question: How would you design a marketplace payment system (like Airbnb) where payment flows through a platform to sellers?
quizBank.answer: Marketplace payments (split payments): (1) Customer pays platform (Airbnb). Platform holds funds. (2) At checkout: split payment → platform fee deducted → remainder goes to seller. (3) Implementation: charge customer for full amount. Create transfer: platform → seller account (minus fees). (4) Timing: hold funds until service delivered (host's payout released after guest checks in). (5) Connect accounts (Stripe Connect): each seller has a connected account on Stripe. Platform makes transfers to connected accounts. Stripe handles KYC for each seller. (6) Tax compliance: 1099-K reporting for sellers above threshold (US). Collect tax ID (EIN/SSN) at onboarding. (7) Dispute resolution: if customer disputes → platform has funds held → can refund customer AND claw back from seller. (8) Multi-party splits: commission to referrer, revenue share to multiple sellers.

quizBank.question: What is 3D Secure (3DS) authentication used for?
options: Adding an extra authentication step (OTP/biometric) to reduce card-not-present fraud liability
quizBank.answer: Adding an extra authentication step (OTP/biometric) to reduce card-not-present fraud liability
quizBank.explanation: 3DS (Verified by Visa / Mastercard SecureCode): issuing bank challenges cardholder with OTP (SMS, app notification) or biometric during online checkout. If authenticated: liability shifts from merchant to issuing bank for fraud losses. Used for high-risk transactions. Adds friction — some merchants avoid it unless fraud risk high. 3DS2 (newer): risk-based, frictionless for low-risk transactions.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between a payment gateway and a payment processor?
quizBank.answer: Payment gateway: software interface between merchant's website/app and payment networks. Handles: encryption of card data, API for payment submission, returns auth response to merchant. Examples: Stripe, PayPal, Square. Payment processor: financial institution that actually moves money between banks. Handles: card network connectivity (Visa/Mastercard/Amex), bank-to-bank settlement, fraud monitoring at transaction level. Examples: First Data, TSYS, Chase Paymentech. In practice: Stripe is both gateway AND processor (full-stack). Traditional setup: merchant → gateway (Authorize.net) → processor (Worldpay). Stripe simplified by combining both into one API. Acquiring bank: bank that holds merchant's account, sponsors merchant into card network.

quizBank.question: How do you implement payment retries safely when a customer's card is declined?
quizBank.answer: Intelligent retry system for failed payments (common in subscription billing): (1) First decline: don't immediately retry — most declines require card update or customer action. (2) Decline reason classification: insufficient_funds → retry in 3 days (payday). do_not_honor (bank blocked) → retry next day. expired_card → email customer to update card, don't retry. lost_stolen → don't retry, require new card. (3) Smart retry schedule: day 1 (first attempt), day 3, day 7, day 14. (4) Dunning emails: progressive urgency. 'Payment failed' → 'Action required' → 'Account will be suspended'. (5) Payment method update: send link to update card. If updated: retry immediately. (6) Idempotency: each retry attempt uses unique idempotency key: {subscription_id}:{period}:{attempt_number}. (7) Give up: after 4 attempts over 14 days → suspend subscription, notify customer.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Payment System (Stripe/PayPal)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

cheatSheet:
# Design a File Storage System (Google Drive/Dropbox)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Upload files of any type (up to 5GB per file)
- Download files
- File versioning: access previous versions
- Sync: changes on one device reflected on all devices
- Share files/folders with other users (view, comment, edit)
- Collaboration: multiple users edit same file
- Deduplication: same file content stored once
- Offline access: changes synced when back online

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Durability: 99.9999999% (eleven nines) — never lose a file
- Availability: 99.99%
- Consistency: sync conflict resolution
- Scale: 1B users, 10PB total storage, 500M file operations/day
- Bandwidth efficient: don't re-upload unchanged parts of a file

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- Users: 1B, average 15GB storage = 15EB total (need tiered storage)
- Free tier: 15GB. Paid: 100GB-2TB
- File operations: 500M/day = ~5,800/sec
- Upload bandwidth: 500M files/day avg 1MB = 500TB/day upload
- Download: assume 10x download ratio = 5PB/day egress
- Metadata: 1B users x 1000 files avg x 500 bytes = 500TB metadata
- Deduplication effect: estimate 30% files are duplicates → 30% storage savings

## 3. Core Concept: Chunked Upload

```
Why chunk files?
- Large file (5GB) upload fails at 4.9GB → must restart without chunking
- With chunking: only re-upload failed chunk
- Deduplication: if chunk already on server, skip upload
- Parallel upload: multiple chunks simultaneously
- Delta sync: only upload changed chunks

Chunk size: 4MB (balance between chunk count overhead and upload efficiency)

Chunk processing:
1. Client splits file into 4MB chunks
2. For each chunk: compute SHA-256 fingerprint
3. Check server: is this chunk already stored? (dedup check)
4. Upload only chunks not already stored
5. Server assembles file from chunk references
```

## 4. High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([Desktop/Mobile/Web Client])
    AG[API Gateway]
    US[Upload Service]
    DS[Download Service]
    SS[Sync Service]
    MS[Metadata Service]
    CS[Chunk Store S3]
    MDB[(Metadata DB - MySQL)]
    CDB[(Chunk Index DB - MySQL)]
    SQ[Sync Queue - Kafka]
    Cache[Redis - Session/Cache]
    CDN[CDN for Download]
    NS[Notification Service]

    Client -->|Upload chunks| AG
    Client -->|Get metadata| AG
    AG --> US
    AG --> DS
    AG --> SS
    AG --> MS
    US --> CS
    US --> CDB
    US --> MDB
    US --> SQ
    DS --> CS
    DS --> CDN
    MS --> MDB
    SQ --> SS
    SS --> NS
    NS --> Client
```

## 5. Upload Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant US as Upload Service
    participant DB as Chunk Index DB
    participant S3 as Chunk Store
    participant MS as Metadata Service
    participant Kafka

    C->>C: Split file into 4MB chunks
    C->>C: Compute SHA-256 for each chunk
    C->>US: POST /upload/init {file_name, total_chunks, chunk_hashes[]}
    US->>DB: Which hashes already exist?
    DB-->>US: existing_hashes: [hash1, hash3]
    US-->>C: Upload these chunks: [hash2, hash4, hash5] (skip existing)
    C->>S3: Upload chunk2 (presigned URL)
    C->>S3: Upload chunk4 (presigned URL)
    C->>S3: Upload chunk5 (presigned URL)
    C->>US: POST /upload/complete {file_id, chunk_order: [hash1,hash2,hash3,hash4,hash5]}
    US->>MS: Create file record with chunk references
    US->>Kafka: file_updated event
    Kafka->>SS: Notify other devices to sync
```

## 6. Deduplication

### Block-Level Deduplication
```
Chunk fingerprint: SHA-256(chunk_content)
Global chunk store: one copy per unique chunk fingerprint
File = ordered list of chunk references

Benefits:
- Same file uploaded by 1000 users: stored only once
- File that changes partially: only changed chunks stored
- Video with watermark: each chunk unique → no benefit
  (dedup works best for exact duplicates)

Chunk index table:
CREATE TABLE chunks (
    chunk_hash   CHAR(64) PRIMARY KEY,   -- SHA-256 hex
    storage_path TEXT NOT NULL,           -- S3 path
    size_bytes   INT,
    ref_count    INT DEFAULT 1,           -- for garbage collection
    created_at   TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

File chunks mapping:
CREATE TABLE file_chunks (
    file_version_id  BIGINT,
    chunk_seq        INT,        -- position in file
    chunk_hash       CHAR(64),
    PRIMARY KEY (file_version_id, chunk_seq)
);
```

## 7. Versioning

```sql
CREATE TABLE files (
    file_id      BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
    owner_id     BIGINT,
    folder_id    BIGINT,
    name         VARCHAR(255),
    is_deleted   BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    created_at   TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at   TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE file_versions (
    version_id      BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    file_id         BIGINT NOT NULL,
    version_number  INT NOT NULL,
    size_bytes      BIGINT,
    content_hash    CHAR(64),    -- hash of entire file
    created_by      BIGINT,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    is_latest       BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    change_comment  TEXT
);
-- When new version: set previous is_latest=false, insert new with is_latest=true

Version retention:
- Keep last 100 versions (Dropbox)
- Keep versions for 30-365 days depending on plan
- Prune old versions via background job
```

## 8. Sync Protocol

### Sync Architecture
```
Components:
- Sync Service: tracks device state, dispatches sync events
- Long-poll or WebSocket: device waits for change notifications
- Client sync agent: background process on device

Sync flow:
1. Client A edits file on laptop
2. Laptop uploads changed chunks, updates version
3. Kafka: file_updated event {file_id, new_version_id, user_id}
4. Sync Service: find all devices for this user
5. Push notification to Client B (phone), Client C (work laptop)
6. Clients B, C: download diff (only new chunks)

Delta sync:
- Client tracks local version_id per file
- On notification: compare remote version_id > local
- Fetch: GET /files/{id}/diff?from_version={local_version}
- Response: which chunks changed (chunk_hash list)
- Download only new/changed chunks
```

## 9. Conflict Resolution

```
Conflict scenario:
User edits file on Laptop (offline) AND Phone simultaneously
Both create new versions independently

Conflict detection:
Version number or vector clock: both devices based on version_id=5
Laptop creates version 6, Phone creates version 6 (conflict!)

Resolution strategies:
1. Last-Write-Wins: highest timestamp wins. Simple, may lose edits.
2. Conflict copy: keep both versions:
   - 'document.docx' (phone version - newer by timestamp)
   - 'document (laptop conflict 2024-01-15).docx' (laptop version)
   User resolves manually. Dropbox uses this approach.
3. Operational Transform / CRDT: for text files — merge edits
   character-level (Google Docs approach). Complex but seamless.
4. Application-specific: Figma, Notion have custom merge logic.
```

## 10. Sharing & Permissions

```sql
CREATE TABLE shares (
    share_id      BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
    resource_id   BIGINT,        -- file_id or folder_id
    resource_type VARCHAR(10),   -- file, folder
    owner_id      BIGINT,
    shared_with   BIGINT,        -- user_id (NULL for link share)
    permission    VARCHAR(10),   -- view, comment, edit
    link_token    VARCHAR(64),   -- for link-based sharing
    expires_at    TIMESTAMP,
    created_at    TIMESTAMP
);

Permission check:
1. Is user the owner? → full access
2. Is file directly shared with user? → check permission
3. Is parent folder shared with user? → inherit permission
4. Is link_token valid and not expired? → check permission

Folder inheritance: permissions cascade down to all children.
Performance: cache permission checks in Redis (TTL: 5 min)
```

## 11. API Design

```
File Operations:
POST /api/v1/upload/init           — initiate upload, get chunk upload plan
PUT  /api/v1/chunks/{hash}         — upload individual chunk (presigned S3)
POST /api/v1/upload/complete       — finalize file upload
GET  /api/v1/files/{id}            — file metadata
GET  /api/v1/files/{id}/download   — download (redirect to CDN signed URL)
GET  /api/v1/files/{id}/versions   — list versions
GET  /api/v1/files/{id}/versions/{v}/download — download specific version
DEL  /api/v1/files/{id}            — soft delete (trash)

Sync:
GET  /api/v1/sync/changes?since={timestamp}  — poll for changes
WebSocket: ws://sync.example.com            — real-time change push

Sharing:
POST /api/v1/files/{id}/share      — share with user or create link
GET  /api/v1/shared-with-me        — files shared with current user
PUT  /api/v1/shares/{id}           — update permissions
DEL  /api/v1/shares/{id}           — revoke share
```

## 12. Storage Architecture

```
Tiered storage:
Hot tier (S3 Standard): recently accessed files, chunks
  Cost: ~$0.023/GB/month
Warm tier (S3-IA): files not accessed in 30 days
  Cost: ~$0.0125/GB/month
Cold tier (Glacier): files not accessed in 1 year
  Cost: ~$0.004/GB/month

Automatic tiering:
- S3 Intelligent-Tiering: automatically moves objects between tiers
- Or: Lambda function runs daily, checks last_accessed, moves to IA/Glacier

Chunk garbage collection:
- When file version deleted: decrement chunk ref_count
- chunk.ref_count == 0: chunk eligible for deletion
- GC job: daily scan for ref_count=0, delete from S3
```

## 13. Monitoring
- Upload success rate and latency p99
- Sync notification latency (from upload to notification to other devices)
- Deduplication ratio (chunk cache hit rate)
- Storage growth rate per tier
- Conflict resolution frequency (indicator of sync health)
- Download CDN hit rate
- Chunk GC lag (orphaned chunks accumulating)

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows file storage as upload/download. Can describe S3 for storage. Understands basic sharing.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Understands chunked upload for large files. Knows about versioning. Can describe basic sync between devices.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Implements chunk deduplication with SHA-256. Designs delta sync. Handles sync conflicts with conflict copies. Designs permission inheritance.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Designs tiered storage, chunk garbage collection, offline sync, WebSocket-based real-time sync, and content-defined chunking.
skills: Offline conflict detection with vector clocks

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs operational transform for real-time collaboration, cross-region replication with consistency guarantees, exabyte-scale storage optimization, and enterprise security (DLP, e-discovery).
skills: Operational Transform / CRDT for real-time collab
