quizBank.question: Why is chunked upload better than single-file upload for large files?
options: If upload fails, only the failed chunk needs to be re-uploaded, and chunks can be uploaded in parallel
quizBank.answer: If upload fails, only the failed chunk needs to be re-uploaded, and chunks can be uploaded in parallel
quizBank.explanation: Single file 5GB upload fails at 4.9GB → restart from 0. Chunked: resume from failed chunk. Also: parallel chunk uploads (4 chunks simultaneously) = 4x faster. Deduplication: identical chunks already on server = skip upload.

quizBank.question: What hash function is used for block-level deduplication fingerprinting?
quizBank.answer: SHA-256 (secure, no practical collisions)
quizBank.explanation: SHA-256: 256-bit fingerprint. No two different inputs produce same hash (in practice). MD5 has known collisions — attacker could craft malicious file with same hash as legitimate file. SHA-256 is the industry standard for content-addressable storage. BLAKE3 is faster and also secure — an emerging alternative.

quizBank.question: User A edits a file on their laptop while offline. User B edits the same file online. When A reconnects, how do you handle the conflict?
quizBank.answer: Conflict detection: A's client holds last_synced_version_id. When A reconnects, sync service checks: has the server version changed since A's last sync? Server version > A's base version → conflict. Resolution options: (1) Conflict copy (Dropbox): keep server version as canonical. Save A's version as 'filename (A's MacBook conflict 2024-01-15).docx'. User manually merges. Simple, safe, no data loss. (2) Last-Write-Wins: A's version timestamp > B's → A wins, B's changes lost. Simple but loses edits. (3) Three-way merge: use common ancestor (last synced version) + A's changes + B's changes → attempt automatic merge. Works for text, fails for binary. (4) Google Docs approach: OT/CRDT — only possible if all edits go through server (not true offline). Choice depends on file type and product decision.

quizBank.question: What is 'delta sync' and why does it matter?
options: Transferring only the changed chunks of a file instead of the whole file
Periodic full resync of all files
quizBank.answer: Transferring only the changed chunks of a file instead of the whole file
quizBank.explanation: User edits 1 paragraph of a 1GB document. Without delta sync: upload 1GB. With delta sync: only upload the changed 4MB chunk. Bandwidth savings: 99.6%. Implementation: client computes chunk hashes before and after edit. Only hashes that changed need re-upload. Server assembles new file version from mix of old and new chunks.

quizBank.question: How does block-level deduplication work when the same file is uploaded by 1000 users?
quizBank.answer: Without dedup: 1000 users x 1GB = 1TB storage. With dedup: 1GB stored once. Each user's file_version record points to same chunk hashes. When user 2 uploads: client computes chunk hashes, sends to server. Server checks chunk_index: all hashes already exist. Server responds: 'all chunks already stored, no upload needed'. User 2's file record created in < 1 second (no actual data transfer). Storage: 1GB for all 1000 users. Reference counting: chunk.ref_count = 1000. When user deletes their file: ref_count decremented. Only delete chunk from S3 when ref_count = 0.

quizBank.question: How do you implement real-time collaborative editing (multiple users editing same doc simultaneously) like Google Docs?
quizBank.answer: Real-time collaboration requires Operational Transform (OT) or CRDT. Problem: User A inserts 'Hello' at position 5, User B deletes character at position 3. Concurrent operations conflict. OT: transform operations against each other. Server applies A's operation → transforms B's operation to account for A's change → applies transformed B. All clients converge to same state. Implementation: (1) WebSocket connection to collaboration server. (2) Each edit creates an Operation object: {type: insert/delete, position, content, author, timestamp}. (3) Server receives operations from all clients, applies OT, broadcasts transformed operations to all clients. (4) Eventual consistency: all clients converge to same document state. Complex to implement correctly. Libraries: Yjs (CRDT), ShareDB (OT). Google Docs uses OT internally.

quizBank.question: How do you handle permissions when a folder is shared with 'view' access but contains a subfolder with 'edit' access for a different user?
options: Parent folder permissions always override subfolder
Permissions are evaluated independently per resource with inheritance from parent
quizBank.answer: Permissions are evaluated independently per resource with inheritance from parent
quizBank.explanation: Permission evaluation: check if user has direct permission on resource → if not, check parent folder → if not, check grandparent → up to root. Most specific permission wins over inherited. Subfolder with explicit 'edit' for user X overrides parent's 'view' restriction for X. Different users can have different permissions on same resource. Caching: compute and cache effective permissions per (user, resource) in Redis.

quizBank.question: How do you implement chunk garbage collection safely?
quizBank.answer: Reference counting: each chunk has ref_count in chunk_index table. When file version created: increment ref_count for each chunk hash. When file version deleted: decrement ref_count. When ref_count = 0: chunk eligible for deletion. Safe GC process: (1) Don't delete immediately on ref_count=0 — mark as 'pending_deletion' with timestamp. (2) GC grace period: wait 24 hours before actually deleting from S3. Handles: race conditions where new version is being created with same chunk. (3) GC job: daily batch scan for pending_deletion entries older than 24 hours. Delete from S3. Remove from chunk_index. (4) Consistency: use database transactions for ref_count updates. (5) Verification: after S3 delete, verify no remaining references. (6) Audit log: record all chunk deletions for recovery.

quizBank.question: What is content-defined chunking (CDC) and how does it improve deduplication?
options: Variable-size chunking that detects content boundaries, improving dedup for files with insertions/deletions
quizBank.answer: Variable-size chunking that detects content boundaries, improving dedup for files with insertions/deletions
quizBank.explanation: Fixed chunking: insert 1 byte at file start → all chunk boundaries shift → all chunks change → 0% dedup. CDC: uses rolling hash (Rabin fingerprint) to find natural chunk boundaries based on content. Insert 1 byte at start: only first chunk changes. Subsequent chunks have same content → same hash → dedup works. Used by: Dropbox (TTTD algorithm), restic, Borg. Trade-off: variable chunk size, more complex implementation.

quizBank.question: A user accidentally deletes a folder with 10,000 files. How do you implement 'restore from trash'?
quizBank.answer: Soft delete: DELETE request marks files/folders as is_deleted=true, stores deleted_at timestamp. Data preserved in storage. Trash bin: show user all deleted items in last 30 days (free) or 180 days (paid). Restore: user selects items in trash → mark is_deleted=false, clear deleted_at. Folder restore: recursively restore all children. Permission restoration: check if shared permissions are still valid (collaborators may have changed). Conflict: if a new file/folder was created with same name in same location → restore with '(restored)' suffix. Permanent delete: explicitly from trash, or after retention period expires. Background job: permanently delete (and decrement chunk ref_counts) for items past retention period.

quizBank.question: How do you design the sync service to handle a user with 10 devices?
quizBank.answer: Per-device sync state: each device has a record in device_sync table: {device_id, user_id, last_sync_timestamp, sync_cursor}. Change tracking: every file operation creates a change event in a changes log table: {change_id, user_id, file_id, change_type, version_id, created_at}. Sync pull: device polls GET /sync/changes?since={last_sync_cursor}. Returns all changes since cursor. Device applies changes: download new/modified files, delete removed files. Update sync cursor. Push notifications: Kafka event → sync service → WebSocket/push to all user's devices → devices pull immediately. Device offline: when device reconnects, pulls all missed changes from changes log. Changes log retention: 30 days. If device offline >30 days: full resync needed.

quizBank.question: How are large file downloads made efficient and resumable?
options: Download entire file, cache in browser
HTTP Range requests: client requests specific byte ranges, enabling pause/resume without starting over
Split file into chunks on server before download
quizBank.answer: HTTP Range requests: client requests specific byte ranges, enabling pause/resume without starting over
quizBank.explanation: HTTP Range header: 'Range: bytes=500000-999999'. Server returns 206 Partial Content with requested byte range. Download interrupted at 50%: resume with Range: bytes=50000000-. S3 natively supports range requests. Client tracks progress, resumes from last received byte. Also enables parallel download of different ranges simultaneously.

quizBank.question: How do you implement enterprise file retention / legal hold (e-discovery)?
quizBank.answer: Legal hold: organization places hold on specific users or search criteria. During hold: files cannot be permanently deleted (even by user, admin, or GC). Implementation: (1) Legal hold table: {hold_id, scope, created_by, created_at}. Scope: user_id, date_range, file_pattern. (2) Hold check: before any permanent deletion, check if file matches any active hold. If yes: prevent deletion. Return error to user: 'File cannot be deleted due to legal hold'. (3) Hold release: only legal/compliance team can release. Requires MFA. (4) Audit log: immutable log of all actions on held files. (5) Search (e-discovery): search all held files by keyword, date, user. Elasticsearch index of file content and metadata. (6) Export: produce court-admissible evidence package (ZIP with manifest). (7) Chain of custody: cryptographic signature of export at time of creation.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of storing a content hash (SHA-256) for the entire file separately from chunk hashes?
quizBank.answer: File-level content hash (e.g., SHA-256 of all bytes) serves multiple purposes: (1) Integrity verification: after download, client computes hash of received file, compares to stored hash. If different: download was corrupted, retry. (2) Deduplication at file level: quickly detect if two files are completely identical before computing all chunk hashes. If file_hash matches: files are identical. (3) Version tracking: file_versions table stores content_hash. If same content_hash → no actual change (user saved without modifying). Skip creating new version. (4) Legal/compliance: proof of file content at specific point in time. Content hash provides tamper-evident record. (5) Sync optimization: compare file-level hash first — if identical, skip detailed chunk comparison.

quizBank.question: What strategy does Dropbox use for conflict resolution when two users edit the same file simultaneously?
options: Conflict copy — keeps both versions, user resolves manually
quizBank.answer: Conflict copy — keeps both versions, user resolves manually
quizBank.explanation: Dropbox conflict copy: the more recent conflicting version becomes the canonical version (kept at original filename). The older conflicting version saved as 'filename (User's Device conflict date).ext'. User sees both files, manually merges. Google Docs uses OT for seamless merge. Dropbox chose simplicity over seamlessness — file syncing works for all file types (including binary) where OT is impossible.

quizBank.question: How do you implement file sharing via a public link (anyone with link can view)?
quizBank.answer: Link-based sharing: (1) Generate secure random token (256-bit, URL-safe base64): share_token. (2) Store in shares table: {share_id, file_id, share_type: 'link', permission: 'view', link_token, expires_at}. (3) Share URL: https://example.com/s/{share_token}. (4) Access: recipient opens URL, no auth required. Server looks up share by token. Checks: token exists, not expired, file not deleted. Returns file metadata and temporary download URL. (5) Download: generate signed S3 URL valid for 1 hour. Recipient downloads. (6) Security: (a) Token must be unguessable (256 bits of entropy). (b) Optional: password protection (user sets password, SHA-256 stored, checked on access). (c) Optional: link expiry date. (d) View count tracking. (e) Revoke: delete share record instantly invalidates all access.

quizBank.question: How would you design cross-region replication for file storage to ensure durability?
quizBank.answer: Multi-region durability: 11 nines durability (99.999999999%) requires multiple geographic copies. S3 architecture: (1) Primary region (us-east-1): write all chunks here first. (2) S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR): asynchronously replicate to eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1. (3) Custom: Dropbox Magic Pocket — custom distributed storage across 3 zones within region, async replication to second region. (4) Consistency: write to primary, acknowledge to client. Replication happens async (1-10 seconds lag). If primary region fails: reads from secondary. (5) Eventual consistency for replicas: acceptable — files are immutable chunks. New chunks eventually reach all regions. (6) Metadata: synchronous replication (use MySQL Group Replication or DynamoDB Global Tables). Must be consistent — can't serve a file if chunk references aren't replicated. (7) RTO/RPO: if region fails → RTO <5 min (switch to secondary), RPO <30 seconds (replication lag).

quizBank.question: What is tiered storage and why does it matter for a file storage service?
options: Multiple copies stored on different hardware
Automatically moving files to cheaper storage classes based on access frequency to reduce cost
quizBank.answer: Automatically moving files to cheaper storage classes based on access frequency to reduce cost
quizBank.explanation: Storage cost optimization: S3 Standard ($0.023/GB/month) for hot files. S3-IA ($0.0125/GB/month, 46% cheaper) for files not accessed in 30 days. S3 Glacier ($0.004/GB/month, 83% cheaper) for files not accessed in 1 year. Most files are rarely accessed after initial upload. Intelligent-Tiering: S3 automatically moves objects based on access. Cost example: 15EB x $0.023 = $345M/month vs tiered average $0.008 = $120M/month. 65% cost reduction.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a user uploading 10,000 small files (1KB each) simultaneously?
quizBank.answer: Small file problem: 10,000 x 1KB files = 10MB total, but 10,000 separate API calls = high overhead. Optimizations: (1) Batch upload API: POST /upload/batch with multiple files in one request. Server processes atomically. (2) Archive before upload: client ZIP small files, upload as one chunk, server unzips. (3) Chunking not needed for small files — each file IS a chunk. Skip chunk check for files < chunk_size threshold. (4) Metadata batching: single DB insert of 10,000 file records in one transaction. (5) Dedup: check all 10,000 hashes in one DB query (SELECT hash FROM chunks WHERE hash IN (...)). (6) Parallel uploads: client uploads to S3 in parallel (50 concurrent connections). (7) Rate limiting: client must respect API rate limits. Queue locally if hitting limits.

quizBank.question: How do you implement versioning with a retention limit (e.g., keep only last 30 versions)?
quizBank.answer: Versioning with retention: (1) On each file save: create new file_version record with version_number = MAX(version_number) + 1 for that file_id. Set is_latest=true, set previous latest to is_latest=false. (2) Retention enforcement: after creating new version, count total versions for file_id. If count > 30: delete oldest version(s). (3) Cascade: deleting old file_version → decrement ref_count for its chunks → GC eligible if ref_count=0. (4) Soft delete first: mark version as deleted, keep for 24 hours (GC grace period). (5) Version list API: SELECT * FROM file_versions WHERE file_id=? AND is_deleted=false ORDER BY version_number DESC LIMIT 30. (6) Storage credit: deleted versions free up storage quota. (7) Paid plans: extended version history (180 days vs 30 days). Store version_count_limit in user's plan settings.

quizBank.question: Why is the file sync service better implemented with WebSocket notifications rather than polling?
options: WebSocket is required by mobile OS
WebSocket server push eliminates polling delay and reduces server load from constant polling requests
quizBank.answer: WebSocket server push eliminates polling delay and reduces server load from constant polling requests
quizBank.explanation: Polling (GET /sync/changes every 30s): change appears up to 30 seconds late. 1B devices x 2 requests/min = 2B requests/min just for polling. WebSocket: server pushes change notification within 1 second. No polling requests. Devices only make requests when there are actual changes. Better UX (near-instant sync) and lower cost.

quizBank.question: How do you implement data loss prevention (DLP) — detecting when sensitive data (credit cards, SSNs) is uploaded?
quizBank.answer: DLP scanning pipeline: (1) Trigger: on file upload complete (async, post-upload). (2) Text extraction: for documents (PDF, DOCX, XLSX) — extract text content. Images: OCR. Audio: transcription (expensive, optional). (3) Pattern matching: regex patterns for known PII formats: credit card (Luhn algorithm valid), SSN (XXX-XX-XXXX), passport, medical record numbers. (4) ML classifier: context-aware PII detection beyond simple regex (NLP model). (5) Action on detection: (a) Alert admin/compliance dashboard. (b) Quarantine file (prevent sharing/download). (c) Notify file owner. (d) Log to audit trail. (5) User notification: 'This file appears to contain sensitive data. Please review before sharing.' (6) Policy: organization admin configures which patterns trigger which actions. (7) Allowlist: finance team allowed to have financial data files. (8) False positive rate: tune model to minimize friction for legitimate files.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: File Storage System (Google Drive/Dropbox)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns

topic: Design: Distributed Task Scheduler (Cron at Scale)
cheatSheet:
# Design a Distributed Task Scheduler (Cron at Scale)

## 1. Requirements Gathering

### Functional Requirements
- Schedule tasks to run at a specific time (one-time) or on a recurring schedule (cron expression)
- Execute tasks reliably: at-least-once or exactly-once semantics
- Task definitions: HTTP callback, function invocation, message queue publish
- Task history: execution logs, success/failure
- Retry failed tasks with configurable policy
- Priority queues: critical jobs run before low-priority
- Dead letter handling for tasks that exhaust retries
- Cancel/pause/resume scheduled tasks
- Distributed: no single point of failure

### Non-Functional Requirements
- Fault tolerance: scheduler failure doesn't lose scheduled tasks
- Exactly-once execution: task must not run twice
- Timeliness: execute task within 1 second of scheduled time
- Scale: 100M scheduled tasks, 1M executions/day
- High availability: 99.99%

## 2. Capacity Estimation

- Scheduled tasks: 100M total (mix of one-time and recurring)
- Executions: 1M/day = ~12/sec average
- Peak (start of hour, minute): burst to 10,000/sec (all hourly/minutely jobs fire together)
- Task record: ~1KB
- 100M tasks x 1KB = 100GB task definitions
- Execution log: 1M/day x 500B = 500MB/day = 180GB/year
- Workers: 1M/day / 86,400s = 12 executions/sec average. With peak 10,000/sec → 500-1000 worker threads

## 3. Core Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([API Client])
    AG[API Gateway]
    TaskSvc[Task Management Service]
    Scheduler[Scheduler Engine]
    MQ[Priority Queue - Kafka]
    Workers[Worker Pool]
    LeaderElect[Leader Election - ZooKeeper]
    TaskDB[(Task DB - PostgreSQL)]
    ExecLog[(Execution Log - Cassandra)]
    DLQ[Dead Letter Queue]
    NS[Notification Service]
    Redis[(Redis - Lock / State)]

    Client -->|CRUD tasks| AG
    AG --> TaskSvc
    TaskSvc --> TaskDB
    LeaderElect --> Scheduler
    Scheduler -->|Poll due tasks| TaskDB
    Scheduler -->|Enqueue| MQ
    MQ --> Workers
    Workers -->|Execute| ExecLog
    Workers -->|Update status| TaskDB
    Workers -->|On failure| DLQ
    Workers -->|Lock| Redis
    DLQ --> NS
    NS --> Client
```

## 4. Scheduler Engine Design

### Leader Election
```
Problem: multiple scheduler instances running → same job enqueued multiple times
Solution: Leader Election via ZooKeeper or etcd

Process:
1. All scheduler instances try to acquire ZooKeeper lock
2. One instance acquires lock → becomes LEADER
3. Leader: polls task DB for due tasks, enqueues to Kafka
4. Followers: standby, ready to take over
5. Leader failure: ZooKeeper detects (session timeout ~10s)
6. New election: next scheduler acquires lock in <1 second
7. Maximum leadership gap: ~11 seconds (acceptable)
```

### Task Polling (Tick-based)
```python
# Leader scheduler pseudo-code
def scheduler_loop():
    while True:
        now = current_time()
        due_tasks = db.query("""
            SELECT * FROM tasks
            WHERE status = 'SCHEDULED'
            AND next_run_at <= :now
            AND next_run_at > :now - 60  -- don't pick up very stale
            LIMIT 1000
        """, now=now)

        for task in due_tasks:
            # Atomic: claim task before enqueuing
            updated = db.update("""
                UPDATE tasks SET status='QUEUED', queued_at=:now
                WHERE task_id=:id AND status='SCHEDULED'
            """, id=task.id, now=now)
            if updated.rowcount == 1:  # Won the race
                kafka.publish(task, priority=task.priority)

        sleep(1)  # Poll every second
```

## 5. Exactly-Once Execution

### The Problem
```
Task is enqueued to Kafka.
Worker 1 starts executing.
Worker 1 crashes mid-execution.
Kafka replays task (at-least-once).
Worker 2 picks up and re-executes.
→ Task ran twice (or more).
```

### Solution: Distributed Lock + Idempotency

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant K as Kafka
    participant W as Worker
    participant Redis
    participant TaskDB
    participant ExtSvc as External Service

    K->>W: Task execution event
    W->>Redis: SET lock:exec:{task_id}:{execution_id} NX EX 300
    alt Lock acquired
        Redis-->>W: OK
        W->>TaskDB: UPDATE execution status = RUNNING
        W->>ExtSvc: Execute task (HTTP/function)
        ExtSvc-->>W: Success
        W->>TaskDB: UPDATE execution status = COMPLETED
        W->>Redis: DEL lock:exec:{task_id}:{execution_id}
        W->>K: Commit Kafka offset
    else Lock NOT acquired (another worker has it)
        Redis-->>W: nil
        W->>K: Skip — commit offset (don't reprocess)
    end
```

### Idempotency at Execution Level
- Each task execution gets unique `execution_id` (Snowflake)
- Worker checks: execution_id already in COMPLETED state → skip
- External service must also be idempotent (send idempotency key)

## 6. Cron Expression Parsing

```
Cron format: minute hour day month weekday
Examples:
  '0 9 * * 1'      → every Monday at 9:00 AM
  '*/5 * * * *'    → every 5 minutes
  '0 0 1 * *'      → first day of every month at midnight
  '0 */6 * * *'    → every 6 hours

Next run calculation:
Libraries: croniter (Python), cron-parser (JavaScript), Quartz (Java)

Storage:
STORE: cron_expression + timezone + last_run_at
CALCULATE: next_run_at = croniter.get_next(after=last_run_at, tz=timezone)
UPDATE: after each execution, calculate and store next_run_at

Timezone handling:
- Store cron expression with explicit timezone
- Convert to UTC for comparison
- DST edge cases: skip or repeat on clock change (configurable)
```

## 7. Priority Queues

```
Kafka topics by priority:
  tasks.critical  — SLA: execute within 1 second
  tasks.high      — SLA: execute within 5 seconds
  tasks.normal    — SLA: execute within 60 seconds
  tasks.low       — SLA: execute within 1 hour (best effort)

Worker pools:
  critical_workers: 50 dedicated threads, always running
  high_workers:     20 threads
  normal_workers:   10 threads
  low_workers:      5 threads

Alternative: Single priority queue (SortedSet in Redis)
  ZADD tasks:queue score={priority*10^9 + deadline_ts} member=task_id
  BZPOPMIN tasks:queue 0  -- blocking pop of lowest score
```

## 8. Failure Handling & Retry

```
Task execution failure modes:
1. Task returns error (4xx/5xx from HTTP callback)
2. Task times out (exceeded max_execution_time)
3. Worker crashes during execution (task in RUNNING state, never completes)

Retry policy (configurable per task):
{
  max_retries: 3,
  retry_strategy: exponential,  // linear, fixed, exponential
  initial_delay: 60,  // seconds
  backoff_multiplier: 2,
  max_delay: 3600  // cap at 1 hour
}

Retry schedule example:
  Attempt 1 (original): fails
  Attempt 2: retry after 60s
  Attempt 3: retry after 120s
  Attempt 4: retry after 240s → still fails
  → Move to Dead Letter Queue

Stuck task detection:
Background watcher: scan for tasks in RUNNING state for > max_execution_time
Reset to SCHEDULED (or FAILED if max_retries exhausted)
```

## 9. Data Model

```sql
CREATE TABLE tasks (
    task_id          UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    name             VARCHAR(255),
    owner_id         BIGINT,
    task_type        VARCHAR(20),    -- http, function, kafka
    payload          JSONB,          -- URL/function name/topic + params
    schedule_type    VARCHAR(10),    -- one_time, recurring
    cron_expression  VARCHAR(100),   -- NULL for one_time
    timezone         VARCHAR(50),
    priority         INT DEFAULT 5,  -- 1=critical, 10=low
    status           VARCHAR(20),    -- active, paused, disabled
    next_run_at      TIMESTAMP,
    last_run_at      TIMESTAMP,
    max_retries      INT DEFAULT 3,
    retry_strategy   VARCHAR(20),
    max_exec_seconds INT DEFAULT 300,
    created_at       TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_next_run ON tasks (next_run_at, status);

CREATE TABLE task_executions (
    execution_id  UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    task_id       UUID NOT NULL,
    status        VARCHAR(20),    -- queued, running, completed, failed
    attempt       INT DEFAULT 1,
    started_at    TIMESTAMP,
    completed_at  TIMESTAMP,
    duration_ms   INT,
    result        TEXT,           -- response body or error message
    worker_id     VARCHAR(50),
    created_at    TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_task_execs ON task_executions (task_id, created_at DESC);
```

## 10. API Design

```
Task Management:
POST /api/v1/tasks              — create task
GET  /api/v1/tasks/{id}        — get task definition
PUT  /api/v1/tasks/{id}        — update task
DEL  /api/v1/tasks/{id}        — delete/cancel task
POST /api/v1/tasks/{id}/pause  — pause recurring task
POST /api/v1/tasks/{id}/resume — resume paused task
POST /api/v1/tasks/{id}/trigger — manually trigger immediate execution

Execution History:
GET /api/v1/tasks/{id}/executions        — execution history
GET /api/v1/tasks/{id}/executions/{exec_id}  — specific execution details

Dead Letter Queue:
GET  /api/v1/dlq                          — list DLQ tasks
POST /api/v1/dlq/{exec_id}/retry          — manually retry from DLQ
DEL  /api/v1/dlq/{exec_id}               — discard from DLQ

Request Body Example:
{
  "name": "daily-report",
  "task_type": "http",
  "payload": { "url": "https://api.example.com/reports/generate", "method": "POST" },
  "schedule_type": "recurring",
  "cron_expression": "0 9 * * 1-5",
  "timezone": "America/New_York",
  "priority": 5,
  "max_retries": 3,
  "retry_strategy": "exponential"
}
```

## 11. The Thundering Herd Problem

```
Problem: All hourly jobs fire at :00 of every hour
→ 1M tasks enqueued simultaneously
→ Workers overwhelmed
→ System unstable

Solutions:
1. Jitter: randomize execution time ±60 seconds for non-critical jobs
   next_run_at = scheduled_time + random(0, 60) seconds

2. Staggered scheduling: spread recurring tasks across time window
   When user creates '0 * * * *' job: assign to random minute offset
   Stored as: next_run_at = next_hour + user_specific_offset

3. Backpressure: rate-limit enqueue rate
   If Kafka queue depth > 100K: slow down polling, enqueue gradually

4. Priority preservation: even during overload, critical tasks go first
```

## 12. Monitoring
- Task execution success rate (target >99.9%)
- Scheduling latency: scheduled_time vs actual_start_time (target <1s)
- Queue depth per priority level
- Retry rate (high retry rate → external service problems)
- DLQ size (alert if growing)
- Worker utilization (avoid 100% — leaves no headroom)
- Stuck task count (RUNNING but past max_execution_time)
- Leader election frequency (frequent re-elections → instability)

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Knows cron jobs run on a schedule. Can describe a simple single-server cron. Understands scheduled vs immediate execution.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Understands why single-server cron fails (SPOF). Knows about job queues. Can describe basic retry logic.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Implements leader election for distributed scheduler. Designs exactly-once execution with distributed locks. Handles stuck tasks.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Handles thundering herd at :00, DLQ design, complex retry strategies, task dependency chains, worker autoscaling.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Designs workflow orchestration (Temporal-like), stateful long-running jobs, distributed sagas, global multi-region scheduler, SLA-based scheduling.

quizBank.question: Why is a single-server cron scheduler not suitable for production at scale?
options: Single server is a single point of failure — if it crashes, no jobs run
Cron cannot handle more than 1000 jobs
quizBank.answer: Single server is a single point of failure — if it crashes, no jobs run
quizBank.explanation: Linux cron: runs on one machine. Machine crashes/reboots → scheduled jobs missed. No retry, no monitoring, no distributed execution. Production needs: HA (multiple instances), fault tolerance, monitoring, exactly-once guarantees.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of leader election in a distributed task scheduler?
options: To choose which worker executes each task
To ensure only one scheduler instance polls and enqueues tasks, preventing duplicate job execution
To balance load across worker nodes
To elect the highest-priority task to run next
quizBank.answer: To ensure only one scheduler instance polls and enqueues tasks, preventing duplicate job execution
quizBank.explanation: Without leader election: 3 scheduler instances all poll the DB simultaneously → same task enqueued 3 times → executed 3 times. Leader election: only the leader polls and enqueues. Followers monitor leader health, ready to take over. Ensures single point of control for scheduling decisions.

quizBank.question: A worker picks up a task and starts executing. The worker crashes mid-execution. What happens?
quizBank.answer: Problem: task is in RUNNING state, never completes. Kafka consumer offset NOT committed (since worker crashed). On Kafka consumer group rebalance, another worker picks up the message. Without protection: re-executes → duplicate execution. Solution: (1) Redis distributed lock: worker acquires lock at start with TTL = max_execution_time. On crash: TTL expires automatically. (2) Second worker: tries to acquire same lock → fails (another worker has/had it). (3) Check execution status in DB: if RUNNING and start_time + max_exec_time < now → treat as failed. Reset to SCHEDULED. (4) If task is idempotent: safe to re-execute. If not: the lock + status check provides exactly-once guarantee.

quizBank.question: What is the 'thundering herd' problem in a task scheduler?
options: Too many workers competing for locks
All hourly/minutely jobs firing simultaneously at the scheduled boundary, overwhelming the system
Too many retry attempts happening at once
quizBank.answer: All hourly/minutely jobs firing simultaneously at the scheduled boundary, overwhelming the system
quizBank.explanation: If 100,000 jobs are scheduled for '0 * * * *' (every hour): at exactly :00, all 100K are due simultaneously. Scheduler enqueues 100K tasks in seconds. Workers overwhelmed. System unstable. Prevention: jitter (random ±60s delay), stagger offsets across the hour.

quizBank.question: How do you calculate the next_run_at for a cron expression after a successful execution?
quizBank.answer: Cron evaluation: use a cron parsing library (croniter in Python, cron-parser in JS, Quartz in Java). Steps: (1) Parse cron expression: '0 9 * * 1-5' = 9:00 AM Monday-Friday. (2) After execution at T: next_run_at = croniter.get_next(base=T, tz=task.timezone). (3) Timezone handling: store cron in user's timezone, convert to UTC for DB storage. '0 9 * * *' in 'America/New_York' = 14:00 UTC (EST) or 13:00 UTC (EDT). (4) Edge cases: DST spring-forward (hour skipped) → next occurrence might skip. DST fall-back (hour repeated) → might run twice without handling. Configure: 'skip on ambiguous' or 'run once on ambiguous'. (5) Store: next_run_at in UTC, display to user in their timezone.

quizBank.question: What Redis command is used to implement a distributed lock for exactly-once task execution?
options: SETNX lock:task_id worker_id (SET if Not eXists)
quizBank.answer: SETNX lock:task_id worker_id (SET if Not eXists)
quizBank.explanation: Modern Redis: SET lock:exec:{execution_id} {worker_id} NX EX {ttl_seconds}. NX = only set if not exists (atomic). EX = set TTL. Returns OK if lock acquired, nil if already held. TTL = max_execution_time ensures lock releases even if worker crashes. On completion: DEL lock to release immediately.

quizBank.question: You have 1 million tasks due in the next second (month-end batch processing). How do you handle this?
quizBank.answer: Mass firing strategy: (1) Don't enqueue all 1M at once — Kafka can handle high throughput but workers would be overwhelmed. (2) Rate-limited enqueue: scheduler enqueues at controlled rate (e.g., 10K/sec). 1M / 10K = 100 seconds to enqueue all. (3) Execution priority: critical jobs enqueued first, low-priority jobs last. (4) Auto-scale workers: K8s HPA scales worker pods based on Kafka consumer lag. Pre-scale before known mass events. (5) Task timeout: if task still waiting after 1 hour, skip this run, log as MISSED (rather than backing up indefinitely). (6) Backpressure: if DB write rate too high, temporarily reduce polling frequency. (7) SLA tiers: critical tasks: must run within 1s. Normal: within 1 min. Low: within 1 hour. Communicate SLA to task owners.

quizBank.question: How do you implement a dead letter queue (DLQ) for tasks that exhaust all retries?
quizBank.answer: DLQ design: (1) After final retry failure: set task_execution status = FAILED_PERMANENT. Move execution_id to DLQ table. (2) DLQ table: {dlq_id, task_id, execution_id, failure_reason, failed_at, error_details}. (3) Notification: Kafka event → notification service → alert task owner via email/Slack/PagerDuty. (4) Dashboard: ops team sees DLQ depth per task type. (5) Manual retry: operator investigates root cause, fixes underlying issue, POST /dlq/{id}/retry → re-enqueue for execution. (6) Discard: if task no longer needed, DELETE from DLQ. (7) Aging: DLQ entries older than 30 days auto-archived. (8) Metrics: DLQ depth alert if >100 (configurable). High DLQ rate → something broken upstream.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between 'at-least-once' and 'exactly-once' task execution?
options: At-least-once never skips, may run multiple times; exactly-once runs exactly once even under failures
quizBank.answer: At-least-once never skips, may run multiple times; exactly-once runs exactly once even under failures
quizBank.explanation: At-least-once: guaranteed to execute but may run more than once (on retry after crash). Acceptable if task is idempotent (same result regardless of how many times run). Exactly-once: guarantee that task runs exactly one time. Requires distributed locks, idempotency checks, and careful failure handling. Much harder to implement. Use at-least-once when idempotent; exactly-once for non-idempotent (e.g., send exactly one email).

quizBank.question: How would you design task dependency — Task B runs only after Task A completes successfully?
quizBank.answer: DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of tasks. Data model: (1) tasks table as before. (2) task_dependencies table: (task_id, depends_on_task_id). Task B depends_on Task A. (3) Scheduling logic: task is eligible to run only if all its dependencies have status=COMPLETED (latest execution). (4) Trigger: when Task A completes → find all tasks that depend on A → check if all their other dependencies are also met → if yes, schedule them. (5) Implementation: on task completion, Kafka event → dependency resolver service → checks dependency graph → updates next_run_at for unblocked tasks. (6) Cycle detection: validate DAG on task creation (no cycles allowed). DFS cycle check. (7) Complex workflows: use a workflow engine (Airflow, Temporal) instead of implementing from scratch. (8) Failure propagation: if Task A fails → should Task B run? Configurable: fail_on_dependency_failure=true.

quizBank.question: Why must the database index on tasks include both next_run_at AND status?
options: To efficiently find tasks WHERE status='SCHEDULED' AND next_run_at <= NOW() without full table scan
quizBank.answer: To efficiently find tasks WHERE status='SCHEDULED' AND next_run_at <= NOW() without full table scan
quizBank.explanation: Query: SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE status='SCHEDULED' AND next_run_at <= NOW() LIMIT 1000. Without index: full table scan of 100M rows every second → unacceptable. Composite index: (next_run_at, status) or (status, next_run_at). Index allows efficient range scan. Most tasks are SCHEDULED (or COMPLETED), so status cardinality matters for query planning.

quizBank.question: A task is supposed to run every minute, but due to a 5-minute outage, 5 executions were missed. What should happen on recovery?
quizBank.answer: Missed execution policy is configurable: (1) Run once: execute once after recovery regardless of how many were missed. Skip missed executions. Most common for periodic jobs where only the latest run matters. (2) Run all missed: catch up on all 5 missed executions. Dangerous: can cause cascading load. Only for jobs that must run exact N times (e.g., billing). (3) Skip all missed: resume from current time, don't run old instances. (4) Implementation: store next_run_at in DB. After outage: if next_run_at is in the past by more than N intervals: mark as MISSED, schedule next from NOW(). (5) MISSED status: visible in execution history. Alert for critical jobs that missed runs. (6) Production default: Run-once catch-up. Configure per task: catch_up: false|once|all.

quizBank.question: What types of task execution does a scheduler need to support (HTTP callback, etc.)?
quizBank.answer: Task execution types: (1) HTTP callback: POST/GET to a URL with payload. Most universal. Target service handles the work. (2) Message queue publish: publish message to Kafka topic or SQS queue. Target service consumes when ready. Good for decoupling. (3) Function invocation: invoke a serverless function (AWS Lambda, GCP Cloud Functions). Pass parameters. (4) Shell command: execute a script or command. Useful for ops tasks (cleanup, reports). (5) Internal function: call a registered internal function in the worker's code. Less flexible but faster. (6) Workflow trigger: start a Temporal/Airflow workflow with parameters. For complex multi-step jobs. Worker design: plugin architecture — each task_type has a corresponding executor. Executor handles auth, timeout, response parsing.

quizBank.question: What happens if the ZooKeeper cluster used for leader election goes down?
options: No scheduler instance can become leader — no new tasks are enqueued until ZooKeeper recovers
Tasks are executed directly from the database
quizBank.answer: No scheduler instance can become leader — no new tasks are enqueued until ZooKeeper recovers
quizBank.explanation: ZooKeeper failure: all scheduler instances lose leader status (ZK session timeout). No new tasks enqueued. Running tasks complete (workers are independent). Impact: tasks scheduled during ZK outage are delayed. Recovery: ZK recovers, new election, tasks that missed their window are processed (with miss handling policy). Mitigation: ZK cluster (3-5 nodes for HA), monitor ZK health, use etcd as alternative.

quizBank.question: How do you implement a 'paused' state for a recurring task that should not execute during system maintenance?
quizBank.answer: Pause implementation: (1) API: POST /tasks/{id}/pause → set status='PAUSED'. (2) Scheduler polling: WHERE status='SCHEDULED' AND next_run_at <= NOW(). Paused tasks excluded. (3) In-flight tasks: tasks already QUEUED or RUNNING complete normally. Pause only prevents future scheduling. (4) Pause duration: optional pause_until timestamp. Background job: UPDATE tasks SET status='SCHEDULED' WHERE status='PAUSED' AND pause_until <= NOW(). (5) Audit log: record pause/resume events with actor and reason. (6) Bulk pause: pause all tasks for a specific service/owner (maintenance mode). (7) Maintenance window: schedule automatic pause+resume around known maintenance. (8) Notification: warn task owners whose tasks are paused. (9) Resume: POST /tasks/{id}/resume → set status='SCHEDULED', recalculate next_run_at from NOW().

quizBank.question: How do you design the scheduler to guarantee task execution within 1 second of scheduled time at scale?
quizBank.answer: Sub-second scheduling requires: (1) Poll frequency: scheduler polls DB every 1 second. Any task due in next 1s is enqueued. (2) Worker capacity: workers must be idle and ready. Pre-scale worker pool for known peak times. Auto-scale based on queue depth. (3) Queue priority: critical tasks go to dedicated Kafka partition with dedicated consumers (never waiting behind lower priority tasks). (4) Network latency: scheduler and workers in same region/AZ. Kafka in same region. (5) DB index performance: next_run_at index query must complete in <50ms. Shard DB if needed. (6) Clock sync: NTP on all nodes. Tolerate ±100ms drift. (7) End-to-end measurement: record scheduled_at, enqueued_at, dequeued_at, started_at. Alert if started_at - scheduled_at > 1s. (8) Reality: 1s SLA achievable for normal load. Burst (thundering herd) may cause temporary breach — acceptable with jitter mitigation.

quizBank.question: What is 'jitter' in the context of retry strategies?
options: Random delay added to retry intervals to prevent multiple retrying services from hammering the same target simultaneously
A bug in the cron parser
quizBank.answer: Random delay added to retry intervals to prevent multiple retrying services from hammering the same target simultaneously
quizBank.explanation: Without jitter: 1000 tasks all fail at the same time, all retry after exactly 60 seconds → 1000 simultaneous retries → system overwhelmed → all fail again → repeat. With jitter: each adds random(0, 30) to their retry delay → retries spread across 60-90 second window → system handles gradual retry load. Exponential backoff with jitter is the gold standard.

quizBank.question: A task has max_execution_time of 5 minutes but the external service it calls hangs indefinitely. What happens?
quizBank.answer: Timeout handling: (1) Worker sets timeout on HTTP call: 5 minutes (connect_timeout=5s, read_timeout=300s). (2) Worker receives ReadTimeoutError after 300 seconds. (3) Worker: mark execution as FAILED with reason='timeout'. (4) Worker: release Redis lock. (5) Retry logic: check attempt number vs max_retries. If retries remain: schedule retry with backoff. (6) If all retries exhausted: move to DLQ. (7) Stuck detection (defense in depth): separate watchdog process scans for executions in RUNNING state for longer than max_execution_time + buffer. Marks them FAILED. Handles edge case where worker itself crashed without cleaning up. (8) Alert: repeated timeouts for same task → PagerDuty. External service may be down. (9) Circuit breaker: if external service shows sustained failures, temporarily suspend task type.

quizBank.question: How would you implement task deduplication — ensuring a task that should run once per day doesn't accidentally run twice?
quizBank.answer: Deduplication at multiple layers: (1) Exactly-one scheduling: Redis distributed lock when claiming task from DB. UPDATE tasks SET status='QUEUED' WHERE status='SCHEDULED'. Only one scheduler instance succeeds. (2) Exactly-one execution: Redis lock on execution_id before executing. Worker without lock: skip. (3) Execution idempotency key: generate execution_id as {task_id}:{date}. Daily tasks get same execution_id per day. Duplicate execution attempt: check if execution_id already COMPLETED → skip. (4) External idempotency: pass execution_id to HTTP callback. External service uses it as idempotency key. (5) Audit: execution_log shows exactly one COMPLETED record per day. (6) Monitoring: alert if duplicate execution_id detected. Indicates a deduplication bug.

quizBank.question: A recurring task has been running every minute for a year. It has 525,600 execution log entries. How do you manage this data growth?
options: Partition execution log by task_id and date, apply TTL-based deletion for old entries beyond retention period
Store only the last 10 executions per task
Archive to cold storage after 90 days
quizBank.answer: Partition execution log by task_id and date, apply TTL-based deletion for old entries beyond retention period
quizBank.explanation: 525,600 rows per task after 1 year. 1M tasks = 525B rows total — unsustainable. Cassandra time-series: partition by task_id, cluster by created_at. Cassandra TTL: default_time_to_live = 90 days (configurable). Old rows automatically compacted. Retention policy: paid plans get longer history. S3 archive: before TTL expiry, archive old logs to Parquet on S3 for long-term analytics. Index remains in Cassandra for recent history.

quizBank.question: How would you design a multi-tenant scheduler where thousands of different teams use the same infrastructure?
quizBank.answer: Multi-tenant scheduler requirements: isolation (one team's tasks don't affect others), fair sharing, independent rate limits, separate billing. Design: (1) Task isolation: each task tagged with tenant_id (organization_id). Workers process tasks with tenant awareness. (2) Resource quotas: max_concurrent_tasks per tenant. Max_tasks_per_second per tenant. Enforced via Redis counters: INCR tenant:{id}:running. (3) Noisy neighbor prevention: each tenant gets dedicated Kafka partitions. High-volume tenant can't starve others. Or: weighted fair queuing across tenants. (4) Priority isolation: tenant can set priority within their own tasks. Cross-tenant: fair scheduling. (5) Billing: track execution time, task count per tenant. Kafka event → billing service. (6) Admin: per-tenant dashboard, rate limit configuration, usage metrics. (7) Data isolation: row-level security — tenant can only see their own tasks/executions.

quizBank.question: Why should a task scheduler use atomic database operations (UPDATE with WHERE clause) when claiming tasks?
options: To prevent two scheduler instances from claiming the same task simultaneously (race condition)
quizBank.answer: To prevent two scheduler instances from claiming the same task simultaneously (race condition)
quizBank.explanation: Race condition: two schedulers both query tasks WHERE status='SCHEDULED' → both see task_id=123 → both enqueue → task runs twice. Atomic claim: UPDATE tasks SET status='QUEUED' WHERE task_id=123 AND status='SCHEDULED'. Database row-level locking ensures only one UPDATE succeeds (rows_affected=1). The other scheduler gets rows_affected=0 → knows it lost the race → skips. This is optimistic concurrency control.

interviewTips: Spend 5 minutes on requirements before designing anything
Calculate capacity: daily active users → QPS → storage → bandwidth
Start with API design — define the contract before the architecture
Design the database schema early — it drives many architectural decisions
Think about read vs write patterns — this determines caching and scaling strategy
Consider edge cases: what about celebrity users? Viral content? Peak traffic?
Discuss data partitioning strategy for each data store
Always address the single point of failure question
Mention how you would handle the top 1% of traffic (hot spots)
Propose a monitoring dashboard — what metrics would you track?
commonMistakes: Not understanding the fundamentals of Design: Distributed Task Scheduler (Cron at Scale)
Jumping to implementation without clarifying requirements
Ignoring edge cases and error handling
Not discussing trade-offs between different approaches
Memorizing solutions instead of understanding patterns
